BRCA1.editorial_description: One of the most studied cancer genes. Mutations dramatically increase risk of breast and ovarian cancer. Made famous when Angelina Jolie publicly disclosed her BRCA1 mutation and preventive surgery in 2013.
BRCA1.what_it_means: BRCA1 is a tumor suppressor that repairs damaged DNA. When functioning normally, it prevents cells from growing uncontrollably. Specific mutations (not common variants) disable this protection.
BRCA1.carrier_note: Pathogenic BRCA1 mutations are rare (~1 in 400 people, higher in Ashkenazi Jewish populations). Consumer arrays test some but not all known mutations.
BRCA1.nonref_interpretation: Most non-reference variants here are benign common polymorphisms. Pathogenic mutations require clinical-grade sequencing to confirm.
BRCA1.actionability: Carriers should discuss screening schedules with a genetic counselor.
BRCA1.fun_fact: BRCA1 is enormous — 81kb encoding a 1,863 amino acid protein. It has over 3,400 PubMed publications.

APOE.editorial_description: The most significant genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer's disease. Your APOE genotype (e2/e3/e4) is determined by just two variants that profoundly influence cholesterol transport and brain health.
APOE.what_it_means: APOE carries cholesterol in your bloodstream and brain. The e4 version is less efficient at clearing amyloid plaques. One copy of e4 increases Alzheimer's risk ~3x; two copies ~12x. The e2 version is protective.
APOE.carrier_note: About 25% of people carry at least one e4 allele. It's a risk factor, not a diagnosis — many e4 carriers never develop Alzheimer's.
APOE.nonref_interpretation: The two key variants (rs429358, rs7412) determine your e2/e3/e4 status. Other variants in this region are mostly neutral.
APOE.actionability: e4 carriers may benefit from cardiovascular risk reduction, cognitive engagement, and earlier screening discussions.
APOE.fun_fact: APOE is tiny — only 3.6kb — but those two SNPs create one of the strongest genetic signals in all of medicine.

MTHFR.editorial_description: The internet's most famous gene. Converts folate (vitamin B9) into its active form. Two common variants reduce enzyme efficiency, affecting methylation — but the clinical impact is far more nuanced than wellness blogs suggest.
MTHFR.what_it_means: MTHFR produces methylfolate, essential for DNA synthesis and methylation reactions. The C677T variant reduces activity by ~30% (one copy) or ~60% (two copies). A1298C has a milder effect.
MTHFR.carrier_note: C677T is extremely common — ~10-15% of people are homozygous (TT). This is NOT a disease. Most people with reduced MTHFR function are completely healthy, especially with adequate folate intake.
MTHFR.nonref_interpretation: If you see non-reference variants at rs1801133 (C677T) or rs1801131 (A1298C), that's your MTHFR status. Compound heterozygosity (one of each) has mild effects.
MTHFR.actionability: Ensure adequate folate intake through diet or supplements. No special medical intervention needed for most people.
MTHFR.fun_fact: Despite thousands of blog posts claiming MTHFR mutations cause everything from anxiety to infertility, most large studies find minimal clinical impact in people with adequate nutrition.

COMT.editorial_description: The 'warrior vs worrier' gene. A single variant determines how quickly your brain breaks down dopamine, influencing stress response, pain sensitivity, and cognitive style.
COMT.what_it_means: COMT breaks down catecholamines (dopamine, norepinephrine). The Val158Met variant creates two modes: Val/Val ('warrior') clears dopamine fast — better under stress but lower baseline cognition. Met/Met ('worrier') clears slowly — sharper focus but more stress-sensitive.
COMT.carrier_note: This is one variant (rs4680) with clear functional impact. About 25% of people are Val/Val, 25% Met/Met, and 50% heterozygous (balanced).
COMT.nonref_interpretation: A non-reference call at rs4680 means you carry the Met allele (slower dopamine clearance). Dosage 1.0 = heterozygous, 2.0 = Met/Met.
COMT.actionability: Self-knowledge tool. Met carriers may benefit from stress management techniques; Val carriers may perform better under pressure.
COMT.fun_fact: The warrior/worrier framing is an oversimplification — COMT interacts with dozens of other genes and environmental factors. But it's one of the few single-gene personality associations that replicates well.

FTO.editorial_description: The first 'obesity gene' discovered by GWAS. Common variants near FTO influence appetite regulation and are associated with 1-3 kg higher body weight per risk allele.
FTO.what_it_means: FTO variants don't make you fat — they slightly increase appetite and reduce satiety signaling. The effect is real but modest: about 1.5 kg per allele. Exercise completely attenuates the risk in most studies.
FTO.carrier_note: The risk allele at rs9939609 is carried by ~40-45% of Europeans. It's one of many contributors to weight — genetics explains ~40-70% of BMI variation across hundreds of genes.
FTO.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants in FTO are extremely common. Having them is normal, not pathological.
FTO.actionability: Knowledge that genetic factors influence appetite can reduce self-blame. Physical activity is the strongest modifier of FTO risk.
FTO.fun_fact: FTO stands for 'Fat mass and Obesity-associated' — named after what it does in genetics studies, not its biological function (which actually involves RNA modification).

TP53.editorial_description: The 'guardian of the genome.' TP53 is mutated in over half of all cancers. Its protein p53 decides whether damaged cells get repaired or self-destruct — preventing cancer at the cellular level.
TP53.what_it_means: When DNA damage occurs, p53 activates to either pause cell division for repairs or trigger apoptosis (cell death) if damage is irreparable. Loss of this function allows damaged cells to proliferate into tumors.
TP53.carrier_note: Germline TP53 mutations cause Li-Fraumeni syndrome (very rare, ~1 in 5,000-20,000). The common variant rs1042522 (Pro72Arg) subtly influences p53 efficiency but is NOT pathogenic.
TP53.nonref_interpretation: Common variants here are functional polymorphisms, not cancer mutations. Actual pathogenic TP53 mutations require clinical sequencing.
TP53.actionability: Li-Fraumeni carriers need intensive cancer surveillance. Common variant carriers (most people) need no special action.
TP53.fun_fact: Elephants have 20 copies of TP53 (humans have 2). This is thought to explain why elephants almost never get cancer despite their massive cell count.

FOXO3.editorial_description: The most replicated longevity gene in humans. A variant in FOXO3 is consistently associated with reaching 100+ years across multiple populations worldwide.
FOXO3.what_it_means: FOXO3 activates stress resistance, DNA repair, and autophagy (cellular cleanup). The longevity-associated variant (rs2802292 G allele) increases FOXO3 expression, enhancing these protective pathways.
FOXO3.carrier_note: The protective G allele at rs2802292 is carried by ~25-35% of the general population but ~60-75% of centenarians. It's one of very few variants consistently linked to extreme longevity.
FOXO3.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs2802292 is the longevity-associated allele. Having it doesn't guarantee a long life, but it's a favorable card in the deck.
FOXO3.actionability: The pathways FOXO3 activates (autophagy, stress resistance) are also triggered by fasting, exercise, and caloric restriction — regardless of genotype.
FOXO3.fun_fact: FOXO3 is named after 'Forkhead box O3' — the protein has a wing-like DNA-binding domain that looks like a fork. It's conserved from worms to humans, and extends lifespan when overexpressed in all species tested.

MTOR.editorial_description: The master growth switch. mTOR senses nutrients and decides whether cells should grow or conserve. Rapamycin (its inhibitor, discovered on Easter Island) extends lifespan in every organism tested.
MTOR.what_it_means: mTOR integrates signals from nutrients, insulin, and growth factors to control cell growth, protein synthesis, and autophagy. High mTOR = growth mode. Low mTOR = repair/longevity mode.
MTOR.carrier_note: MTOR has no well-known common pathogenic variants — it's so essential that damaging mutations are lethal. Genetic variation here is subtle and not well-characterized for consumer genetics.
MTOR.nonref_interpretation: Variants in this gene are mostly in non-coding regulatory regions. Their individual effects are tiny and poorly understood.
MTOR.actionability: mTOR is downregulated by fasting, exercise, and rapamycin. Understanding this pathway helps contextualize why caloric restriction extends lifespan.
MTOR.fun_fact: mTOR is named after rapamycin, which was discovered in soil bacteria from Rapa Nui (Easter Island) in 1972. It's now one of the most promising anti-aging drug candidates.

BRCA2.editorial_description: BRCA1's equally important sibling. Mutations in BRCA2 also dramatically increase breast cancer risk and are the leading genetic cause of male breast cancer and pancreatic cancer.
BRCA2.what_it_means: BRCA2 repairs double-strand DNA breaks through homologous recombination. Without it, cells accumulate mutations that can lead to cancer. It works alongside BRCA1 but through a different mechanism.
BRCA2.carrier_note: Pathogenic BRCA2 mutations are found in ~1 in 400 people (~1 in 40 in Ashkenazi Jewish populations). Most people with BRCA2 variants on consumer arrays have benign polymorphisms.
BRCA2.nonref_interpretation: Common non-reference variants here are overwhelmingly benign. Pathogenic mutations require clinical confirmation — consumer arrays miss most of them.
BRCA2.actionability: Confirmed carriers should consult a genetic counselor about enhanced screening and risk-reduction options.
BRCA2.fun_fact: BRCA2 is even larger than BRCA1 — its protein is 3,418 amino acids long, making it one of the biggest proteins your cells produce. It was cloned in 1995, just one year after BRCA1.

BDNF.editorial_description: Brain fertilizer in a gene. BDNF produces a growth factor that strengthens neurons, builds new connections, and is boosted by exercise — the molecular reason running improves your mood.
BDNF.what_it_means: BDNF (Brain-Derived Neurotrophic Factor) promotes neuron survival, synaptic plasticity, and memory formation. The Val66Met variant (rs6265) affects how efficiently BDNF is released in the brain.
BDNF.carrier_note: The Met allele (reduced BDNF secretion) is carried by ~20-30% of Europeans and ~40-50% of East Asians. It's associated with subtle differences in memory and stress response, not disease.
BDNF.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs6265 means you carry the Met allele — slightly reduced activity-dependent BDNF release. This is common and not pathological.
BDNF.actionability: Exercise is the strongest natural BDNF booster regardless of genotype. Met carriers may benefit even more from regular aerobic activity.
BDNF.fun_fact: A single bout of exercise can double BDNF levels in your blood. This is likely why aerobic exercise is as effective as antidepressants in some clinical trials.

TNF.editorial_description: The body's fire alarm. TNF triggers inflammation to fight infection — but when chronically elevated, it drives autoimmune diseases. Anti-TNF drugs (like Humira) are among the best-selling medicines ever.
TNF.what_it_means: TNF (Tumor Necrosis Factor) is a master inflammatory cytokine. It activates immune cells, causes fever, and can kill tumor cells. Variants in its promoter region affect how much TNF you produce.
TNF.carrier_note: The high-expression variant at rs1800629 (-308 G>A) is carried by ~20% of Europeans. It's linked to slightly stronger inflammatory responses but is extremely common.
TNF.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at the promoter SNP suggests higher TNF expression — stronger acute immune responses but potentially more inflammation-related risk.
TNF.actionability: High-expression carriers may benefit from anti-inflammatory lifestyle factors: omega-3s, regular exercise, adequate sleep, and stress management.
TNF.fun_fact: TNF was named in 1975 because it could kill tumor cells in mice. Ironically, chronic TNF actually promotes cancer — it's a double-edged sword that depends entirely on context.

ACE.editorial_description: The blood pressure gene. ACE controls a hormonal cascade that tightens blood vessels. ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) are among the world's most prescribed medications.
ACE.what_it_means: ACE converts angiotensin I to angiotensin II, a potent vasoconstrictor. The I/D polymorphism (an insertion/deletion in intron 16) determines ACE levels — DD genotype has ~2x the ACE activity of II.
ACE.carrier_note: The D allele is carried by ~55% of Europeans. DD genotype (~30% of people) is linked to higher blood pressure and cardiovascular risk, but the effect is modest.
ACE.nonref_interpretation: ACE genotyping on arrays is complex because the key variant is a 287bp insertion/deletion. Proxy SNPs may be used. Interpret with caution.
ACE.actionability: DD carriers benefit from standard cardiovascular prevention: exercise, sodium moderation, weight management. Discuss ACE inhibitor response with a doctor if relevant.
ACE.fun_fact: The ACE D allele is significantly overrepresented in elite sprinters and power athletes — higher ACE activity may favor fast-twitch muscle performance.

VDR.editorial_description: Your vitamin D receptor — the lock that sunshine's hormone turns. VDR variants influence how efficiently you use vitamin D, affecting bone density, immune function, and possibly mood.
VDR.what_it_means: VDR is a nuclear receptor that activates hundreds of genes when bound by vitamin D. Common variants (FokI, BsmI, TaqI, ApaI) subtly alter receptor efficiency or expression levels.
VDR.carrier_note: VDR variants are extremely common and their individual effects are small. Having a 'less efficient' variant doesn't mean you're vitamin D deficient — it means you might need slightly more.
VDR.nonref_interpretation: Multiple VDR SNPs are commonly tested. Non-reference calls suggest variant receptor forms that may have modestly different vitamin D sensitivity.
VDR.actionability: Get your vitamin D levels tested directly (25-OH-D blood test) rather than relying on genetics. Supplement if deficient regardless of VDR genotype.
VDR.fun_fact: VDR is expressed in virtually every tissue — not just bone. Vitamin D regulates over 1,000 genes, which is why deficiency is linked to such a bewildering range of conditions.

TERT.editorial_description: The telomere gene. TERT rebuilds the protective caps on your chromosomes that shorten with each cell division — the closest thing biology has to a cellular aging clock.
TERT.what_it_means: TERT is the catalytic component of telomerase, the enzyme that extends telomeres. Most adult cells have TERT silenced, causing progressive telomere shortening. Variants near TERT affect telomere length.
TERT.carrier_note: Common TERT variants (e.g., rs2736100) have small effects on average telomere length. Longer telomeres are linked to longevity but paradoxically also to slightly increased cancer risk.
TERT.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants near TERT may influence telomere maintenance. The effects are real but modest — lifestyle factors (stress, sleep, exercise) also strongly affect telomere length.
TERT.actionability: Telomere-protective behaviors: manage chronic stress, exercise regularly, sleep well, avoid smoking. These matter more than TERT genotype for most people.
TERT.fun_fact: The 2009 Nobel Prize in Physiology was awarded for telomerase discovery. Lobsters express telomerase in all tissues and show negligible senescence — they don't appear to age.

HIF1A.editorial_description: The altitude gene. HIF1A senses oxygen levels and rewires metabolism when oxygen drops — the same pathway that lets Tibetans thrive at 4,500m and helps athletes adapt to training.
HIF1A.what_it_means: HIF-1α is the master oxygen sensor. When oxygen drops, it activates genes for blood vessel growth, red blood cell production, and glycolysis. The Pro582Ser variant (rs11549465) affects its stability.
HIF1A.carrier_note: The Ser582 variant is found in ~5-10% of Europeans and is overrepresented in endurance athletes. It's a normal variant with subtle effects on oxygen adaptation.
HIF1A.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs11549465 suggests a HIF-1α protein that is more stable under normal oxygen — potentially enhanced endurance adaptation capacity.
HIF1A.actionability: Interesting context for athletes. Altitude training, HIIT, and breath-hold exercises all activate HIF pathways regardless of genotype.
HIF1A.fun_fact: The 2019 Nobel Prize in Physiology went to the discovery of how cells sense oxygen — the HIF pathway. It's now a drug target for anemia, cancer, and heart disease.

ABCB1.editorial_description: Your cells' bouncer. ABCB1 encodes P-glycoprotein, a molecular pump that ejects drugs and toxins from cells. Variants determine how quickly you clear medications — affecting doses that work for you.
ABCB1.what_it_means: P-glycoprotein sits in cell membranes (especially gut, liver, brain) and pumps foreign molecules out. The C3435T variant (rs1045642) affects expression — TT genotype has lower pump activity, leading to higher drug levels.
ABCB1.carrier_note: The T allele at rs1045642 is carried by ~50% of Europeans. TT homozygotes (~25%) may have higher blood levels of many common drugs including statins, antihistamines, and some antidepressants.
ABCB1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants suggest altered P-glycoprotein expression. This primarily matters for pharmacogenomics — how you respond to specific medications.
ABCB1.actionability: Relevant for pharmacogenomics. If you experience unusual drug sensitivity or resistance, ABCB1 status may be a factor worth discussing with your pharmacist.
ABCB1.fun_fact: P-glycoprotein was discovered because it made cancer cells resistant to chemotherapy — it literally pumps the drugs back out. Some foods (grapefruit juice) inhibit it, which is why grapefruit interacts with so many medications.

SLC6A4.editorial_description: Better known as the serotonin transporter (5-HTT). Once called 'the depression gene,' its famous short/long promoter variant was the poster child for gene-environment interaction research, though the story is more complex than early headlines suggested.
SLC6A4.what_it_means: SLC6A4 recycles serotonin from the synapse back into neurons. The 5-HTTLPR polymorphism (short vs long) affects how much transporter is produced — SSRIs (Prozac, Zoloft) directly block this same protein.
SLC6A4.carrier_note: The short allele is carried by ~40-45% of Europeans and ~70-80% of East Asians. Early studies linking it to depression haven't replicated well in large GWAS — the effect, if real, requires specific life stressors.
SLC6A4.nonref_interpretation: The key variant (5-HTTLPR) is a length polymorphism that most arrays can't directly genotype. Proxy SNPs may be reported instead. Interpret cautiously.
SLC6A4.actionability: May inform SSRI response discussions with a psychiatrist. Not useful for predicting depression on its own.
SLC6A4.fun_fact: The 2003 Caspi & Moffitt paper linking 5-HTTLPR to depression after stressful life events is one of the most cited (and debated) papers in psychiatric genetics. It launched an entire field of gene×environment research.

EGFR.editorial_description: A growth signal receptor that tells cells to divide. When mutated in tumors, it's stuck 'on' — driving cancers of the lung, brain, and colon. Targeted drugs against EGFR revolutionized cancer treatment.
EGFR.what_it_means: EGFR sits on cell surfaces and receives growth factor signals. Activating mutations (in tumors, not germline) make it signal continuously. Drugs like erlotinib and cetuximab specifically block mutant EGFR.
EGFR.carrier_note: Germline EGFR variants are mostly benign. The clinically important mutations are somatic (tumor-only). Consumer arrays test germline DNA and won't detect cancer-driving EGFR mutations.
EGFR.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants in EGFR are generally common polymorphisms with no cancer significance. Somatic mutations require tumor sequencing.
EGFR.actionability: No germline action needed. EGFR testing matters for tumor profiling in cancer patients to guide targeted therapy selection.
EGFR.fun_fact: EGFR-targeted therapy was the first 'precision oncology' success. Lung cancer patients with EGFR mutations respond dramatically to pills instead of chemo — turning a death sentence into a manageable condition for many.

NOS3.editorial_description: The nitric oxide gene. NOS3 produces the gas that relaxes blood vessels, lowers blood pressure, and gives you that post-exercise flush. Variants affect cardiovascular health and exercise performance.
NOS3.what_it_means: NOS3 (endothelial nitric oxide synthase) produces NO in blood vessel walls, causing vasodilation. The Glu298Asp variant (rs1799983) reduces NO production, associated with higher blood pressure and stiffer arteries.
NOS3.carrier_note: The Asp298 variant is carried by ~30-35% of Europeans. It's a common polymorphism with small but consistent cardiovascular effects — not a disease-causing mutation.
NOS3.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs1799983 indicates the Asp variant with modestly reduced NO production. Relevant for cardiovascular risk context.
NOS3.actionability: NO production is boosted by exercise, dietary nitrate (beets, leafy greens), and adequate L-arginine. These strategies help regardless of genotype.
NOS3.fun_fact: Nitric oxide's role in vasodilation is why beetroot juice improves exercise performance — and why Viagra works. The 1998 Nobel Prize was awarded for discovering NO as a signaling molecule.

ESR1.editorial_description: The estrogen receptor alpha — the primary lock that estrogen turns to affect bone density, cardiovascular health, and reproductive biology. Variants influence menopause timing and osteoporosis risk.
ESR1.what_it_means: ESR1 is a nuclear receptor that, when bound by estrogen, activates hundreds of genes. It's critical in bone, breast, uterus, brain, and cardiovascular tissue. Variants alter receptor expression or sensitivity.
ESR1.carrier_note: Common ESR1 polymorphisms (PvuII, XbaI) are found in 30-50% of people. Effects on bone density and breast cancer risk are statistically significant but individually small.
ESR1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants at ESR1 are common and may modestly influence estrogen sensitivity. Most relevant for bone health and hormone-related trait context.
ESR1.actionability: Relevant context for bone density screening decisions and hormone therapy discussions, especially around menopause.
ESR1.fun_fact: Tamoxifen, one of the most successful cancer drugs ever, works by blocking ESR1 in breast tissue. Remarkably, it activates ESR1 in bone — so it treats breast cancer while preventing osteoporosis simultaneously.

PPARG.editorial_description: The fat cell master switch. PPARγ decides whether stem cells become fat cells and controls insulin sensitivity. It's the direct target of thiazolidinedione diabetes drugs (rosiglitazone, pioglitazone).
PPARG.what_it_means: PPARγ is a nuclear receptor that drives adipocyte differentiation and improves insulin sensitivity. The Pro12Ala variant (rs1801282) modestly reduces activity — paradoxically protective against type 2 diabetes.
PPARG.carrier_note: The protective Ala12 variant is carried by ~15-25% of Europeans. It reduces type 2 diabetes risk by about 20% — one of the earliest and most replicated diabetes GWAS hits.
PPARG.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs1801282 is the Ala allele — the diabetes-protective variant. This is a favorable finding.
PPARG.actionability: Context for metabolic health. The Pro/Pro genotype (reference) responds well to lifestyle intervention for diabetes prevention.
PPARG.fun_fact: PPARγ was the target of a $6 billion drug (Avandia/rosiglitazone) that was eventually restricted due to heart risks — proving that even well-understood targets can have dangerous off-target effects.

IL6.editorial_description: The fever gene. IL-6 is a key inflammatory messenger that rises during infection, after exercise, and in chronic disease. It was a critical target during COVID-19 treatment with tocilizumab.
IL6.what_it_means: IL-6 is a pleiotropic cytokine — it drives acute inflammation, stimulates antibody production, and triggers fever. The -174 G>C promoter variant (rs1800795) affects how much IL-6 you produce.
IL6.carrier_note: The C allele (lower IL-6 production) is found in ~40% of Europeans. GG individuals produce more IL-6 — stronger acute immune responses but potentially more chronic inflammation.
IL6.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs1800795 indicates the lower-expression C allele. This may mean slightly reduced inflammatory responses.
IL6.actionability: High-expression carriers (GG) may benefit from anti-inflammatory diet and lifestyle. IL-6 is strongly reduced by regular exercise (paradoxically, muscle-derived IL-6 during exercise is anti-inflammatory).
IL6.fun_fact: During COVID-19, IL-6 blockers (tocilizumab) saved thousands of lives by preventing cytokine storms. Exercise acutely raises IL-6 by up to 100-fold — but this muscle-derived IL-6 triggers anti-inflammatory cascades.

PTEN.editorial_description: The brakes on cell growth. PTEN is the second most commonly mutated tumor suppressor after TP53. It counteracts the PI3K growth pathway — when PTEN is lost, cells grow without restraint.
PTEN.what_it_means: PTEN is a phosphatase that opposes PI3K signaling, keeping cell growth in check. It's a critical gatekeeper — loss of one copy is enough to promote tumor development in some tissues.
PTEN.carrier_note: Germline PTEN mutations cause Cowden syndrome (rare, ~1 in 200,000) with increased cancer risk. Common variants in or near PTEN are benign polymorphisms.
PTEN.nonref_interpretation: Common non-reference variants near PTEN are not pathogenic. True loss-of-function mutations are rare and would be flagged in clinical sequencing.
PTEN.actionability: Cowden syndrome carriers need multi-organ cancer surveillance. Common variant carriers need no special action.
PTEN.fun_fact: PTEN stands for 'Phosphatase and Tensin Homolog' — named for its structural similarity to tensin, a protein that anchors cells. It's so important that even losing one copy (haploinsufficiency) increases cancer risk.

GSTM1.editorial_description: Your detox enzyme — or lack thereof. About half of all humans have a complete deletion of GSTM1, meaning they entirely lack this enzyme that neutralizes carcinogens, drugs, and environmental toxins.
GSTM1.what_it_means: GSTM1 conjugates glutathione to toxins, making them water-soluble for excretion. The 'null' genotype (homozygous deletion) produces zero enzyme. Other GST family members partially compensate.
GSTM1.carrier_note: GSTM1 null is present in ~50% of Europeans, ~55% of East Asians, and ~27% of Africans. It's technically not a variant — half the population simply doesn't have this gene. You're in good company.
GSTM1.nonref_interpretation: If your array shows no calls at GSTM1, you may be null (deleted). A single non-reference variant suggests you have at least one copy. Interpretation depends on array design.
GSTM1.actionability: Null carriers may benefit from reducing exposure to environmental carcinogens (smoke, charred food) and eating cruciferous vegetables, which upregulate alternative detox pathways.
GSTM1.fun_fact: GSTM1 deletion is so common it's debated whether it's even disadvantageous — some researchers argue the null genotype may have been positively selected. It's one of the most common gene deletions in humans.

SNCA.editorial_description: The Parkinson's gene. Alpha-synuclein misfolds into toxic clumps (Lewy bodies) that kill dopamine neurons. It was the first gene linked to Parkinson's disease and remains central to understanding neurodegeneration.
SNCA.what_it_means: SNCA produces alpha-synuclein, a small protein involved in neurotransmitter release at synapses. When it misfolds and aggregates, it spreads between neurons like a prion, progressively destroying brain circuits.
SNCA.carrier_note: Pathogenic SNCA mutations are extremely rare (a handful of families worldwide). Common variants near SNCA (e.g., rs356219) modestly affect Parkinson's risk in GWAS but are not diagnostic.
SNCA.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants near SNCA are common regulatory polymorphisms. They contribute tiny amounts to polygenic Parkinson's risk — not actionable individually.
SNCA.actionability: No individual action for common variants. Exercise is the strongest modifiable protective factor for Parkinson's across all genotypes.
SNCA.fun_fact: Alpha-synuclein spreading between cells is eerily similar to prion diseases. Some researchers now call Parkinson's a 'prion-like' disorder — and there's evidence it may start in the gut and travel to the brain via the vagus nerve.

BRAF.editorial_description: The melanoma oncogene. A single mutation (V600E) in BRAF drives ~50% of melanomas and is the target of vemurafenib — one of the most dramatic success stories in precision oncology.
BRAF.what_it_means: BRAF is a kinase in the MAPK growth signaling pathway. The V600E somatic mutation locks it permanently 'on,' driving uncontrolled cell division. Germline variants are a different story entirely.
BRAF.carrier_note: The cancer-relevant V600E is a somatic mutation (tumor-only), not inherited. Germline BRAF variants are rare; some cause developmental syndromes (cardiofaciocutaneous syndrome). Consumer arrays test germline DNA.
BRAF.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants in BRAF are uncommon. If present, they're likely benign polymorphisms — not the cancer-driving somatic mutations.
BRAF.actionability: No germline action for most people. BRAF V600E testing on tumor tissue guides melanoma, colorectal, and thyroid cancer treatment.
BRAF.fun_fact: When vemurafenib was first given to V600E melanoma patients, tumors melted away in days — one of the fastest cancer drug responses ever seen. Unfortunately, resistance typically develops within months.

APOB.editorial_description: The cholesterol delivery truck. Every LDL particle has exactly one APOB molecule wrapping it. Your APOB level directly reflects how many atherogenic particles are in your blood — arguably a better heart risk marker than LDL-C.
APOB.what_it_means: APOB is the structural protein of LDL, VLDL, and lipoprotein(a) particles. Variants affect how many particles you produce, how well they're cleared, and ultimately your atherosclerosis risk.
APOB.carrier_note: Common APOB variants have modest effects on cholesterol levels. Rare loss-of-function mutations cause familial hypobetalipoproteinemia (very low LDL — actually protective). Gain-of-function contributes to familial hypercholesterolemia.
APOB.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may influence LDL particle number. The direction of effect depends on the specific variant — some raise and some lower cardiovascular risk.
APOB.actionability: Get an APOB blood test for direct measurement. It's increasingly considered the single best lipid marker for cardiovascular risk.
APOB.fun_fact: APOB is one of the largest proteins your body makes (4,563 amino acids). Every single LDL particle is wrapped by exactly one APOB molecule — so counting APOB literally counts your atherogenic particles.

IGF1.editorial_description: The growth hormone's messenger. IGF-1 drives muscle growth, bone development, and tissue repair. It's why teenagers grow and why growth hormone doping works — but high levels long-term may accelerate aging.
IGF1.what_it_means: IGF-1 mediates most of growth hormone's effects: cell proliferation, protein synthesis, and glucose metabolism. Variants near IGF1 affect circulating levels, influencing height, muscle mass, and cancer risk.
IGF1.carrier_note: Common IGF1 variants contribute to normal variation in height and body composition. They're not disease-causing — they're part of why people naturally vary in size and build.
IGF1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants near IGF1 may modestly influence circulating IGF-1 levels. Effects are subtle and part of normal polygenic variation.
IGF1.actionability: IGF-1 is lowered by fasting and caloric restriction, raised by protein intake and sleep. The longevity field debates whether lower IGF-1 extends lifespan (it does in mice and short-statured humans).
IGF1.fun_fact: Ecuadorian villagers with Laron syndrome (IGF-1 deficiency) are very short but almost never get cancer or diabetes. They've become one of the most studied populations in aging research.

NFE2L2.editorial_description: Better known as NRF2 — your antioxidant master switch. This gene activates over 200 protective genes when cells are stressed — the reason broccoli, exercise, and fasting trigger cellular defense systems.
NFE2L2.what_it_means: NRF2 is normally held inactive by KEAP1. Under oxidative stress, it's released and activates a massive battery of detoxification, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory genes simultaneously.
NFE2L2.carrier_note: NFE2L2 variants are poorly characterized in consumer genetics. The gene is so critical that damaging mutations are likely selected against. Common polymorphisms have subtle effects.
NFE2L2.nonref_interpretation: Variants here are mostly in regulatory regions with small, poorly-understood effects on NRF2 expression or activation threshold.
NFE2L2.actionability: NRF2 is activated by sulforaphane (broccoli sprouts), exercise, fasting, and mild stress. These hormetic triggers boost cellular defense regardless of genotype.
NFE2L2.fun_fact: NRF2 is why 'hormesis' works — small stresses (exercise, heat, plant toxins) activate this pathway, making you MORE resilient. It's the molecular basis for 'what doesn't kill you makes you stronger.'

HLA-B.editorial_description: The most polymorphic gene in the human genome. HLA-B presents protein fragments to your immune system, determining which infections you fight well — and which autoimmune diseases you're susceptible to.
HLA-B.what_it_means: HLA-B displays intracellular peptides on cell surfaces for T-cell inspection. Different HLA-B alleles present different peptides, explaining why people vary enormously in immune responses to the same pathogen.
HLA-B.carrier_note: There are over 7,000 known HLA-B alleles. Everyone carries two. Specific alleles are linked to drug hypersensitivities (HLA-B*57:01 and abacavir) and autoimmune conditions (HLA-B27 and ankylosing spondylitis).
HLA-B.nonref_interpretation: HLA typing from consumer arrays is imprecise. The region is so complex and polymorphic that SNP arrays often can't resolve true HLA alleles. Clinical HLA typing uses specialized methods.
HLA-B.actionability: HLA-B*57:01 testing is required before prescribing abacavir (HIV drug). HLA-B27 testing can support ankylosing spondylitis diagnosis. Consumer array HLA calls should be confirmed clinically.
HLA-B.fun_fact: HLA genes are so diverse because of pathogen-driven selection — populations with more HLA diversity survive pandemics better. This diversity is also why organ transplant matching is so difficult.

ADIPOQ.editorial_description: The 'good fat' hormone gene. Adiponectin (from ADIPOQ) is paradoxically produced by fat cells but protects against obesity's harmful effects — improving insulin sensitivity and reducing inflammation.
ADIPOQ.what_it_means: Adiponectin improves insulin sensitivity, promotes fat burning, and reduces inflammation. Unlike most adipokines, its levels DECREASE with obesity — creating a vicious cycle. Variants affect how much you produce.
ADIPOQ.carrier_note: Common ADIPOQ variants (e.g., rs2241766, rs1501299) are found in 20-40% of people. They modestly influence adiponectin levels and metabolic risk, but are far less important than actual body composition.
ADIPOQ.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate lower adiponectin production, slightly increasing metabolic risk. The effect is small compared to lifestyle factors.
ADIPOQ.actionability: Adiponectin is increased by exercise, weight loss, omega-3 fatty acids, and adequate sleep. These are beneficial regardless of genotype.
ADIPOQ.fun_fact: Adiponectin is one of the most abundant proteins in blood — about 1,000x more concentrated than most hormones. Thin people have MORE of this fat-cell hormone than obese people, which puzzled researchers for years.

AR.editorial_description: The androgen receptor — the lock that testosterone turns. A CAG repeat length in AR determines receptor sensitivity, influencing muscle development, hair loss, prostate risk, and PCOS in women.
AR.what_it_means: AR binds testosterone and DHT, activating genes for male development, muscle growth, and body hair. Shorter CAG repeats = more sensitive receptor = stronger androgen effects. Longer repeats = weaker signaling.
AR.carrier_note: CAG repeat length varies from ~8 to ~35. Shorter repeats are linked to male pattern baldness and prostate cancer risk; longer repeats to reduced androgen sensitivity. Most people are in the normal middle range.
AR.nonref_interpretation: Consumer arrays poorly capture CAG repeat length (the key functional variant). SNP-based calls in AR may not reflect your actual repeat length.
AR.actionability: Context for understanding androgen sensitivity. Short-repeat carriers might discuss earlier prostate screening. Clinical CAG repeat testing is available if relevant.
AR.fun_fact: AR is on the X chromosome, so males have only one copy — no backup. Extreme CAG expansion (40+) causes Kennedy's disease (spinal and bulbar muscular atrophy), a neurodegenerative condition affecting motor neurons.

APP.editorial_description: The Alzheimer's plaque gene. APP produces amyloid-beta, the protein that accumulates into the brain plaques defining Alzheimer's disease. Rare mutations cause early-onset familial Alzheimer's in one's 40s-50s.
APP.what_it_means: APP is cut by enzymes (secretases) into fragments. One fragment — amyloid-beta — can aggregate into toxic plaques. Mutations that increase amyloid-beta production or aggregation cause early Alzheimer's.
APP.carrier_note: Pathogenic APP mutations are extremely rare (<1,000 families worldwide) and cause Alzheimer's before age 60. Common variants near APP have negligible individual effect on late-onset risk.
APP.nonref_interpretation: Common non-reference variants near APP are benign polymorphisms. True pathogenic mutations are exceedingly rare and would require clinical sequencing to confirm.
APP.actionability: Families with early-onset Alzheimer's should seek genetic counseling. For common variants, no specific action — standard brain health practices apply.
APP.fun_fact: An Icelandic APP variant (A673T) actually PROTECTS against Alzheimer's and cognitive decline in aging. Carriers are overrepresented among cognitively sharp 85+ year-olds — it reduces amyloid-beta production by ~40%.

ERBB2.editorial_description: Better known as HER2. When amplified in breast tumors, it drives aggressive cancer — but the development of trastuzumab (Herceptin) turned HER2-positive breast cancer from the worst prognosis to one of the best.
ERBB2.what_it_means: ERBB2/HER2 is a growth factor receptor. In ~20% of breast cancers, the gene is amplified (extra copies), flooding the cell surface with receptors and driving uncontrolled growth. This is a somatic event, not inherited.
ERBB2.carrier_note: HER2 amplification is a tumor characteristic, not a germline trait. Consumer arrays test your inherited DNA — they won't detect HER2-positive cancer status. Germline ERBB2 variants are mostly benign.
ERBB2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants in ERBB2 are common polymorphisms unrelated to HER2-positive cancer status. Tumor HER2 testing requires tissue biopsy.
ERBB2.actionability: No germline action needed. HER2 status matters for breast cancer treatment selection — tested on tumor tissue, not consumer arrays.
ERBB2.fun_fact: Herceptin's development is one of medicine's greatest stories. Dennis Slamon spent a decade fighting skeptics before proving HER2-targeted therapy worked. It transformed HER2+ breast cancer from the deadliest subtype to one with excellent outcomes.

MAPT.editorial_description: The tau protein gene. Tau tangles are the other hallmark of Alzheimer's (alongside amyloid plaques) and the primary driver of frontotemporal dementia. Tau pathology correlates with cognitive decline better than amyloid.
MAPT.what_it_means: MAPT produces tau, which stabilizes microtubule 'train tracks' inside neurons. When tau detaches and aggregates into tangles, neuronal transport collapses and cells die. Different mutations cause different dementias.
MAPT.carrier_note: Pathogenic MAPT mutations cause frontotemporal dementia (rare, ~1 in 10,000). Common MAPT variants define two haplotypes (H1/H2) — H1 is associated with slightly higher Parkinson's and PSP risk.
MAPT.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants likely indicate H2 haplotype, which is actually mildly protective against Parkinson's and tauopathies. H2 is found in ~20% of Europeans.
MAPT.actionability: No action for common haplotype variants. Families with early-onset dementia should consider genetic counseling for rare MAPT mutations.
MAPT.fun_fact: The H2 haplotype is a 900kb inversion — a huge chunk of chromosome 17 is flipped. It entered the human lineage ~3 million years ago and can't recombine with H1, creating two completely separate evolutionary lineages.

CRP.editorial_description: The inflammation blood test gene. CRP (C-reactive protein) is the most widely ordered inflammatory marker in medicine. Genetic variants explain why some people run 'hot' on this test regardless of actual inflammation.
CRP.what_it_means: CRP is produced by the liver in response to IL-6 signaling during inflammation. Variants in the CRP gene affect baseline production levels — independent of actual inflammatory state.
CRP.carrier_note: Common CRP variants (e.g., rs1205, rs3093077) can shift baseline CRP by 20-30%. If your hs-CRP is always mildly elevated despite a healthy lifestyle, genetics may explain it.
CRP.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants at CRP may indicate genetically higher or lower baseline CRP levels. This is useful context when interpreting CRP blood tests.
CRP.actionability: Useful context for interpreting hs-CRP blood tests. If your CRP is genetically elevated, it may not reflect true cardiovascular risk as accurately as in others.
CRP.fun_fact: CRP was discovered in 1930 and named because it reacts with the C-polysaccharide of pneumococcus bacteria. It can rise 1,000-fold during severe infection — one of the most dramatic acute-phase responses in biology.

CFTR.editorial_description: The cystic fibrosis gene. One in 25 Europeans silently carries a CFTR mutation. Two copies cause CF — but carriers (one copy) may have been protected against cholera, explaining why the mutations persist.
CFTR.what_it_means: CFTR is a chloride channel that regulates salt and water balance in lungs, pancreas, and sweat glands. When both copies are broken, thick mucus accumulates in airways and digestive organs.
CFTR.carrier_note: ~1 in 25 Europeans carries one CFTR mutation (completely healthy). The risk is having children with another carrier — 25% chance each child has CF. Over 2,000 different mutations are known; ΔF508 is the most common (~70%).
CFTR.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants in CFTR may indicate carrier status. Consumer arrays detect some but not all CF mutations. Clinical carrier testing is more comprehensive.
CFTR.actionability: Carriers should be aware for family planning. Partner carrier testing is recommended if one partner is a known carrier. Genetic counseling available.
CFTR.fun_fact: CFTR carrier frequency is puzzlingly high for a lethal disease. The leading theory: carriers have drier intestinal surfaces, providing resistance to cholera and typhoid — a selective advantage that maintained the mutations for millennia.

KRAS.editorial_description: The 'undruggable' oncogene — until 2021. KRAS mutations drive ~25% of all cancers. For 40 years it resisted every drug. Then sotorasib cracked the G12C mutation, opening a new era in cancer therapy.
KRAS.what_it_means: KRAS is a molecular switch in growth signaling. Normally it flips on and off. Somatic mutations lock it 'on' permanently, driving relentless cell division in pancreatic, lung, and colorectal cancers.
KRAS.carrier_note: KRAS mutations in cancer are somatic (tumor-only), not inherited. Germline KRAS variants are extremely rare — activating germline mutations cause Noonan syndrome. Consumer arrays test germline DNA.
KRAS.nonref_interpretation: Germline non-reference variants in KRAS are very uncommon on consumer arrays. If present, they're likely benign or in non-coding regions.
KRAS.actionability: No germline action for most people. KRAS mutation testing on tumor tissue guides treatment decisions in lung, colorectal, and pancreatic cancer.
KRAS.fun_fact: KRAS stands for 'Kirsten Rat Sarcoma' — named after a virus that caused cancer in rats in 1967. It took 54 years from discovery to the first approved drug targeting it. Researchers called it 'undruggable' for decades.

TLR4.editorial_description: Your bacterial alarm sensor. TLR4 detects lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from gram-negative bacteria and triggers the inflammatory immune response. It's why sepsis can kill — TLR4 overreacts to overwhelming infection.
TLR4.what_it_means: TLR4 sits on immune cell surfaces and recognizes bacterial cell wall components. When activated, it triggers NF-κB and produces inflammatory cytokines. The Asp299Gly variant (rs4986790) reduces TLR4 responsiveness.
TLR4.carrier_note: The Gly299 variant is carried by ~5-10% of Europeans. It reduces inflammatory responses to bacterial products — potentially protective against septic shock but possibly increasing infection susceptibility.
TLR4.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs4986790 indicates reduced TLR4 signaling — a hypo-responsive innate immune phenotype. Effects are subtle in everyday health.
TLR4.actionability: Mostly research-grade information. May provide context for individuals with unusual infection susceptibility or inflammatory patterns.
TLR4.fun_fact: TLR4 was identified through a mouse strain (C3H/HeJ) that was mysteriously resistant to endotoxic shock. The 1998 discovery of Toll-like receptors in humans earned Jules Hoffmann and Bruce Beutler the 2011 Nobel Prize.

CDKN2A.editorial_description: The cell cycle brake pedal. CDKN2A encodes TWO tumor suppressors (p16 and p14ARF) from the same DNA. Germline mutations cause familial melanoma and pancreatic cancer; somatic loss occurs in most cancers.
CDKN2A.what_it_means: p16 (from CDKN2A) blocks CDK4/6 from pushing cells through the cell cycle. It's also a senescence marker — p16 accumulates as you age, stopping damaged old cells from dividing.
CDKN2A.carrier_note: Germline CDKN2A mutations are rare but high-penetrance for familial melanoma (70%+ lifetime risk). Common variants near CDKN2A (9p21 locus) affect cardiovascular and cancer risk at a population level.
CDKN2A.nonref_interpretation: The 9p21 risk region near CDKN2A is one of the strongest GWAS loci for coronary artery disease. Non-reference variants here contribute to polygenic cardiovascular risk.
CDKN2A.actionability: Families with multiple melanomas or pancreatic cancers should consider CDKN2A testing and enhanced skin surveillance. For common 9p21 variants, standard cardiovascular prevention applies.
CDKN2A.fun_fact: CDKN2A is a genetic marvel — it produces two completely different proteins (p16 and p14ARF) from overlapping reading frames of the same DNA. It's one of the most efficiently packed genes in the human genome.

CDH1.editorial_description: The cellular glue gene. E-cadherin holds epithelial cells together. When lost, cells break free and spread — which is why CDH1 mutations cause diffuse gastric cancer and lobular breast cancer with a uniquely infiltrative pattern.
CDH1.what_it_means: E-cadherin is the primary adhesion molecule between epithelial cells. Without it, cells lose contact inhibition and can invade surrounding tissue. It's both a tumor suppressor and a metastasis suppressor.
CDH1.carrier_note: Germline CDH1 mutations cause hereditary diffuse gastric cancer (HDGC) — rare but high-penetrance (70%+ lifetime gastric cancer risk). Most people carrying common CDH1 variants have benign polymorphisms.
CDH1.nonref_interpretation: Common non-reference variants are generally benign. Pathogenic CDH1 mutations are rare and typically identified through family cancer history and clinical testing.
CDH1.actionability: Families with multiple diffuse gastric or lobular breast cancers should consider CDH1 testing. Confirmed carriers may be advised to consider prophylactic gastrectomy.
CDH1.fun_fact: Prophylactic total gastrectomy (stomach removal) is recommended for CDH1 mutation carriers — one of the most dramatic preventive surgeries in medicine. People can live well without a stomach, eating small frequent meals.

VEGFA.editorial_description: The blood vessel builder. VEGF tells your body to grow new blood vessels — essential for wound healing and exercise adaptation, but hijacked by tumors to feed themselves. Anti-VEGF drugs (bevacizumab) starve cancers.
VEGFA.what_it_means: VEGF-A is the master signal for angiogenesis (new blood vessel formation). It's activated by hypoxia, exercise, and wound healing. Tumors exploit it to build their own blood supply.
VEGFA.carrier_note: Common VEGFA variants affect circulating VEGF levels and may modestly influence wound healing speed, exercise angiogenesis, and cancer risk. Effects are subtle at the individual level.
VEGFA.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants in the VEGFA promoter may indicate higher or lower VEGF production. Relevant context for understanding individual variation in vascular responses.
VEGFA.actionability: Mostly research context. Exercise strongly induces VEGF regardless of genotype — it's a key mechanism behind cardiovascular fitness adaptation.
VEGFA.fun_fact: Judah Folkman proposed in 1971 that starving tumors of blood supply could treat cancer. He was ridiculed for decades. Anti-VEGF therapy (Avastin) was finally approved in 2004 and is now used across many cancer types.

BCL2.editorial_description: The zombie cell gene. BCL-2 prevents apoptosis (programmed cell death), keeping cells alive that should die. In cancer, it creates immortal tumor cells. Venetoclax, which blocks BCL-2, revolutionized leukemia treatment.
BCL2.what_it_means: BCL-2 sits on mitochondrial membranes and blocks the death signal cascade. It's essential for long-lived cells like memory lymphocytes. But when overexpressed in cancer, it makes tumor cells unkillable.
BCL2.carrier_note: BCL2 overexpression in cancer is typically caused by the t(14;18) chromosomal translocation (somatic, in lymphomas). Germline variants are common polymorphisms without pathogenic significance.
BCL2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants in BCL2 are benign regulatory polymorphisms. Cancer-relevant BCL2 changes are somatic translocations not detected by consumer arrays.
BCL2.actionability: No germline action needed. BCL-2 status matters for hematologic cancer treatment decisions (venetoclax eligibility).
BCL2.fun_fact: BCL-2 was the first oncogene discovered that promotes cancer by preventing death rather than accelerating growth. It redefined cancer biology — proving tumors don't just grow too fast, they also refuse to die.

IL1B.editorial_description: The inflammation amplifier. IL-1β is a master inflammatory cytokine that drives fever, pain, and tissue destruction. Blocking it (with anakinra or canakinumab) treats everything from gout to heart attacks.
IL1B.what_it_means: IL-1β is produced by activated immune cells and amplifies inflammation by recruiting more immune cells and inducing fever. It requires inflammasome activation to be released — a tightly controlled two-step process.
IL1B.carrier_note: The -511 C>T promoter variant (rs16944) is carried by ~30-40% of people and increases IL-1β production. It's linked to slightly stronger inflammatory responses and periodontitis risk.
IL1B.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at promoter SNPs suggests higher IL-1β expression — more robust acute inflammation but potentially more chronic inflammatory risk.
IL1B.actionability: Anti-inflammatory lifestyle (exercise, omega-3s, oral hygiene) benefits everyone. High-expression carriers may particularly benefit from periodontal care.
IL1B.fun_fact: The CANTOS trial proved that blocking IL-1β with canakinumab reduced heart attacks by 15% — the first proof that pure anti-inflammatory therapy prevents cardiovascular events, independent of cholesterol.

IL10.editorial_description: The peacekeeper cytokine. While most interleukins amplify inflammation, IL-10 shuts it down. It's the immune system's 'stand down' signal — preventing collateral damage after infections are cleared.
IL10.what_it_means: IL-10 suppresses inflammatory cytokine production and antigen presentation. It's critical for preventing autoimmunity and limiting tissue damage during immune responses. Low IL-10 = chronic inflammation.
IL10.carrier_note: Promoter variants (rs1800896, rs1800871) affect IL-10 production. Low-producer genotypes are found in ~20-30% of Europeans and associated with slightly higher autoimmune and inflammatory disease risk.
IL10.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference promoter variants may indicate higher or lower IL-10 production depending on the specific SNP. Context-dependent — check which variant is called.
IL10.actionability: IL-10 is boosted by exercise (especially moderate intensity), meditation, and adequate sleep. Anti-inflammatory lifestyle supports IL-10 pathways.
IL10.fun_fact: IL-10 was originally called 'cytokine synthesis inhibitory factor' because it stops other cytokines from being made. Parasitic worms produce IL-10 mimics to suppress your immune response — molecular camouflage.

HLA-DRB1.editorial_description: The autoimmune susceptibility gene. HLA-DRB1 presents protein fragments to helper T cells. Specific alleles are the strongest genetic risk factors for rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes, and multiple sclerosis.
HLA-DRB1.what_it_means: HLA-DRB1 is an MHC class II molecule that presents extracellular peptides to CD4+ T cells. Which peptides it presents depends on its shape — and certain shapes accidentally present self-proteins, triggering autoimmunity.
HLA-DRB1.carrier_note: HLA-DRB1*04:01 increases RA risk ~4x. HLA-DRB1*15:01 increases MS risk ~3x. But most carriers never develop these diseases — additional triggers (infections, smoking, vitamin D) are required.
HLA-DRB1.nonref_interpretation: Consumer array HLA typing is imprecise. The region's extreme polymorphism makes SNP-based allele calling unreliable. Clinical HLA typing uses sequence-based methods.
HLA-DRB1.actionability: Context for autoimmune risk. Modifiable co-factors (smoking cessation for RA, vitamin D for MS) may be especially valuable for high-risk HLA carriers.
HLA-DRB1.fun_fact: The HLA region is the most gene-dense and polymorphic region in the human genome. Natural selection maintains thousands of alleles because heterozygotes (two different alleles) fight a wider range of pathogens.

IFNG.editorial_description: The antiviral warrior cytokine. Interferon-gamma activates macrophages, kills intracellular pathogens, and coordinates the Th1 immune response. It's the reason your immune system can clear viruses and tuberculosis.
IFNG.what_it_means: IFN-γ is produced by T cells and NK cells to activate cell-mediated immunity. It makes macrophages better killers, upregulates antigen presentation, and inhibits viral replication directly.
IFNG.carrier_note: The +874 T>A variant (rs2430561) affects IFN-γ production. The A allele (lower production) is found in ~40-50% of people and associated with slightly higher susceptibility to intracellular infections like TB.
IFNG.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered IFN-γ production capacity. Relevant context for understanding individual variation in antiviral and antimycobacterial immunity.
IFNG.actionability: General immune health practices: sleep, exercise, nutrition. IFN-γ variants are most relevant in populations with high TB exposure.
IFNG.fun_fact: IFN-γ is the only type II interferon (types I and III have many members). It's so potent at activating macrophages that it's used as a drug (Actimmune) for chronic granulomatous disease patients who can't kill intracellular bacteria.

TGFB1.editorial_description: The tissue remodeler. TGF-β1 is biology's most context-dependent molecule — it suppresses early cancers but promotes late-stage metastasis, heals wounds but causes fibrosis, and calms immunity but enables tumor immune evasion.
TGFB1.what_it_means: TGF-β1 controls cell growth, differentiation, and extracellular matrix production. In early cancer it's tumor-suppressive (growth arrest). In late cancer it's tumor-promoting (EMT, immune suppression, metastasis).
TGFB1.carrier_note: Common variants (e.g., rs1800469, rs1982073) affect TGF-β1 levels and are carried by 30-50% of people. High-producer genotypes are linked to fibrosis risk; low-producers to inflammatory conditions.
TGFB1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate higher or lower TGF-β1 production. Both directions have trade-offs — there's no clearly 'good' or 'bad' allele.
TGFB1.actionability: Mostly research context. TGF-β biology is too context-dependent for simple lifestyle recommendations based on genotype.
TGFB1.fun_fact: TGF-β1 is the most cited cytokine in scientific literature. Its dual nature (tumor suppressor AND promoter) has confused researchers for decades and is called the 'TGF-β paradox' — the same molecule does opposite things depending on timing.

PTGS2.editorial_description: Better known as COX-2 — the target of ibuprofen, aspirin, and celecoxib. PTGS2 produces prostaglandins that cause pain, fever, and inflammation. It's arguably the most commercially important drug target in history.
PTGS2.what_it_means: COX-2 converts arachidonic acid into prostaglandins at sites of inflammation and injury. Unlike COX-1 (constitutive), COX-2 is induced only during inflammation — which is why selective COX-2 inhibitors were developed.
PTGS2.carrier_note: Common promoter variants (e.g., rs20417 -765G>C) affect COX-2 expression and are found in ~15-25% of people. Lower-expression variants are associated with reduced colorectal cancer risk.
PTGS2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at promoter SNPs may indicate altered COX-2 inducibility. Potentially relevant for NSAID response and colorectal cancer risk context.
PTGS2.actionability: Context for NSAID use and colorectal cancer screening discussions. Regular aspirin use reduces colorectal cancer partly through COX-2 inhibition.
PTGS2.fun_fact: Aspirin was used for 100 years before anyone knew it worked by blocking COX. John Vane won the 1982 Nobel Prize for figuring this out. The selective COX-2 inhibitor Vioxx was later withdrawn after causing heart attacks — one of the biggest drug safety scandals in history.

CCR5.editorial_description: The HIV doorway gene. CCR5 is the receptor HIV uses to enter immune cells. People with the Δ32 deletion are naturally resistant to HIV — and this mutation was used to cure the 'Berlin Patient' of AIDS in 2008.
CCR5.what_it_means: CCR5 is a chemokine receptor on T cells. HIV binds it to gain entry. The Δ32 deletion removes part of the receptor, making it non-functional — so HIV can't get in.
CCR5.carrier_note: The Δ32 deletion is carried by ~10% of Europeans (heterozygous) and ~1% are homozygous (highly resistant to HIV). It's virtually absent in African and Asian populations. Heterozygotes have partial protection and slower disease progression.
CCR5.nonref_interpretation: The key variant is a 32bp deletion that most arrays can't directly genotype. Proxy SNPs may be used. Homozygous deletion = strong HIV resistance.
CCR5.actionability: Interesting self-knowledge. Homozygous Δ32 carriers are near-immune to R5-tropic HIV. Does NOT eliminate need for safe practices (X4-tropic strains use a different receptor).
CCR5.fun_fact: Timothy Ray Brown (the 'Berlin Patient') was cured of HIV by receiving a bone marrow transplant from a Δ32 homozygous donor. He is one of only a handful of people ever cured of AIDS. The gene-editing baby scandal in China targeted this exact gene.

SIRT1.editorial_description: The resveratrol gene — or rather, the gene resveratrol was supposed to activate. SIRT1 is a deacetylase that senses energy status and promotes cellular maintenance. It's why caloric restriction extends lifespan in lab animals.
SIRT1.what_it_means: SIRT1 removes acetyl groups from proteins involved in metabolism, stress resistance, and inflammation. When energy is scarce (fasting, exercise), SIRT1 activates repair and recycling programs.
SIRT1.carrier_note: Common SIRT1 variants have small effects on metabolic traits. The gene is highly conserved — damaging variants are selected against. Most genetic variation is regulatory and subtle.
SIRT1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are mostly in regulatory regions with modest effects on SIRT1 expression. Individual impact is small compared to lifestyle activation of the pathway.
SIRT1.actionability: SIRT1 is activated by fasting, exercise, and NAD+ precursors (NMN, NR). Resveratrol's effect is debated — the doses in wine are far too low to matter.
SIRT1.fun_fact: The resveratrol-SIRT1 story launched a billion-dollar supplement industry and a $720M pharma acquisition (Sirtris by GSK in 2008). The drug program was later shut down. The science remains contested, but SIRT1 itself is real.

LMNA.editorial_description: The progeria gene. LMNA provides structural scaffolding for the cell nucleus. Mutations cause Hutchinson-Gilford progeria (rapid aging in children) and reveal what happens when nuclear architecture fails.
LMNA.what_it_means: Lamin A/C forms a mesh inside the nuclear envelope that supports DNA organization. The progeria mutation creates a toxic truncated protein (progerin) that distorts the nucleus and accelerates aging hallmarks.
LMNA.carrier_note: Progeria mutations are de novo (not inherited) and affect ~1 in 4-8 million births. Common LMNA variants are benign. Some rarer mutations cause dilated cardiomyopathy or muscular dystrophy in adults.
LMNA.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants on consumer arrays are common polymorphisms. True pathogenic LMNA mutations are clinically sequenced in families with cardiomyopathy or muscular dystrophy.
LMNA.actionability: Families with unexplained dilated cardiomyopathy or muscular dystrophy should consider LMNA screening. Common variant carriers need no action.
LMNA.fun_fact: All of us accumulate small amounts of progerin (the toxic protein) as we age normally. Progeria children experience a horrifyingly accelerated version of natural aging — teaching us that nuclear structure is fundamental to the aging process.

HFE.editorial_description: The iron overload gene. HFE mutations cause hereditary hemochromatosis — one of the most common genetic diseases in Northern Europeans. Your body absorbs too much iron, slowly damaging organs over decades.
HFE.what_it_means: HFE regulates hepcidin, the master iron-control hormone. When HFE is broken, hepcidin stays low and iron absorption is unrestrained. Iron accumulates in liver, heart, joints, and pancreas.
HFE.carrier_note: C282Y homozygosity (the main risk genotype) is found in ~1 in 200 Northern Europeans. About 30% of male and 1-2% of female C282Y homozygotes develop clinical iron overload. H63D is much milder.
HFE.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs1800562 (C282Y) or rs1799945 (H63D) indicates carrier or at-risk status. C282Y homozygotes should monitor ferritin levels.
HFE.actionability: C282Y homozygotes should get serum ferritin tested regularly. Treatment is simple — periodic blood donation or phlebotomy. Early detection completely prevents organ damage.
HFE.fun_fact: Hemochromatosis is called the 'Celtic curse' due to its high frequency in Irish populations (~1 in 83 are homozygous). The C282Y mutation arose ~60 generations ago and may have been advantageous during iron-poor diets and frequent blood loss.

DRD2.editorial_description: The reward receptor gene. DRD2 encodes the dopamine D2 receptor — the brain's primary reward sensor. Variants affect addiction susceptibility, pleasure sensitivity, and are the target of all antipsychotic medications.
DRD2.what_it_means: D2 receptors in the striatum mediate reward, motivation, and pleasure. Fewer D2 receptors = reduced reward sensitivity = potentially more reward-seeking behavior (food, substances, gambling) to compensate.
DRD2.carrier_note: The Taq1A variant (rs1800497, actually in ANKK1 nearby) is carried by ~30-40% of people. A1 carriers have modestly fewer D2 receptors. It's one of many variants affecting addiction risk — not deterministic.
DRD2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs1800497 indicates the A1 allele (reduced D2 receptor density). Provides context for reward sensitivity but doesn't predict addiction.
DRD2.actionability: Self-knowledge for understanding reward sensitivity. A1 carriers may benefit from awareness of addictive tendencies and building alternative reward systems (exercise, social connection).
DRD2.fun_fact: Every antipsychotic drug ever made blocks D2 receptors — it's the one mechanism all of them share. DRD2 is also why Parkinson's drugs (which boost dopamine) can cause compulsive gambling as a side effect.

NLRP3.editorial_description: The inflammasome. NLRP3 is an intracellular danger sensor that assembles into a molecular machine to trigger IL-1β release and pyroptosis (inflammatory cell death). It's central to gout, atherosclerosis, and Alzheimer's inflammation.
NLRP3.what_it_means: NLRP3 detects cellular danger signals (crystals, ATP, misfolded proteins) and assembles the inflammasome complex. This activates caspase-1, which processes IL-1β and IL-18 into their active inflammatory forms.
NLRP3.carrier_note: Gain-of-function NLRP3 mutations cause rare autoinflammatory syndromes (CAPS). Common variants modestly affect inflammasome activation threshold and are not pathogenic.
NLRP3.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants in NLRP3 are mostly regulatory with subtle effects on inflammatory responses. Not clinically actionable for most people.
NLRP3.actionability: NLRP3 is inhibited by autophagy and anti-inflammatory signals. Fasting, exercise, and omega-3s reduce inflammasome activation. Directly relevant for gout and metabolic inflammation.
NLRP3.fun_fact: Uric acid crystals (gout), cholesterol crystals (atherosclerosis), and amyloid-β (Alzheimer's) all activate NLRP3 — making it a unifying mechanism behind seemingly unrelated diseases of aging.

LDLR.editorial_description: The cholesterol vacuum cleaner. LDLR on liver cells pulls LDL from blood. When broken, LDL accumulates — causing familial hypercholesterolemia (FH), the most common lethal genetic disease. Statins work by upregulating LDLR.
LDLR.what_it_means: LDLR binds LDL particles and internalizes them for recycling. The liver clears ~70% of blood LDL this way. FH mutations reduce LDLR function, causing lifelong LDL elevation and premature atherosclerosis.
LDLR.carrier_note: Heterozygous FH affects ~1 in 250 people (often undiagnosed). LDL is typically 2-3x normal from birth. Homozygous FH (~1 in 300,000) causes heart attacks in childhood without treatment.
LDLR.nonref_interpretation: Common variants near LDLR modestly affect LDL levels in GWAS. True FH-causing mutations are rare and require clinical sequencing of the full gene.
LDLR.actionability: If LDL is very high (>190 mg/dL) without obvious lifestyle causes, consider FH testing. Early statin treatment in FH normalizes lifespan. Cascade family screening saves lives.
LDLR.fun_fact: Brown and Goldstein won the 1985 Nobel Prize for discovering LDLR and the cholesterol feedback system. Their work directly led to statins — now taken by over 200 million people worldwide.

SOD2.editorial_description: Your mitochondrial fire extinguisher. SOD2 neutralizes superoxide radicals inside mitochondria — the most dangerous reactive oxygen species your cells produce. A common variant determines how efficiently yours works.
SOD2.what_it_means: SOD2 (MnSOD) converts superoxide to hydrogen peroxide inside mitochondria. The Ala16Val variant (rs4880) affects how efficiently the protein enters mitochondria — Ala/Ala gets in fastest.
SOD2.carrier_note: The Val allele is carried by ~50% of most populations. Val/Val has ~30-40% less mitochondrial SOD2 activity. It's one of the most studied antioxidant gene variants but individual health effects are modest.
SOD2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs4880 indicates the Val allele (less efficient mitochondrial targeting). Homozygous Val/Val suggests higher baseline mitochondrial oxidative stress.
SOD2.actionability: Mitochondrial antioxidant support: CoQ10, adequate manganese (SOD2's cofactor), and regular exercise (which upregulates SOD2 expression regardless of genotype).
SOD2.fun_fact: SOD2 knockout mice die within days of birth from massive oxidative damage — one of the few single-gene knockouts that's immediately lethal. Your mitochondria produce superoxide constantly; without SOD2, they'd self-destruct.

CYP2D6.editorial_description: The most important pharmacogenomic gene. CYP2D6 metabolizes ~25% of all prescription drugs. You're either a poor, intermediate, normal, or ultra-rapid metabolizer — and it dramatically changes what doses work for you.
CYP2D6.what_it_means: CYP2D6 is a liver enzyme that breaks down codeine, tamoxifen, antidepressants, beta-blockers, and antipsychotics. Gene duplications make ultra-rapid metabolizers; deletions/mutations create poor metabolizers.
CYP2D6.carrier_note: ~7-10% of Europeans are poor metabolizers (two non-functional copies). ~1-2% are ultra-rapid (gene duplications). These extremes have real clinical consequences for many common medications.
CYP2D6.nonref_interpretation: CYP2D6 is extremely complex — gene deletions, duplications, and dozens of alleles. Consumer arrays capture some but not all variation. Clinical pharmacogenomic panels are more comprehensive.
CYP2D6.actionability: Consider pharmacogenomic testing before starting CYP2D6 substrates (codeine, tamoxifen, many antidepressants). Poor metabolizers get no pain relief from codeine; ultra-rapid metabolizers risk overdose.
CYP2D6.fun_fact: Ultra-rapid metabolizers convert codeine to morphine so fast it can be lethal — especially in breastfeeding mothers (morphine passes to infant). This led to codeine being contraindicated in children in many countries.

CYP2C19.editorial_description: The clopidogrel gene. CYP2C19 activates the blood thinner Plavix. Poor metabolizers can't activate it — meaning the drug doesn't work, leaving them unprotected after heart stents. One of pharmacogenomics' clearest success stories.
CYP2C19.what_it_means: CYP2C19 metabolizes clopidogrel (prodrug activation), PPIs (omeprazole), some antidepressants, and antifungals. Loss-of-function alleles (*2, *3) are common and have clear clinical consequences.
CYP2C19.carrier_note: ~2-5% of Europeans and ~15-20% of East Asians are poor metabolizers. The *17 gain-of-function allele (rapid metabolizer) is found in ~20-30% of Europeans.
CYP2C19.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants likely indicate *2 or *3 (loss-of-function) or *17 (gain-of-function) alleles. Directly relevant for clopidogrel and PPI dosing.
CYP2C19.actionability: CYP2C19 testing is recommended before clopidogrel by multiple guidelines. Poor metabolizers should use alternative antiplatelet agents (prasugrel, ticagrelor).
CYP2C19.fun_fact: The FDA added a boxed warning to clopidogrel about CYP2C19 poor metabolizers in 2010. It's one of the few cases where a genetic test directly changes which drug you should receive — pharmacogenomics in real clinical practice.

F5.editorial_description: Factor V Leiden — the most common inherited clotting disorder. A single mutation makes Factor V resistant to being turned off, increasing blood clot risk. Carried by ~5% of Europeans.
F5.what_it_means: Factor V is a clotting factor normally inactivated by Protein C. The Leiden mutation (R506Q, rs6025) makes it resistant to inactivation, tipping the coagulation balance toward clotting.
F5.carrier_note: Heterozygous carriers (~5% of Europeans) have 3-8x higher DVT risk. Homozygotes (~1 in 5,000) have 50-80x risk. Most carriers never clot — but combined with other risk factors (pills, surgery, immobility), risk compounds.
F5.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs6025 indicates Factor V Leiden carrier status. One copy = moderate risk increase; two copies = significant clinical concern.
F5.actionability: Carriers should inform surgeons (prophylactic anticoagulation may be needed), avoid estrogen-containing contraceptives if possible, and stay mobile during long travel. Genetic counseling recommended.
F5.fun_fact: Factor V Leiden is named after the Dutch city of Leiden where it was discovered in 1994. It's so common in Europeans (5%) that it may have been positively selected — possibly by reducing fatal postpartum hemorrhage.

APC.editorial_description: The colon cancer gatekeeper. APC mutations initiate the vast majority of colorectal cancers. Germline mutations cause thousands of polyps by age 20 (FAP syndrome) — making colonoscopy literally life-saving.
APC.what_it_means: APC regulates the Wnt signaling pathway by targeting beta-catenin for destruction. When APC is lost, Wnt signaling goes haywire, driving uncontrolled cell proliferation in the colon.
APC.carrier_note: Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) from germline APC mutations affects ~1 in 10,000. The common I1307K variant in Ashkenazi Jewish populations (~6%) modestly increases colorectal cancer risk.
APC.nonref_interpretation: Common non-reference variants are generally benign. True pathogenic truncating mutations cause FAP and require clinical-grade sequencing to detect reliably.
APC.actionability: FAP carriers need annual colonoscopy from early teens and typically require prophylactic colectomy. For common variants, standard colorectal screening guidelines apply.
APC.fun_fact: APC was the key to Vogelstein's famous 'adenoma-carcinoma sequence' model showing cancer develops through accumulating mutations. APC loss is almost always Step 1 — the initiating event in colon cancer.

PON1.editorial_description: Your pesticide detox enzyme. PON1 breaks down organophosphate pesticides and prevents LDL oxidation. A common variant creates 8-fold variation in enzyme activity between people — meaning pesticide sensitivity varies enormously.
PON1.what_it_means: PON1 (paraoxonase 1) hydrolyzes organophosphates, nerve agents, and oxidized lipids. The Q192R variant (rs662) dramatically affects activity toward specific substrates. It's carried on HDL particles.
PON1.carrier_note: The R192 variant is found in ~30% of Europeans (~70% in some Asian populations). Which allele is 'better' depends on the substrate — R is faster at paraoxon but Q is better at other organophosphates.
PON1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs662 indicates R192 (different substrate specificity). Neither allele is simply 'good' or 'bad' — context matters.
PON1.actionability: Low PON1 activity individuals may benefit from reducing organophosphate pesticide exposure (choosing organic for high-spray crops). PON1 activity increases with moderate alcohol and decreases with smoking.
PON1.fun_fact: PON1 was named for its ability to break down paraoxon (a nerve agent metabolite). Military researchers studied it to understand why some soldiers were more vulnerable to nerve gas. The 8-fold activity variation between people is one of the largest for any common enzyme variant.

ADRB2.editorial_description: The adrenaline receptor gene. ADRB2 is what beta-agonist inhalers (salbutamol/albuterol) target in asthma, and what adrenaline activates during fight-or-flight. Variants affect both asthma drug response and exercise performance.
ADRB2.what_it_means: Beta-2 adrenergic receptors relax smooth muscle in airways and blood vessels. Two common variants (Arg16Gly, Gln27Glu) affect receptor regulation — how quickly it desensitizes with repeated stimulation.
ADRB2.carrier_note: Gly16 is found in ~40-65% of populations and associated with enhanced receptor downregulation (reduced response to repeated beta-agonist use). Both variants are extremely common.
ADRB2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants indicate altered receptor regulation dynamics. May provide context for beta-agonist inhaler response or exercise-induced bronchodilation.
ADRB2.actionability: Relevant for asthma management — Arg16Arg individuals may have better long-term response to regular beta-agonist use. Discuss inhaler strategy with your doctor.
ADRB2.fun_fact: ADRB2 variants are overrepresented in elite endurance athletes. The receptor mediates fat mobilization during exercise, vasodilation in muscles, and airway opening — all performance-relevant during sustained effort.

ATM.editorial_description: The DNA damage alarm. ATM senses double-strand breaks and mobilizes the entire DNA repair system. It's the first responder to radiation damage — and heterozygous carriers have moderately increased breast cancer risk.
ATM.what_it_means: ATM (Ataxia-Telangiectasia Mutated) is a kinase that detects DNA breaks and phosphorylates p53, BRCA1, and checkpoint proteins. It coordinates the decision between repair, cell cycle arrest, and apoptosis.
ATM.carrier_note: Biallelic ATM loss causes ataxia-telangiectasia (rare, ~1 in 40,000-100,000). Heterozygous carriers (~1-2% of population) have 2-3x increased breast cancer risk — moderate, not as high as BRCA1/2.
ATM.nonref_interpretation: Common variants near ATM contribute to polygenic cancer risk. Rare pathogenic variants require clinical sequencing. Consumer arrays may flag some known pathogenic variants.
ATM.actionability: Heterozygous carriers of pathogenic ATM variants should discuss enhanced breast screening (MRI) with a genetic counselor. Radiation sensitivity means mammogram timing may need consideration.
ATM.fun_fact: ATM carriers may be more sensitive to radiation (including diagnostic X-rays and CT scans). This is because they have only one working copy of the gene that repairs radiation-induced DNA breaks — half the usual capacity.

ACE2.editorial_description: The COVID receptor. ACE2 became the world's most famous protein in 2020 — it's the doorway SARS-CoV-2 uses to enter human cells. Variants were immediately studied for susceptibility differences between populations.
ACE2.what_it_means: ACE2 normally converts angiotensin II to angiotensin 1-7 (protective, vasodilatory). SARS-CoV-2 spike protein binds ACE2 to enter cells. The virus essentially hijacks a cardiovascular protective enzyme.
ACE2.carrier_note: Despite intense study, no common ACE2 variant has been convincingly shown to dramatically alter COVID-19 susceptibility. ACE2 is on the X chromosome, so males are hemizygous (one copy).
ACE2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants in ACE2 have been extensively studied since 2020. Most have negligible effects on COVID susceptibility. The real risk factors are age, comorbidities, and immune status.
