ACE2.actionability: ACE2 genotype is not a useful predictor of COVID severity. Vaccination, overall health, and age matter far more than ACE2 variants.
ACE2.fun_fact: ACE2 was an obscure gene studied by a small community of cardiovascular researchers. Overnight in January 2020, it became the most intensely studied protein on Earth. More ACE2 papers were published in 2020 than in the prior 20 years combined.

VHL.editorial_description: The oxygen-sensing tumor suppressor. VHL degrades HIF when oxygen is present. When VHL is lost, cells act as if oxygen-starved — growing new blood vessels and proliferating. It causes clear cell kidney cancer and links to the Nobel Prize-winning HIF pathway.
VHL.what_it_means: VHL tags HIF-α for destruction under normal oxygen. Without VHL, HIF accumulates and activates hypoxia genes (VEGF, EPO, glycolysis) even in oxygen-rich conditions — feeding tumor growth.
VHL.carrier_note: Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome (germline VHL mutations) affects ~1 in 36,000. It causes kidney cancer, pheochromocytomas, and hemangioblastomas. Common variants near VHL are benign.
VHL.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference common variants near VHL are not pathogenic. Clinical VHL mutations are rare and typically identified through family history of VHL-associated tumors.
VHL.actionability: VHL syndrome carriers need lifelong surveillance (renal imaging, catecholamine testing). For common variants, no action needed.
VHL.fun_fact: VHL is the mechanistic link behind the 2019 Nobel Prize in Physiology (oxygen sensing). The VHL-HIF pathway explains why Chuvash polycythemia patients (a VHL variant) have elevated red blood cells — they permanently sense low oxygen.

MSH2.editorial_description: The DNA spellchecker. MSH2 detects and repairs mismatched DNA bases after replication. Germline mutations cause Lynch syndrome — the most common hereditary cancer predisposition, causing colorectal and endometrial cancer.
MSH2.what_it_means: MSH2 scans newly replicated DNA for errors (mismatches, small insertions/deletions). It forms a heterodimer with MSH6 to recognize mistakes. Without it, mutations accumulate rapidly in dividing cells.
MSH2.carrier_note: Lynch syndrome (from MSH2, MLH1, MSH6, or PMS2 mutations) affects ~1 in 280 people — far more common than previously thought. Lifetime colorectal cancer risk is 40-80% without surveillance.
MSH2.nonref_interpretation: Common variants are benign polymorphisms. Pathogenic mutations typically truncate the protein and require clinical-grade gene panel testing.
MSH2.actionability: Lynch syndrome carriers need colonoscopy every 1-2 years starting at age 20-25. Aspirin reduces cancer risk in Lynch. Cascade testing of relatives is critical.
MSH2.fun_fact: Lynch syndrome was originally called HNPCC (Hereditary Non-Polyposis Colorectal Cancer). Unlike FAP (thousands of polyps), Lynch causes few polyps that transform to cancer unusually fast — which is why annual colonoscopy is needed.

MLH1.editorial_description: The other Lynch syndrome gene. MLH1 works with MSH2 to repair DNA replication errors. Its loss causes microsatellite instability — now a biomarker that predicts response to immunotherapy across all cancer types.
MLH1.what_it_means: MLH1 coordinates the downstream repair after MSH2 detects a mismatch. It recruits the excision and resynthesis machinery. MLH1 silencing (by promoter methylation) is also extremely common in sporadic colorectal cancer.
MLH1.carrier_note: Germline MLH1 mutations carry the highest Lynch syndrome cancer risks (~50-80% lifetime colorectal cancer). MLH1 is also the most commonly silenced mismatch repair gene in sporadic (non-hereditary) tumors.
MLH1.nonref_interpretation: Common variants are benign. Pathogenic mutations require clinical testing. Consumer arrays may detect some known pathogenic variants but miss many.
MLH1.actionability: Same as MSH2 — early colonoscopy, aspirin consideration, cascade family testing. MLH1-deficient tumors respond well to checkpoint immunotherapy.
MLH1.fun_fact: Tumors that lose MLH1 accumulate so many mutations they become 'hypermutated' — paradoxically making them MORE visible to the immune system. This is why MSI-high cancers respond dramatically to immunotherapy regardless of organ of origin.

CTLA4.editorial_description: The immune brake that won a Nobel Prize. CTLA-4 tells T cells to stand down. Blocking it with ipilimumab unleashes anti-tumor immunity — the first checkpoint immunotherapy, which launched the cancer immunotherapy revolution.
CTLA4.what_it_means: CTLA-4 competes with CD28 for binding B7 ligands on antigen-presenting cells. When CTLA-4 wins, T cell activation is suppressed. It prevents autoimmunity but also allows tumors to escape immune destruction.
CTLA4.carrier_note: Common CTLA4 variants (e.g., +49 A>G, rs231775) affect expression levels and are associated with autoimmune disease risk (type 1 diabetes, thyroiditis, Graves'). They're carried by 30-50% of people.
CTLA4.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered CTLA-4 expression — potentially affecting autoimmune susceptibility. Effects are modest and polygenic.
CTLA4.actionability: Context for autoimmune risk. No direct action for common variants. CTLA-4 blocking drugs (ipilimumab) are prescribed for melanoma and other cancers.
CTLA4.fun_fact: James Allison won the 2018 Nobel Prize for discovering that blocking CTLA-4 unleashes anti-cancer immunity. Ipilimumab was the first drug to extend melanoma survival — some patients treated in early trials are still alive 15+ years later.

GHRL.editorial_description: The hunger hormone gene. Ghrelin is produced by your stomach when it's empty, telling your brain 'time to eat.' It rises before meals, drops after eating, and surges during dieting — which is why sustained weight loss is so hard.
GHRL.what_it_means: Ghrelin is the only known orexigenic (appetite-stimulating) gut hormone. It signals through the growth hormone secretagogue receptor in the hypothalamus. Variants affect baseline ghrelin levels and hunger signaling.
GHRL.carrier_note: Common GHRL variants (e.g., Leu72Met, rs696217) are found in ~5-15% of populations. Effects on BMI and eating behavior are statistically detectable but individually small.
GHRL.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may modestly alter ghrelin levels or receptor binding. Context for understanding appetite regulation but not predictive of obesity.
GHRL.actionability: Ghrelin is suppressed by protein-rich meals, adequate sleep, and regular meal timing. Understanding hunger hormones can help with sustainable eating patterns.
GHRL.fun_fact: Ghrelin was named as a play on 'ghre' (Proto-Indo-European root for 'grow') because it stimulates growth hormone release. After gastric bypass surgery, ghrelin drops dramatically — which is partly why patients lose appetite, not just stomach capacity.

LEP.editorial_description: The satiety hormone gene. Leptin tells your brain how much fat you have stored. The discovery of leptin in 1994 proved obesity has biological underpinnings — though the story turned out far more complex than a simple 'thin gene.'
LEP.what_it_means: Fat cells secrete leptin proportional to their size. The brain reads leptin as a satiety signal — high leptin = enough stored energy = reduce appetite. In rare leptin deficiency, appetite is insatiable.
LEP.carrier_note: Congenital leptin deficiency is extremely rare (~50 cases worldwide) and causes severe childhood obesity. Common LEP variants have tiny effects. Most obese people have HIGH leptin (leptin resistance), not low.
LEP.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants in LEP have minimal individual effects. The obesity epidemic is primarily about leptin resistance (the brain ignoring the signal), not leptin production variants.
LEP.actionability: Leptin sensitivity is improved by sleep, exercise, and reducing processed food. Leptin replacement only works for the extremely rare cases of true deficiency.
LEP.fun_fact: Jeffrey Friedman's 1994 discovery of leptin set off a billion-dollar pharmaceutical race. Amgen paid $20 million for rights, expecting a simple obesity cure. It failed — because obese people already have too much leptin. They just can't respond to it.

JAK2.editorial_description: The blood cancer switch. The JAK2 V617F mutation is found in 95% of polycythemia vera and ~50% of essential thrombocythemia. It's one of the most specific mutation-disease relationships in all of oncology.
JAK2.what_it_means: JAK2 transduces signals from cytokine receptors (EPO, TPO). The V617F somatic mutation makes it constitutively active — driving overproduction of red blood cells, platelets, or white cells depending on context.
JAK2.carrier_note: JAK2 V617F is a somatic mutation (acquired, not inherited). Consumer arrays test germline DNA and won't detect it. It's diagnosed via blood testing when blood counts are abnormal.
JAK2.nonref_interpretation: Germline variants in JAK2 are mostly benign polymorphisms. The clinically important V617F is somatic and requires specific molecular testing on blood.
JAK2.actionability: No germline action needed. JAK2 V617F testing is done on blood when myeloproliferative neoplasms are suspected (elevated blood counts). JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib) target this pathway.
JAK2.fun_fact: JAK2 V617F was discovered simultaneously by four independent groups in 2005 — one of the most dramatic races in cancer genetics. Within a decade, JAK inhibitors became standard treatment, transforming myelofibrosis from untreatable to manageable.

LRRK2.editorial_description: The most common genetic cause of Parkinson's disease. LRRK2 mutations cause a form of Parkinson's clinically identical to the sporadic disease — making it the most promising drug target for slowing neurodegeneration in broader Parkinson's.
LRRK2.what_it_means: LRRK2 is a large kinase involved in vesicle trafficking, autophagy, and immune function. The G2019S mutation (most common) increases kinase activity, leading to neuronal toxicity through multiple mechanisms.
LRRK2.carrier_note: G2019S is found in 1-2% of sporadic Parkinson's and ~5% of familial cases. It's much more common in Ashkenazi Jewish (~15-20% of PD cases) and North African Berber populations. Penetrance is incomplete (~30-75% by age 80).
LRRK2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs34637584 (G2019S) indicates the most common pathogenic LRRK2 variant. Carrier status is clinically significant but penetrance is incomplete.
LRRK2.actionability: LRRK2 carriers should be aware of early Parkinson's symptoms (smell loss, REM sleep behavior disorder). LRRK2 kinase inhibitors are in clinical trials — carriers may be eligible for preventive trials.
LRRK2.fun_fact: LRRK2 is nicknamed 'dardarin' from the Basque word 'dardara' (tremor) because it was first identified in Basque families with Parkinson's. It's one of the largest human proteins (2,527 amino acids) with an unusual combination of kinase and GTPase domains.

NFKB1.editorial_description: The master inflammation switch. NF-κB is activated by almost every immune threat and controls hundreds of inflammatory genes. It's the convergence point for innate immunity, cancer, and aging — and the target of aspirin and corticosteroids.
NFKB1.what_it_means: NF-κB sits inactive in the cytoplasm until danger signals (TNF, LPS, viruses) trigger IκB degradation, releasing it to enter the nucleus and activate inflammatory genes en masse.
NFKB1.carrier_note: Common NFKB1 variants (e.g., -94 ins/del ATTG, rs28362491) affect NF-κB expression and are found in ~30-40% of people. The deletion allele is associated with slightly higher inflammatory disease risk.
NFKB1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered NF-κB baseline expression. Effects are modest and interact with many environmental factors.
NFKB1.actionability: NF-κB is suppressed by exercise, omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, and corticosteroids. Chronic NF-κB activation drives 'inflammaging' — anti-inflammatory lifestyle broadly beneficial.
NFKB1.fun_fact: NF-κB was discovered by David Baltimore (a Nobel laureate for reverse transcriptase) in 1986. It's now implicated in virtually every inflammatory disease and every cancer. Over 75,000 papers reference it.

PDCD1.editorial_description: Better known as PD-1 (Programmed Death-1) — the 'don't kill me' receptor on T cells. Tumors exploit PD-1 to hide from immunity. Blocking it with pembrolizumab (Keytruda) or nivolumab (Opdivo) has become the backbone of modern cancer immunotherapy.
PDCD1.what_it_means: PD-1 on T cells binds PD-L1 on target cells, delivering an inhibitory signal that prevents killing. This normally prevents autoimmunity but tumors hijack it by expressing PD-L1 to evade immune destruction.
PDCD1.carrier_note: Common PDCD1 variants (e.g., PD-1.3, rs11568821) are associated with autoimmune disease susceptibility. They affect PD-1 expression and threshold for T cell inhibition.
PDCD1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered PD-1 expression — potentially affecting autoimmune risk. Not predictive of cancer immunotherapy response.
PDCD1.actionability: Context for autoimmune risk. PD-1 inhibitor drugs are prescribed based on tumor characteristics (PD-L1 expression, MSI status), not patient germline genotype.
PDCD1.fun_fact: Tasuku Honjo discovered PD-1 in 1992 while studying T cell death (hence 'Programmed Death-1'). He won the 2018 Nobel Prize alongside James Allison (CTLA-4). Keytruda is now the world's best-selling drug at >$25 billion/year.

CD274.editorial_description: Better known as PD-L1 — the tumor's invisibility cloak. This gene encodes the ligand that tumors display to shut down attacking T cells. Its expression on biopsies is now one of the most ordered biomarker tests in oncology.
CD274.what_it_means: PD-L1 on cell surfaces engages PD-1 on T cells to suppress immune attack. Tumors upregulate PD-L1 to become invisible to immunity. PD-L1 expression level predicts immunotherapy response in many cancers.
CD274.carrier_note: Germline CD274 variants are poorly characterized for individual health effects. The clinically relevant question is tumor PD-L1 expression (a somatic/microenvironmental property), not germline genotype.
CD274.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants have uncertain significance for individual health. PD-L1 biology is primarily studied in tumor context.
CD274.actionability: No germline action. Tumor PD-L1 testing (by immunohistochemistry on biopsy) guides immunotherapy decisions in lung cancer, melanoma, and many other cancers.
CD274.fun_fact: PD-L1 testing has created a paradox in oncology — different drugs use different antibodies, cutoffs, and scoring systems. Two pathologists can look at the same slide and give different PD-L1 scores, making it one of the most controversial biomarkers in clinical use.

MYC.editorial_description: The 'God gene' of cancer. MYC is the most frequently amplified oncogene in human tumors, controlling up to 15% of all human genes. It's the ultimate growth accelerator — and one of the most sought-after drug targets in oncology.
MYC.what_it_means: MYC is a transcription factor that activates genes for cell growth, metabolism, and division. When overexpressed (via amplification or translocation), it drives virtually unlimited proliferation.
MYC.carrier_note: MYC amplification/translocation is somatic (tumor-only). Germline MYC variants are common polymorphisms in the 8q24 region and contribute modestly to polygenic cancer risk in GWAS.
MYC.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants in the 8q24 region near MYC are common GWAS hits for prostate, breast, and colorectal cancer risk. Effects are individually tiny but replicate robustly.
MYC.actionability: No individual germline action needed. MYC amplification in tumors indicates aggressive biology. Decades of effort to drug MYC directly have mostly failed — it's considered one of oncology's 'undruggable' targets.
MYC.fun_fact: MYC was named after myelocytomatosis (a chicken virus) where it was discovered in 1982. Despite being arguably the most important oncogene, it has resisted direct drug targeting for 40+ years. Some scientists call it 'undruggable' — though new degrader approaches show promise.

RB1.editorial_description: The first tumor suppressor ever discovered. RB1 was found through childhood eye cancer (retinoblastoma) and proved Knudson's 'two-hit hypothesis' — that both copies of a gene must be lost for cancer to develop.
RB1.what_it_means: pRB normally blocks E2F transcription factors from activating cell cycle genes. When both copies of RB1 are lost, E2F is unleashed and cells divide uncontrollably. It's the original gatekeeper of cell division.
RB1.carrier_note: Germline RB1 mutations cause hereditary retinoblastoma (~40% of cases, presenting in both eyes before age 5). Carriers also have elevated lifetime risk of osteosarcoma and other cancers.
RB1.nonref_interpretation: Common variants near RB1 are benign. Pathogenic mutations are rare and typically identified through family history of childhood eye cancer. Clinical testing required for diagnosis.
RB1.actionability: Children of RB1 mutation carriers need ophthalmologic surveillance from birth. Early detection of retinoblastoma is curative. Lifelong cancer surveillance for carriers.
RB1.fun_fact: Alfred Knudson predicted in 1971 that retinoblastoma required 'two hits' to both RB1 copies — a hereditary hit plus a somatic one. This 'two-hit hypothesis' was confirmed when RB1 was cloned in 1986 and became the foundation of tumor suppressor biology.

IDH1.editorial_description: The cancer metabolism gene. IDH1 mutations create a 'neomorphic' enzyme that produces a new oncometabolite (2-HG) not found in normal cells. This rewires epigenetics and defines a distinct subtype of brain tumors and leukemia.
IDH1.what_it_means: Normal IDH1 converts isocitrate to alpha-ketoglutarate in the citric acid cycle. Mutant IDH1 instead produces 2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG), which inhibits DNA and histone demethylases, trapping cells in an undifferentiated state.
IDH1.carrier_note: IDH1 mutations are somatic (tumor-only), found in ~80% of low-grade gliomas and ~20% of AML. They're actually a favorable prognostic sign. Germline variants are benign polymorphisms.
IDH1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants in IDH1 are benign. The oncogenic R132H mutation is somatic and not detectable on consumer arrays.
IDH1.actionability: No germline action. IDH-mutant brain tumors have better prognosis and now have targeted drugs (vorasidenib, ivosidenib) approved. Tumor IDH testing guides treatment.
IDH1.fun_fact: IDH mutations were completely unexpected — nobody thought a metabolic enzyme could be an oncogene. The discovery in 2008 created an entirely new field ('oncometabolism') and showed that cancer can be driven by abnormal metabolites, not just growth signals.

KIT.editorial_description: The stem cell receptor. KIT drives blood cell development, melanocytes (skin color), and gut pacemaker cells. Somatic KIT mutations cause GIST (gastrointestinal stromal tumor) — where imatinib (Gleevec) produces dramatic responses.
KIT.what_it_means: KIT is a receptor tyrosine kinase activated by stem cell factor. It's essential for hematopoiesis, melanocyte migration, and interstitial cells of Cajal (gut motility). Activating mutations drive several cancers.
KIT.carrier_note: Germline KIT mutations can cause piebaldism (white patches of skin/hair — harmless). Cancer-relevant KIT mutations are somatic. Consumer arrays test germline DNA.
KIT.nonref_interpretation: Germline non-reference variants are mostly benign. Some affect pigmentation. Cancer-driving KIT mutations require tumor tissue testing.
KIT.actionability: No germline action for most people. KIT-mutant GISTs respond dramatically to imatinib — one of the first precision oncology successes outside of leukemia.
KIT.fun_fact: KIT is named after a kitten — it was discovered as 'v-kit' in a feline sarcoma virus. Piebald cats (white patches) actually have natural KIT mutations, making it one of the few genes where the animal naming makes perfect sense.

NOTCH1.editorial_description: The cell fate decision-maker. NOTCH1 determines whether a cell becomes one type or another during development. In T-ALL leukemia, activating mutations trap cells as immortal immature T cells that never grow up.
NOTCH1.what_it_means: NOTCH1 is a transmembrane receptor that's cleaved upon ligand binding, releasing its intracellular domain to enter the nucleus and activate target genes. It controls differentiation decisions throughout development and adult tissue renewal.
NOTCH1.carrier_note: Activating NOTCH1 mutations are found in >50% of T-cell ALL (somatic). Loss-of-function NOTCH1 mutations occur in some solid tumors (tumor suppressor role). Germline variants are mostly benign.
NOTCH1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants are common polymorphisms. NOTCH1 is context-dependent — oncogene in blood cancers, tumor suppressor in some solid tumors.
NOTCH1.actionability: No germline action for most people. NOTCH pathway inhibitors (gamma-secretase inhibitors) are in development for T-ALL and other NOTCH-driven cancers.
NOTCH1.fun_fact: Notch was named in 1917 after the wing notches seen in Drosophila flies with mutations in this gene. A century later, it's recognized as one of the most fundamental signaling pathways in all of animal biology — conserved from worms to humans.

CYP2C9.editorial_description: The warfarin gene. CYP2C9 metabolizes the blood thinner warfarin — and poor metabolizers need dramatically lower doses to avoid life-threatening bleeding. One of the oldest and most validated pharmacogenomic applications.
CYP2C9.what_it_means: CYP2C9 inactivates S-warfarin (the more potent enantiomer). The *2 and *3 variants reduce enzyme activity by 30% and 80% respectively, causing warfarin to accumulate and over-anticoagulate.
CYP2C9.carrier_note: *2 is carried by ~15% and *3 by ~7% of Europeans. Combined with VKORC1 variants, they explain ~40% of warfarin dose variation. Homozygous *3 carriers may need less than half the standard dose.
CYP2C9.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs1799853 (*2) or rs1057910 (*3) indicates reduced warfarin metabolism. Directly relevant if warfarin is prescribed.
CYP2C9.actionability: CYP2C9 genotyping is recommended before warfarin initiation (FDA label includes pharmacogenomic dosing table). Also relevant for NSAID metabolism and some diabetes drugs.
CYP2C9.fun_fact: Warfarin was originally a rat poison (Wisconsin Alumni Research Foundation + coumarin = warfarin). It became a human drug after a soldier attempted suicide with rat poison and survived with just over-anticoagulation — suggesting it was controllable.

F2.editorial_description: Prothrombin — the second most common inherited clotting disorder after Factor V Leiden. The G20210A variant increases prothrombin levels, raising blood clot risk. Often found alongside Factor V Leiden for compounded risk.
F2.what_it_means: F2 encodes prothrombin, which is cleaved to thrombin — the key enzyme that converts fibrinogen to fibrin clots. The 20210A variant increases mRNA stability, producing ~30% more prothrombin.
F2.carrier_note: The 20210A variant (rs1799963) is carried by ~2-3% of Europeans. Heterozygotes have 2-5x increased DVT/PE risk. Combined with Factor V Leiden, oral contraceptives, or immobility, risk compounds substantially.
F2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs1799963 indicates the prothrombotic A allele. Clinically significant for thrombotic risk assessment, especially in combination with other risk factors.
F2.actionability: Carriers should inform physicians before surgery, pregnancy, or starting estrogen-containing contraceptives. Avoid prolonged immobility. Consider compression stockings for long flights.
F2.fun_fact: The coagulation cascade was worked out in the 1960s using Roman numeral factor names (I through XIII). Factor II (prothrombin) was among the first identified. Confusingly, Factor VI doesn't exist — it was later found to be activated Factor V.

SERPINE1.editorial_description: Better known as PAI-1 (Plasminogen Activator Inhibitor-1). This gene prevents your body from breaking down blood clots. A unique Amish community with loss-of-function mutations lives significantly longer and has less diabetes, suggesting PAI-1 accelerates aging.
SERPINE1.what_it_means: PAI-1 inhibits tissue plasminogen activator (tPA), blocking fibrinolysis. High PAI-1 = clots persist longer. The 4G/5G polymorphism (rs1799889) in the promoter determines expression level.
SERPINE1.carrier_note: The 4G allele (higher PAI-1) is found in ~50% of people. 4G/4G homozygotes have the highest PAI-1 levels and modestly elevated cardiovascular risk. The 5G allele = lower expression.
SERPINE1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at the 4G/5G locus indicates the 5G (lower PAI-1) allele — generally considered favorable for cardiovascular health and possibly longevity.
SERPINE1.actionability: PAI-1 is reduced by exercise and weight loss. High PAI-1 carriers (4G/4G) may benefit especially from regular physical activity and maintaining healthy weight.
SERPINE1.fun_fact: An Amish community in Indiana carries a SERPINE1 loss-of-function mutation. Carriers live 10 years longer, have 50% less diabetes, and longer telomeres. This finding launched PAI-1 inhibitors as potential anti-aging drugs.

AGT.editorial_description: Angiotensinogen — the precursor at the top of the blood pressure cascade. AGT variants affect how much raw material your RAAS system has to work with, influencing baseline blood pressure from birth.
AGT.what_it_means: AGT is cleaved by renin to produce angiotensin I, then converted by ACE to angiotensin II (vasoconstrictor). The M235T variant (rs699) increases AGT expression by ~20%, providing more substrate for the entire cascade.
AGT.carrier_note: The T235 allele is found in ~40% of Europeans and ~80% of Africans. It's associated with mildly higher blood pressure and preeclampsia risk. Effect size is modest individually.
AGT.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs699 (T235) indicates higher AGT expression. Contributes to polygenic blood pressure variation alongside ACE, NOS3, and hundreds of other loci.
AGT.actionability: Context for understanding blood pressure genetics. Standard hypertension prevention applies: sodium reduction, exercise, weight management, DASH diet.
AGT.fun_fact: The RAAS (renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system) is one of the most drugged pathways in medicine — ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and renin inhibitors all target it. AGT sits at the very top, feeding the entire cascade.

ABCG2.editorial_description: The gout gene and drug efflux pump. ABCG2 exports uric acid in the kidneys and gut. A common variant (Q141K) reduces urate excretion, raising uric acid levels — it's the strongest genetic risk factor for gout.
ABCG2.what_it_means: ABCG2 is a transporter that pumps urate and various drugs out of cells. The Q141K variant (rs2231142) reduces transport function by ~50%, causing uric acid to accumulate in blood.
ABCG2.carrier_note: Q141K is found in ~10% of Europeans and ~30% of East Asians. It's one of the main reasons gout prevalence differs between populations. Homozygotes have substantially elevated uric acid.
ABCG2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs2231142 indicates reduced urate transport capacity. Directly relevant for gout risk and potentially for drug clearance of ABCG2 substrates.
ABCG2.actionability: Carriers with elevated uric acid should discuss monitoring with their doctor. Hydration, limiting purine-rich foods, and avoiding fructose help. Also affects rosuvastatin and methotrexate pharmacokinetics.
ABCG2.fun_fact: ABCG2 was originally named 'breast cancer resistance protein' (BCRP) because it was discovered pumping chemotherapy drugs out of resistant breast cancer cells. Its role in gout via urate transport was only recognized later.

INS.editorial_description: The insulin gene itself. Variants in the INS promoter region are one of the strongest genetic risk factors for type 1 diabetes — they affect how much insulin is expressed in the thymus, determining immune tolerance.
INS.what_it_means: INS encodes preproinsulin. Beyond its metabolic role, insulin expression in the thymus is critical for teaching T cells NOT to attack beta cells. Low thymic INS expression = inadequate tolerance = autoimmune risk.
INS.carrier_note: The VNTR upstream of INS (Class I vs Class III) affects type 1 diabetes risk. Class I (~60-70% of Europeans) is associated with ~2x risk. This is about immune tolerance, not insulin production.
INS.nonref_interpretation: Variants near INS contribute to polygenic type 1 diabetes risk through thymic expression effects. Not related to type 2 diabetes insulin resistance.
INS.actionability: Context for type 1 diabetes family risk. No preventive intervention currently available for T1D, though immune-modulating trials (teplizumab) show promise for high-risk individuals.
INS.fun_fact: Insulin was the first protein ever sequenced (Frederick Sanger, 1951) and the first human protein produced by recombinant DNA (Genentech, 1978). The INS gene is tiny — just 1.4kb encoding a 110 amino acid preprotein.

PRKN.editorial_description: The mitochondrial garbage collector. Parkin (PRKN) tags damaged mitochondria for destruction. Mutations cause early-onset Parkinson's disease — revealing that mitochondrial quality control is central to neurodegeneration.
PRKN.what_it_means: Parkin is an E3 ubiquitin ligase that ubiquitinates damaged mitochondria (flagging them for autophagy/mitophagy). When Parkin is lost, damaged mitochondria accumulate, poisoning dopamine neurons.
PRKN.carrier_note: Biallelic PRKN mutations are the most common cause of autosomal recessive early-onset Parkinson's (before age 40). Carriers (one copy) may have very slightly elevated risk but usually remain healthy.
PRKN.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate carrier status for recessive Parkinson's. Single carriers are generally unaffected. Two pathogenic copies cause disease.
PRKN.actionability: Relevant for families with early-onset Parkinson's. Exercise strongly promotes mitophagy and is neuroprotective regardless of genotype.
PRKN.fun_fact: Parkin and PINK1 work as a team — PINK1 accumulates on damaged mitochondria and recruits Parkin to destroy them. This 'PINK1-Parkin mitophagy pathway' is now one of the hottest targets in neurodegeneration and aging research.

MET.editorial_description: The invasion receptor. MET drives cell migration, growth, and tissue repair through hepatocyte growth factor signaling. In cancer, MET amplification enables tumor cells to metastasize — and it's an emerging resistance mechanism in targeted therapy.
MET.what_it_means: MET is a receptor tyrosine kinase activated by HGF (hepatocyte growth factor). It drives the 'invasive growth' program — cells scatter, migrate, and proliferate. Essential in embryonic development and wound healing.
MET.carrier_note: Germline MET mutations are rare (hereditary papillary renal carcinoma). MET amplification in tumors is somatic. Common germline variants are benign polymorphisms.
MET.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants are generally benign. Cancer-relevant MET changes (amplification, exon 14 skipping) are somatic and require tumor testing.
MET.actionability: No germline action for most people. MET-amplified and MET exon 14-mutant cancers now have targeted drugs (capmatinib, tepotinib) — a growing niche in precision oncology.
MET.fun_fact: MET amplification is one of the main ways lung cancers become resistant to EGFR inhibitors — the tumor activates a parallel bypass pathway. This 'MET bypass' discovery showed that cancers evolve in real-time to escape targeted therapy.

SOD1.editorial_description: The ALS gene. SOD1 mutations cause ~20% of familial ALS (Lou Gehrig's disease). A new antisense drug (tofersen) can now silence mutant SOD1 — the first genetically-targeted ALS treatment and a landmark for gene-silencing therapy.
SOD1.what_it_means: SOD1 converts cytoplasmic superoxide to hydrogen peroxide. ALS-causing mutations don't simply lose function — they create a toxic gain-of-function protein that misfolds, aggregates, and poisons motor neurons.
SOD1.carrier_note: SOD1 mutations cause ~2% of all ALS (~20% of familial cases). Over 200 different mutations are known. Consumer arrays detect some known variants but clinical sequencing is more reliable.
SOD1.nonref_interpretation: Common SOD1 variants are benign polymorphisms. Known pathogenic mutations (e.g., A4V, D90A) are rare and typically found through family history.
SOD1.actionability: Families with ALS should consider SOD1 genetic testing. Tofersen (an antisense oligonucleotide) is now approved specifically for SOD1-ALS — making genetic diagnosis directly therapeutic.
SOD1.fun_fact: The Ice Bucket Challenge (2014) raised $220 million for ALS research. Some of that funding contributed to the tofersen trials — meaning viral social media literally helped produce a new drug. SOD1 was the first ALS gene discovered (1993).

FMR1.editorial_description: The Fragile X gene — the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability and autism. A CGG repeat expansion silences FMR1, depriving the brain of FMRP protein needed for synaptic development.
FMR1.what_it_means: FMRP (Fragile X Messenger Ribonucleoprotein) regulates translation of hundreds of synaptic proteins. Without it, synapses form excessively but immaturely — too many connections, poorly organized.
FMR1.carrier_note: ~1 in 250 women and 1 in 800 men carry a 'premutation' (55-200 CGG repeats). Full mutations (>200 repeats) cause Fragile X syndrome. Premutation carriers face risks of FXTAS (tremor/ataxia in men >50) and premature ovarian failure.
FMR1.nonref_interpretation: Consumer arrays cannot measure CGG repeat length (the key pathogenic variant). SNPs near FMR1 are mostly uninformative for Fragile X carrier status. Clinical testing uses specialized PCR.
FMR1.actionability: Carrier screening is recommended for women planning pregnancy (ACOG guidelines). Premutation carriers should be aware of FXTAS risk (men) and ovarian reserve concerns (women).
FMR1.fun_fact: FMR1 is silenced by methylation when CGG repeats exceed ~200 — the DNA literally shuts itself off. The 'fragile' site is visible under a microscope as a gap on the X chromosome, which is how it was originally discovered in 1991.

FOXP3.editorial_description: The tolerance gene. FOXP3 creates regulatory T cells (Tregs) that prevent your immune system from attacking your own body. Without it, total immune chaos — IPEX syndrome is one of the most dramatic autoimmune diseases known.
FOXP3.what_it_means: FOXP3 is the master transcription factor that defines Tregs — the immune cells that suppress other immune cells. Tregs patrol tissues preventing autoimmune attack and maintaining tolerance to self.
FOXP3.carrier_note: FOXP3 is on the X chromosome. Males with loss-of-function mutations develop IPEX syndrome (lethal multi-organ autoimmunity in infancy). Common variants subtly affect Treg function and autoimmune susceptibility.
FOXP3.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate subtle differences in Treg biology. Mostly research context — common variants don't cause IPEX.
FOXP3.actionability: Context for autoimmune risk. Treg function is supported by vitamin D, short-chain fatty acids (fiber), and adequate sleep. No specific action for common variants.
FOXP3.fun_fact: IPEX babies (no FOXP3) develop diabetes, eczema, and autoimmune enteritis within weeks of birth — proving that immune tolerance is an active process requiring dedicated suppressor cells, not just passive absence of reactivity.

CYP3A4.editorial_description: The liver's workhorse. CYP3A4 metabolizes approximately 50% of all prescription drugs. It processes everything from statins to immunosuppressants — and it's the reason grapefruit juice has so many drug interactions.
CYP3A4.what_it_means: CYP3A4 is the most abundant cytochrome P450 in liver and intestine. It has an unusually large active site, accepting hundreds of structurally diverse substrates — explaining its extraordinary breadth.
CYP3A4.carrier_note: Unlike CYP2D6, CYP3A4 has fewer common loss-of-function variants. Activity varies 10-40 fold between people, but this is driven more by induction/inhibition (drugs, food) than genetics in most populations.
CYP3A4.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants in CYP3A4 exist but most are rare or have modest effects. Environmental modulators (drugs, food, supplements) often matter more than genotype for CYP3A4 activity.
CYP3A4.actionability: Be aware of CYP3A4 interactions: grapefruit (inhibits), St. John's Wort (induces), and many drugs. Discuss interactions when starting new medications.
CYP3A4.fun_fact: A single glass of grapefruit juice can inhibit intestinal CYP3A4 for 72 hours, effectively doubling the dose of many drugs. This is why grapefruit warnings appear on so many medication labels — one enzyme, hundreds of drugs.

PARP1.editorial_description: The DNA repair sensor and synthetic lethality target. PARP1 detects single-strand breaks and recruits repair machinery. PARP inhibitors (olaparib) kill BRCA-mutant cancers by exploiting their existing DNA repair deficiency — a landmark in precision oncology.
PARP1.what_it_means: PARP1 binds DNA breaks and adds poly(ADP-ribose) chains to recruit repair proteins. When PARP is blocked in BRCA-deficient cells, single-strand breaks become lethal double-strand breaks with no backup repair — synthetic lethality.
PARP1.carrier_note: Germline PARP1 variants are common and mostly benign. The clinical relevance is in BRCA-mutant tumors where PARP inhibitors are therapeutic. Consumer arrays test germline DNA, not tumor BRCA status.
PARP1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants in PARP1 are generally common polymorphisms. Some may subtly affect DNA repair efficiency but are not actionable individually.
PARP1.actionability: No direct germline action. PARP inhibitors (olaparib, niraparib) are prescribed for BRCA-mutant cancers. PARP1 genotype itself doesn't determine eligibility.
PARP1.fun_fact: Synthetic lethality (two non-lethal defects combining to kill a cell) was theorized in 1946 but took 60 years to become a drug. PARP inhibitors for BRCA cancers are now a multi-billion dollar drug class and the proof-of-concept for exploiting cancer's own weaknesses.

MDM2.editorial_description: p53's nemesis. MDM2 destroys the tumor suppressor p53 — keeping it in check during normal life but becoming cancer's best friend when overexpressed. Drugs that block MDM2-p53 binding could reactivate p53 in millions of tumors.
MDM2.what_it_means: MDM2 ubiquitinates p53, targeting it for destruction. This keeps p53 levels low when there's no DNA damage. In cancers that amplify MDM2, p53 is effectively silenced without being mutated.
MDM2.carrier_note: The SNP309 variant (rs2279744) in the MDM2 promoter increases MDM2 expression and is found in ~40% of people. It's associated with modestly earlier cancer onset in some studies, particularly in women.
MDM2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs2279744 indicates higher MDM2 expression (T>G). May modestly attenuate p53 function, contributing to polygenic cancer risk.
MDM2.actionability: Mostly research context. MDM2 inhibitors (nutlins, idasanutlin) are in clinical trials to restore p53 function in cancers retaining wild-type p53.
MDM2.fun_fact: MDM2 stands for 'Mouse Double Minute 2' — named after tiny chromosome fragments seen in a mouse tumor cell line in 1987. It creates a precise feedback loop with p53: p53 activates MDM2 transcription, and MDM2 destroys p53 protein.

PIK3CA.editorial_description: The most commonly mutated oncogene in breast cancer. PIK3CA mutations activate the PI3K growth pathway in ~40% of ER+ breast cancers. Alpelisib specifically targets mutant PI3Kα — a precision therapy guided by tumor genotyping.
PIK3CA.what_it_means: PIK3CA encodes the catalytic subunit of PI3Kα, which generates PIP3 to activate AKT/mTOR growth signaling. Hotspot mutations (E545K, H1047R) make it constitutively active, driving proliferation and survival.
PIK3CA.carrier_note: PIK3CA mutations are somatic (tumor-only). They're found in 40% of ER+ breast cancer, 25% of colorectal cancer, and many others. Germline variants are mostly benign polymorphisms.
PIK3CA.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants in PIK3CA are common and benign. Cancer-relevant hotspot mutations are somatic and require tumor sequencing.
PIK3CA.actionability: No germline action. Tumor PIK3CA testing is standard for advanced ER+ breast cancer to determine alpelisib eligibility.
PIK3CA.fun_fact: PIK3CA mutations also cause a non-cancer condition — PIK3CA-Related Overgrowth Spectrum (PROS), where somatic mosaicism causes asymmetric limb or tissue overgrowth. The same mutations that drive cancer cause benign overgrowth when occurring early in embryonic development.

RET.editorial_description: The thyroid cancer gene. Germline RET mutations cause MEN2 (Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2) — one of the few hereditary cancer syndromes where prophylactic surgery in childhood is recommended based on genotype alone.
RET.what_it_means: RET is a receptor tyrosine kinase essential for kidney and nervous system development. Gain-of-function mutations cause medullary thyroid cancer and pheochromocytoma. Loss-of-function causes Hirschsprung's disease (aganglionic colon).
RET.carrier_note: MEN2 (germline RET mutations) affects ~1 in 30,000. The specific mutation determines cancer risk timing — some cause cancer in infancy (codon 918), others in adulthood. Genotype-phenotype correlation is unusually precise.
RET.nonref_interpretation: Common RET variants are benign polymorphisms. Known pathogenic mutations (codons 634, 918, etc.) are rare and identified through family history of medullary thyroid cancer or pheochromocytoma.
RET.actionability: MEN2 carriers undergo prophylactic thyroidectomy — timing based on specific mutation (some in infancy, some by age 5, some by 20). One of the clearest genotype-to-surgery decisions in medicine.
RET.fun_fact: RET stands for 'Rearranged during Transfection' — named for how it was discovered in 1985. It's remarkable for having opposing roles: gain-of-function causes cancer (MEN2) while loss-of-function causes a developmental disorder (Hirschsprung's). Same gene, opposite problems.

TARDBP.editorial_description: TDP-43 — the protein that misbehaves in almost all ALS and half of frontotemporal dementia. It normally manages RNA in the nucleus, but in disease it clumps in the cytoplasm, strangling motor neurons.
TARDBP.what_it_means: TDP-43 binds and regulates thousands of RNA targets. In ALS/FTD, it mislocalizes from nucleus to cytoplasm, forming insoluble aggregates. This simultaneously depletes its normal function AND creates toxic clumps.
TARDBP.carrier_note: Germline TARDBP mutations cause ~3-5% of familial ALS. But TDP-43 pathology is found in ~97% of ALL ALS cases (including sporadic) — making it the unifying pathological feature of the disease.
TARDBP.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may include rare pathogenic mutations. Common variants are benign. Clinical significance depends on the specific variant and family history.
TARDBP.actionability: Families with ALS/FTD should consider TARDBP testing as part of genetic panels. No targeted therapy yet, but understanding the mutation guides prognosis and clinical trial eligibility.
TARDBP.fun_fact: TDP-43 was originally studied as a protein that binds HIV TAR DNA (hence TAR DNA-Binding Protein, 43kDa). Its role in ALS was only discovered in 2006 — instantly making it the central molecule in motor neuron disease research.

CFH.editorial_description: The macular degeneration gene. CFH variants are the strongest genetic risk factor for age-related macular degeneration (AMD) — the leading cause of blindness in elderly populations. They alter complement system regulation in the retina.
CFH.what_it_means: Complement Factor H regulates the alternative complement pathway, preventing it from attacking your own tissues. The Y402H variant (rs1061170) reduces CFH binding to retinal surfaces, allowing complement-mediated damage.
CFH.carrier_note: The risk allele (His402) is carried by ~35% of Europeans. Homozygotes have 5-7x increased AMD risk. Combined with smoking, the risk is dramatically higher.
CFH.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs1061170 indicates the AMD risk allele (Y402H). One of the strongest common variant-disease associations in all of genetics.
CFH.actionability: Risk carriers should not smoke (strongest modifiable risk factor), eat leafy greens (lutein/zeaxanthin), and get regular eye exams after age 50. Anti-VEGF injections treat wet AMD.
CFH.fun_fact: CFH was one of the first GWAS successes (2005) — discovered simultaneously by three groups using only 96 cases and 50 controls. The effect size was so large that tiny sample sizes worked, which is almost unheard of in complex disease genetics.

HMOX1.editorial_description: The carbon monoxide gene. Heme oxygenase-1 breaks down heme into biliverdin, iron, and CO (yes, the 'toxic' gas). Turns out CO at low levels is anti-inflammatory and cytoprotective — your body makes its own carbon monoxide on purpose.
HMOX1.what_it_means: HMOX1 is a stress-inducible enzyme that degrades free heme (which is toxic) into biliverdin (antioxidant), iron (recycled), and CO (signaling molecule). It's powerfully anti-inflammatory and cytoprotective.
HMOX1.carrier_note: A (GT)n repeat polymorphism in the HMOX1 promoter affects inducibility. Short repeats (high expression) are generally protective; long repeats (low expression) associated with cardiovascular and inflammatory disease.
HMOX1.nonref_interpretation: The key variant is a repeat length polymorphism that arrays may not capture well. SNP-based variants in the region provide partial proxy information.
HMOX1.actionability: HMOX1 is induced by exercise, curcumin, sulforaphane, and heme-containing foods. It's part of the broader cellular stress response alongside NRF2.
HMOX1.fun_fact: Your body produces ~500 mL of carbon monoxide daily via HMOX1. Far from being purely toxic, endogenous CO acts as a signaling molecule like nitric oxide. CO-releasing molecules are being developed as anti-inflammatory drugs.

APOA1.editorial_description: The 'good cholesterol' protein. ApoA-I is the structural backbone of HDL particles and drives reverse cholesterol transport — pulling cholesterol out of artery walls back to the liver. A rare variant (ApoA-I Milano) is being developed as a drug.
APOA1.what_it_means: ApoA-I wraps HDL particles and activates LCAT (cholesterol esterification) and ABCA1 (cholesterol efflux from macrophages). It's the functional reason HDL is cardioprotective.
APOA1.carrier_note: Common APOA1 promoter variants modestly affect HDL levels. Rare loss-of-function mutations cause very low HDL. The relationship between genetic HDL levels and heart disease is more nuanced than once thought.
APOA1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered ApoA-I expression and HDL levels. Polygenic context matters — genetically high HDL doesn't always mean reduced cardiovascular risk.
APOA1.actionability: HDL function matters more than HDL level. Exercise is the strongest HDL-raising intervention. Moderate alcohol raises HDL but risks outweigh benefits for most people.
APOA1.fun_fact: A village in Limone sul Garda, Italy carries ApoA-I Milano — a variant that makes their HDL extraordinarily effective at clearing cholesterol despite low HDL levels. Synthetic ApoA-I Milano infusions are being trialed as an atherosclerosis treatment.

VWF.editorial_description: Von Willebrand Factor — the most common inherited bleeding disorder. VWF acts as molecular glue between platelets and damaged blood vessel walls. Deficiency causes easy bruising, heavy periods, and prolonged bleeding after surgery.
VWF.what_it_means: VWF is a massive glycoprotein that tethers platelets to sites of vascular injury and carries Factor VIII in blood. It's produced by endothelial cells and megakaryocytes. Deficiency disrupts primary hemostasis.
VWF.carrier_note: Von Willebrand disease (VWD) affects ~1% of the population (most are Type 1 with mild symptoms). Many are undiagnosed. It's far more common than hemophilia and affects all sexes equally.
VWF.nonref_interpretation: Common VWF variants contribute to normal variation in VWF levels and blood type affects VWF (type O has ~25% lower levels). True VWD-causing mutations require clinical coagulation testing.
VWF.actionability: If you bruise easily, have heavy menstrual bleeding, or bleed prolonged after dental work — ask about VWD testing. It's treatable with desmopressin (DDAVP) or VWF concentrates.
VWF.fun_fact: VWF is one of the largest proteins in your body — its multimers can be over 20 million Daltons. Blood type O people have 25% lower VWF levels, which partly explains why type O is mildly protective against thrombosis.

NOD2.editorial_description: The Crohn's disease gene. NOD2 was the first gene identified for inflammatory bowel disease. It detects bacterial cell wall fragments inside cells — when mutated, gut bacteria trigger inappropriate inflammation.
NOD2.what_it_means: NOD2 is an intracellular pattern recognition receptor that detects muramyl dipeptide (from bacterial peptidoglycan). It activates NF-κB to mount an antimicrobial response. Mutations paradoxically cause BOTH reduced bacterial sensing AND chronic inflammation.
NOD2.carrier_note: Three common NOD2 variants (R702W, G908R, 1007fs) each increase Crohn's risk ~2-4x (one copy) or ~20-40x (two copies). About 15% of Europeans carry at least one risk allele.
NOD2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs2066844, rs2066845, or rs2066847 indicates NOD2 risk variants. Compound heterozygosity (two different variants) confers high Crohn's risk.
NOD2.actionability: Context for IBD risk. Carriers with GI symptoms should have a low threshold for Crohn's evaluation. Gut microbiome diversity (fiber, fermented foods) may be particularly beneficial.
NOD2.fun_fact: NOD2's link to Crohn's was published in 2001 by two independent groups in the same issue of Nature. The paradox is still debated: how does REDUCED bacterial sensing cause INCREASED inflammation? One theory: inadequate initial response leads to bacterial accumulation and secondary overreaction.

NPPB.editorial_description: Better known as BNP (B-type Natriuretic Peptide) — the heart failure biomarker gene. BNP is released when heart walls stretch. The BNP blood test is one of the most ordered in emergency medicine — it tells doctors whether breathlessness is cardiac or pulmonary.
NPPB.what_it_means: BNP is secreted by cardiac ventricles under volume/pressure stress. It causes vasodilation, natriuresis (salt excretion), and suppresses RAAS — the heart's emergency pressure-relief valve.
NPPB.carrier_note: Common NPPB variants affect baseline BNP levels and may confound the diagnostic blood test. Some people have genetically higher or lower BNP independent of heart function.
NPPB.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate genetically altered BNP levels. Useful context if your BNP blood tests are unexpectedly high or low relative to cardiac function.
NPPB.actionability: Context for interpreting BNP blood tests. If BNP seems discordant with your clinical picture, genetic variation may explain it. No direct action needed for common variants.
NPPB.fun_fact: BNP was discovered in pig brain ('B' originally stood for 'Brain') in 1988, but it's actually produced primarily by the heart. Its synthetic form (nesiritide) was briefly used as a heart failure drug, though that application has fallen out of favor.

GSTP1.editorial_description: A detox enzyme and cancer biomarker in one. GSTP1 neutralizes carcinogens and reactive metabolites. Its silencing by methylation in prostate tissue is one of the earliest and most consistent molecular changes in prostate cancer.
GSTP1.what_it_means: GSTP1 conjugates glutathione to electrophilic toxins, rendering them harmless. The Ile105Val variant (rs1695) alters substrate specificity and enzyme activity, affecting carcinogen detoxification.
GSTP1.carrier_note: The Val105 allele is carried by ~30-40% of Europeans. It has reduced activity toward some carcinogens (PAHs, tobacco metabolites) but increased activity toward others. Context-dependent.
GSTP1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs1695 indicates Val105 (altered detox profile). Neither allele is simply protective or harmful — depends on which exposures dominate.
GSTP1.actionability: Val carriers may benefit from reducing exposure to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (charred food, tobacco smoke). General detox support: cruciferous vegetables, adequate glutathione precursors.
GSTP1.fun_fact: GSTP1 promoter methylation is found in >90% of prostate cancers but almost never in normal prostate tissue. It's being developed as a liquid biopsy biomarker — detecting prostate cancer from a urine sample via methylated GSTP1 DNA.

MMP9.editorial_description: The tissue demolition enzyme. MMP-9 dissolves extracellular matrix — essential for wound healing and immune cell migration, but destructive when chronically elevated in arthritis, atherosclerosis, and tumor invasion.
MMP9.what_it_means: MMP-9 (gelatinase B) cleaves type IV collagen and other matrix components. It's released by neutrophils and macrophages during inflammation, enabling tissue remodeling but also enabling metastatic cancer spread.
MMP9.carrier_note: The -1562 C>T promoter variant (rs3918242) increases MMP-9 expression and is found in ~15-20% of Europeans. The T allele is associated with higher cardiovascular disease and aneurysm risk.
MMP9.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at the promoter may indicate higher MMP-9 expression — more tissue remodeling capacity but potentially more destructive inflammation.
MMP9.actionability: MMP-9 is reduced by omega-3 fatty acids, doxycycline (at sub-antibiotic doses), and anti-inflammatory lifestyle. Context for understanding atherosclerotic plaque instability.
MMP9.fun_fact: MMP-9 is what allows neutrophils to squeeze through basement membranes to reach infection sites. Without it, immune cells would be trapped behind tissue barriers. Cancer cells hijack this same machinery to invade and metastasize.

STAT3.editorial_description: The signal relay for immunity and cancer. STAT3 transmits signals from cytokine receptors directly to gene activation in the nucleus. It's constitutively active in most cancers and is a major target for next-generation cancer drugs.
STAT3.what_it_means: STAT3 is phosphorylated by JAKs at the cell membrane, dimerizes, and translocates to the nucleus to activate target genes. It drives cell survival, proliferation, and immune suppression in the tumor microenvironment.
STAT3.carrier_note: Germline gain-of-function STAT3 mutations cause autoimmune diseases (rare). Loss-of-function causes Hyper-IgE syndrome. Common variants are mostly benign polymorphisms.
STAT3.nonref_interpretation: Common non-reference variants in STAT3 are generally benign regulatory polymorphisms without clinical significance for most people.
STAT3.actionability: No direct action for common variants. STAT3 inhibitors are in clinical development for cancers with constitutive STAT3 activation.
STAT3.fun_fact: STAT3 was called 'undruggable' for years because transcription factors lack traditional drug pockets. New approaches (PROTACs, antisense, allosteric inhibitors) are finally making progress against this high-value target.

AKT1.editorial_description: The survival kinase. AKT1 sits at the crossroads of growth factor signaling, telling cells to grow, metabolize, and resist death. Overactive AKT drives many cancers; it's downstream of PI3K and upstream of mTOR.
AKT1.what_it_means: AKT (also called Protein Kinase B) is activated by PI3K and phosphorylates dozens of substrates controlling glucose uptake, protein synthesis (via mTOR), and apoptosis inhibition. It's the central node in growth signaling.
AKT1.carrier_note: Somatic AKT1 mutations (e.g., E17K) are found in breast, ovarian, and colorectal cancers. Germline variants are mostly benign. Rare germline AKT1 variants cause Proteus syndrome (asymmetric overgrowth).
AKT1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants are generally common polymorphisms. Cancer-relevant AKT1 mutations are somatic.
AKT1.actionability: No germline action. AKT is inhibited by the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway inhibitors used in cancer treatment. Fasting and exercise naturally suppress this pathway.
AKT1.fun_fact: AKT is named after a mouse strain (Ak) that developed thymomas (T) — hence AKT. The PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway is essentially a nutrient-to-growth translator: when food is abundant, it pushes cells to grow; when scarce, cells conserve.

ABL1.editorial_description: Better known as the Gleevec gene (c-Abl). The BCR-ABL fusion (Philadelphia chromosome) drives chronic myeloid leukemia. Imatinib (Gleevec) targeting BCR-ABL transformed CML from fatal to manageable — the founding triumph of precision medicine.
ABL1.what_it_means: Normal ABL1 is a tightly regulated tyrosine kinase. The t(9;22) translocation fuses it to BCR, creating a constitutively active kinase that drives white blood cell proliferation in CML.
ABL1.carrier_note: The Philadelphia chromosome is a somatic event (not inherited). Germline ABL1 variants are benign polymorphisms. Consumer arrays test germline DNA and cannot detect BCR-ABL.
ABL1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants are benign. BCR-ABL detection requires specific molecular testing (FISH or PCR) on blood/bone marrow.
ABL1.actionability: No germline action. BCR-ABL testing diagnoses CML. Imatinib and newer TKIs (dasatinib, nilotinib) give CML patients near-normal life expectancy.
ABL1.fun_fact: Before Gleevec (2001), CML was a death sentence within 3-5 years. After Gleevec, 5-year survival exceeded 90%. Brian Druker's development of imatinib is considered one of the greatest achievements in cancer research history.

CCND1.editorial_description: The cell cycle accelerator. Cyclin D1 partners with CDK4/6 to push cells past the restriction point into division. It's amplified in many cancers — and CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib) blocking this partnership transformed breast cancer treatment.
CCND1.what_it_means: Cyclin D1 accumulates in response to growth signals and activates CDK4/6, which phosphorylates RB and releases E2F to drive S-phase entry. Amplification overrides normal growth controls.
CCND1.carrier_note: CCND1 amplification is somatic (in tumors). The A870G polymorphism (rs9344) affects mRNA splicing and is found in ~45% of people. The AA genotype is modestly associated with various cancer risks.
CCND1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs9344 indicates an alternative splice form with subtly different protein stability. Contributes modestly to polygenic cancer risk.
CCND1.actionability: No direct action for common variants. CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib) are standard-of-care for ER+ metastatic breast cancer, blocking the cyclin D1-CDK4/6 complex.
CCND1.fun_fact: CDK4/6 inhibitors were the biggest advance in breast cancer in a decade, extending progression-free survival by 10+ months. They work by exploiting cancer's addiction to cyclin D1-driven proliferation — blocking the very accelerator that makes cancer cells divide.

EDN1.editorial_description: Endothelin-1 — the most potent vasoconstrictor your body makes. EDN1 constricts blood vessels, promotes fibrosis, and was implicated in pulmonary arterial hypertension. Endothelin receptor blockers (bosentan) treat this otherwise fatal condition.
EDN1.what_it_means: ET-1 binds receptors on smooth muscle cells causing prolonged vasoconstriction, cell proliferation, and fibrosis. It opposes nitric oxide. Chronic ET-1 excess drives vascular remodeling and hypertension.
EDN1.carrier_note: Common EDN1 variants (e.g., Lys198Asn, rs5370) are found in ~20-30% of people and modestly affect ET-1 levels and blood pressure. Effects are small individually.
EDN1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered ET-1 production or clearance. Context for understanding vascular tone and blood pressure genetics.
EDN1.actionability: Context for cardiovascular risk. ET-1 is elevated by salt, stress, and sleep apnea. Reduced by exercise and adequate nitric oxide production.
EDN1.fun_fact: Endothelin was discovered in 1988 by Masashi Yanagisawa while studying endothelial cells. It's 100x more potent as a vasoconstrictor than norepinephrine. The endothelin system evolved partly from snake venom peptides — biology repurposed a weapon as a blood pressure regulator.

NR3C1.editorial_description: The stress hormone receptor. The glucocorticoid receptor determines how your cells respond to cortisol — affecting stress resilience, immune suppression, metabolism, and even how well steroid medications work for you.
NR3C1.what_it_means: NR3C1 is the nuclear receptor for cortisol. When bound, it translocates to the nucleus and activates anti-inflammatory genes while suppressing immune activation. It also regulates glucose metabolism and stress responses.
NR3C1.carrier_note: The BclI polymorphism (rs41423247) and N363S (rs6195) variant affect glucocorticoid sensitivity. Higher sensitivity variants (~25-35% of people) are associated with slightly higher cortisol responsiveness, visceral fat, and metabolic syndrome risk.
NR3C1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered glucocorticoid sensitivity — potentially more responsive to cortisol (and exogenous steroids like prednisone).
NR3C1.actionability: Context for understanding stress responses and steroid medication sensitivity. High-sensitivity carriers may need lower doses of corticosteroid medications and may benefit especially from stress management.
NR3C1.fun_fact: Epigenetic modification of NR3C1 is one of the most replicated findings in childhood adversity research — early life stress methylates the promoter, permanently altering cortisol responsiveness. It's a molecular scar of early adversity that persists into adulthood.

SQSTM1.editorial_description: The autophagy cargo receptor. p62/SQSTM1 tags damaged proteins and organelles for destruction. Mutations cause Paget's disease of bone and are linked to ALS — revealing that cellular garbage collection is essential for both bone and brain health.
SQSTM1.what_it_means: p62 binds ubiquitinated cargo and delivers it to autophagosomes for degradation. It's also a signaling hub connecting autophagy, NRF2 activation, and NF-κB signaling. When p62 accumulates, it indicates autophagy failure.
SQSTM1.carrier_note: The P392L variant (rs104893941) causes Paget's disease of bone in ~25-50% of carriers (autosomal dominant, reduced penetrance). Other SQSTM1 variants are linked to familial ALS and frontotemporal dementia.
SQSTM1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants in SQSTM1 may be clinically significant depending on the specific variant. P392L should prompt Paget's awareness. Other variants need individual assessment.
SQSTM1.actionability: P392L carriers should be aware of Paget's disease symptoms (bone pain, deformity, elevated alkaline phosphatase). Autophagy is enhanced by fasting, exercise, and spermidine.
SQSTM1.fun_fact: p62 bodies (visible clumps of accumulated p62) are a hallmark of failed autophagy and appear in aging cells, neurodegeneration, and liver disease. Seeing p62 accumulate under a microscope literally means the cell's garbage collection has broken down.

ICAM1.editorial_description: The immune cell landing pad. ICAM-1 on blood vessel walls grabs passing immune cells and pulls them through into tissues. It's elevated in atherosclerosis, diabetes, and inflammation — and it's the rhinovirus (common cold) receptor.
ICAM1.what_it_means: ICAM-1 is an adhesion molecule on endothelial cells that binds LFA-1 on leukocytes, enabling their extravasation into tissues. It's upregulated by inflammatory cytokines (TNF, IL-1) and serves as a measure of endothelial activation.
ICAM1.carrier_note: The K469E variant (rs5498) is found in ~40-55% of Europeans. The E allele is associated with higher soluble ICAM-1 levels and modestly increased cardiovascular risk in some studies.
ICAM1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs5498 may indicate altered ICAM-1 shedding and endothelial activation. Context for cardiovascular inflammation.
ICAM1.actionability: Soluble ICAM-1 is reduced by exercise, statins, and anti-inflammatory diet. It's a marker of endothelial health that responds to lifestyle changes.
ICAM1.fun_fact: Over 90% of common cold viruses (rhinovirus major group) enter cells by binding ICAM-1. This means your immune system's own traffic signal is the doorway for the most frequent human infection. Blocking ICAM-1 prevents colds in lab settings but would also cripple immunity.

XRCC1.editorial_description: The DNA repair scaffold. XRCC1 coordinates base excision repair — fixing the thousands of small DNA lesions that occur daily from oxidation, alkylation, and spontaneous base loss. Variants affect cancer risk from environmental exposures.
XRCC1.what_it_means: XRCC1 doesn't repair DNA itself — it's a scaffold protein that recruits and coordinates PARP1, DNA ligase III, and polymerase beta at sites of base damage. Without it, repair is slow and error-prone.
XRCC1.carrier_note: Three common variants (R194W, R280H, R399Q) affect XRCC1 function. R399Q (rs25487) is carried by ~25-35% of Europeans and associated with modestly increased cancer risk, particularly in smokers.
XRCC1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs25487 (Gln399) indicates reduced base excision repair efficiency. Most relevant in context of carcinogen exposure (smoking, radiation).
XRCC1.actionability: Carriers of reduced-function variants may particularly benefit from minimizing carcinogen exposure (not smoking, reducing processed meat, sun protection). Antioxidant-rich diet supports DNA repair.
XRCC1.fun_fact: Your DNA sustains ~10,000-100,000 lesions per cell per day from normal metabolism alone. XRCC1-coordinated repair fixes most of them before you finish reading this sentence. Without this constant maintenance, cancer would be inevitable within days.

MUC1.editorial_description: The mucin barrier gene. MUC1 coats epithelial surfaces in a protective sugar layer. Variants cause medullary cystic kidney disease, and MUC1 overexpression on tumors makes it one of the oldest known tumor antigens.
MUC1.what_it_means: MUC1 is a transmembrane mucin that extends far above the cell surface, creating a physical and chemical barrier. Its heavily glycosylated extracellular domain protects epithelia from pathogens and physical stress.
MUC1.carrier_note: A VNTR (variable number tandem repeat) in MUC1 causes autosomal dominant medullary cystic kidney disease. This is challenging to detect by standard sequencing due to the repetitive nature of the region.
MUC1.nonref_interpretation: Common SNPs in MUC1 are generally benign. The pathogenic VNTR mutations require specialized long-read sequencing or specific assays.
MUC1.actionability: Families with unexplained kidney disease (especially with gout and reduced kidney function) should consider MUC1 VNTR testing. Standard arrays may miss the causative variant.
MUC1.fun_fact: MUC1 was one of the first tumor-associated antigens ever discovered (CA 15-3 blood test). In cancer, it's stripped of its normal sugar coating, exposing abnormal epitopes. This makes it a target for cancer vaccines still in clinical trials decades later.

CDKN1A.editorial_description: Better known as p21 — the cell cycle stop sign. When p53 detects DNA damage, it activates p21 to halt cell division for repairs. p21 is also a key mediator of cellular senescence — the permanent growth arrest of damaged cells that accumulates with aging.
CDKN1A.what_it_means: p21 (CDKN1A) inhibits CDK2 and CDK4, blocking cell cycle progression at G1. It's directly activated by p53 after DNA damage, giving cells time to repair. Persistent p21 expression drives senescence.
CDKN1A.carrier_note: Common CDKN1A variants (e.g., C31A codon 31, rs1801270) modestly affect p21 function and are found in ~15-25% of people. Effects on cancer risk are small and inconsistent across studies.
CDKN1A.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may subtly alter p21 activity or expression. Part of the polygenic landscape affecting cell cycle control and potentially cancer risk.
CDKN1A.actionability: No direct action. p21 and senescence are active research areas in aging — senolytic drugs that clear p21-high senescent cells are in clinical trials.
CDKN1A.fun_fact: p21 was discovered simultaneously by two groups in 1993: as a p53 target (WAF1) and as a CDK inhibitor (CIP1). Its dual identity — protector against cancer but also driver of aging via senescence — perfectly embodies the antagonistic pleiotropy theory of aging.

IL17A.editorial_description: The psoriasis cytokine. IL-17A drives neutrophilic inflammation at body surfaces. Blocking it with secukinumab achieved near-complete psoriasis clearance — one of the most dramatic treatment responses in dermatology history.
IL17A.what_it_means: IL-17A is produced by Th17 cells and recruits neutrophils to barrier tissues (skin, gut, lungs). It's essential for fungal defense but drives pathology in psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and ankylosing spondylitis.
IL17A.carrier_note: Common IL17A variants modestly affect expression levels. The clinical revolution came from drug targeting, not from genetics — anti-IL-17 therapy works regardless of IL17A genotype.
IL17A.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may modestly alter IL-17A expression. Context for understanding inflammatory skin and joint disease susceptibility.
IL17A.actionability: Context for autoimmune/inflammatory conditions. Anti-IL-17 drugs (secukinumab, ixekizumab) are prescribed based on disease activity, not genotype.
IL17A.fun_fact: Anti-IL-17 therapy achieves PASI 90 (90% skin clearance) in ~70% of psoriasis patients. Before biologics, psoriasis severely impacted quality of life with few effective treatments. The IL-17 pathway discovery transformed dermatology.

CTNNB1.editorial_description: Beta-catenin — dual-purpose molecule serving as both structural glue between cells and the nuclear effector of Wnt signaling. When Wnt is activated (or APC is lost), beta-catenin floods the nucleus and drives cell proliferation.
CTNNB1.what_it_means: Beta-catenin connects cadherins to the cytoskeleton (structural role) and activates TCF/LEF transcription factors when Wnt signals accumulate it in the nucleus (signaling role). This duality makes it central to both adhesion and growth.
CTNNB1.carrier_note: Somatic CTNNB1 mutations (stabilizing mutations) are found in hepatocellular carcinoma, endometrial cancer, and desmoid tumors. Germline variants are mostly benign — the gene is too essential for major inherited variation.
CTNNB1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants are generally benign polymorphisms. Cancer-relevant stabilizing mutations are somatic.
CTNNB1.actionability: No germline action. Wnt/beta-catenin pathway inhibitors are in development for cancers driven by this pathway.
CTNNB1.fun_fact: Beta-catenin is the executioner of APC loss in colon cancer. When APC can't degrade it, beta-catenin accumulates and activates proliferation genes. This APC-beta-catenin axis is disrupted in ~90% of all colorectal cancers.

HLA-A.editorial_description: Your cellular ID badge. HLA-A displays protein fragments from inside every nucleated cell, allowing CD8+ T cells to inspect for viruses or mutations. It's the most important molecule for organ transplant matching and a key drug hypersensitivity determinant.
HLA-A.what_it_means: HLA-A presents 8-10 amino acid peptides on the cell surface. Cytotoxic T cells check these displays for foreign (viral) or abnormal (cancer) peptides. Every person carries two HLA-A alleles displaying different peptide repertoires.
HLA-A.carrier_note: Over 6,000 HLA-A alleles exist. Specific alleles determine drug hypersensitivities (HLA-A*31:01 and carbamazepine) and HIV control (HLA-A*02, B*57:01). Consumer array HLA typing is imprecise.
HLA-A.nonref_interpretation: HLA-A genotyping from consumer arrays is unreliable due to extreme polymorphism. Clinical-grade HLA typing uses sequence-based methods for accurate allele determination.
HLA-A.actionability: Relevant for transplant matching and drug hypersensitivity testing. HLA-A*31:01 testing is recommended before carbamazepine in some populations. Clinical HLA typing required for accuracy.
HLA-A.fun_fact: HLA-A*02:01 is the most common HLA-A allele in Europeans (~25-50% frequency). Most cancer immunotherapy neoantigen research focuses on this allele because of its high population frequency — creating an unintentional bias in precision medicine.

CXCR4.editorial_description: The stem cell homing beacon. CXCR4 guides stem cells to bone marrow and helps immune cells find infection sites. It's also an HIV co-receptor (alongside CCR5) and is exploited by metastatic cancer cells to home to specific organs.
CXCR4.what_it_means: CXCR4 is a chemokine receptor that senses CXCL12 gradients. Stem cells follow CXCL12 to bone marrow niches; immune cells follow it to inflammation sites. Cancer cells use the same mechanism to metastasize to CXCL12-rich organs (bone, liver, lung).
CXCR4.carrier_note: Germline CXCR4 gain-of-function mutations cause WHIM syndrome (warts, hypogammaglobulinemia, infections, myelokathexis) — very rare. Common variants have subtle effects on immune cell trafficking.
CXCR4.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are mostly benign. CXCR4 is too essential for major germline variation — it's critical for development (knockouts are embryonic lethal).
CXCR4.actionability: No action for common variants. CXCR4 antagonists (plerixafor) are used to mobilize stem cells from bone marrow for transplant collection.
CXCR4.fun_fact: CXCR4 was originally named 'fusin' because it helps HIV fuse with T cells. X4-tropic HIV strains use CXCR4 instead of CCR5 for entry — which is why CCR5-delta32 carriers aren't fully immune to all HIV strains.

FAS.editorial_description: The death receptor. FAS triggers apoptosis when bound by its ligand, allowing the immune system to kill infected or dangerous cells. Mutations cause autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome — where immune cells refuse to die.
FAS.what_it_means: FAS (CD95) is a death receptor on cell surfaces. When FAS ligand binds, it assembles the death-inducing signaling complex (DISC), activating caspase-8 and executing apoptosis. It's critical for immune cell homeostasis.
FAS.carrier_note: Germline FAS mutations cause ALPS (autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome) — lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and autoimmune cytopenias. Common variants are linked to modest differences in apoptotic sensitivity.
FAS.nonref_interpretation: Common non-reference variants may subtly affect FAS expression or signaling efficiency. Most are benign and contribute to normal immune regulation variation.
FAS.actionability: Families with unexplained lymphadenopathy, autoimmune cytopenias, or lymphoma clustering should consider FAS/ALPS testing.
FAS.fun_fact: After an immune response resolves, FAS kills the expanded T cells that are no longer needed — 'activation-induced cell death.' Without this cleanup, you'd accumulate ever-growing armies of T cells from every infection you've ever fought.

CD44.editorial_description: The stem cell and metastasis marker. CD44 helps cells grip hyaluronic acid in the extracellular matrix. Cancer stem cells express high CD44, and it's used to identify the most therapy-resistant, metastasis-capable tumor cells.
CD44.what_it_means: CD44 is a glycoprotein receptor for hyaluronic acid that mediates cell-matrix adhesion, migration, and signaling. Alternative splicing generates many isoforms with different roles in inflammation, wound healing, and cancer.
CD44.carrier_note: Common CD44 variants have minimal known clinical significance for individual health. The gene is more studied for its role in tumor biology and as a surface marker for identifying cancer stem cells.
CD44.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally common polymorphisms without well-established clinical effects. CD44 biology is primarily relevant in tumor context.
CD44.actionability: No direct action for common variants. CD44 is used as a research biomarker for cancer stem cells and aggressive tumor phenotypes.
CD44.fun_fact: CD44+/CD24- is the signature of breast cancer stem cells — the tiny fraction of tumor cells that resist chemotherapy and seed relapses. These cells may be as rare as 1 in 10,000 tumor cells but drive the entire disease.

EZH2.editorial_description: The gene silencer. EZH2 adds methyl marks to histones, compacting DNA and permanently silencing genes. In lymphoma, gain-of-function mutations silence tumor suppressors. EZH2 inhibitors (tazemetostat) are the first epigenetic cancer drugs.
EZH2.what_it_means: EZH2 is the catalytic subunit of PRC2, which trimethylates histone H3K27 to silence gene expression. This is essential for development (silencing lineage-inappropriate genes) but becomes oncogenic when it silences tumor suppressors.
EZH2.carrier_note: EZH2 gain-of-function mutations are somatic (found in ~20% of follicular lymphoma and DLBCL). Loss-of-function germline mutations cause Weaver syndrome (overgrowth). Common variants are benign.
EZH2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants are generally benign polymorphisms. Cancer-relevant activating mutations are somatic and require tumor sequencing.
EZH2.actionability: No germline action. Tazemetostat (EZH2 inhibitor) is approved for EZH2-mutant follicular lymphoma — the first drug targeting a histone modification enzyme.
EZH2.fun_fact: EZH2 means 'Enhancer of Zeste Homolog 2' — named after a Drosophila gene ('zeste' meaning 'lemon-colored' in Italian, describing a fly eye color mutant). From fly eye color to cancer drug target in one gene.

CYP1A1.editorial_description: The carcinogen activator. CYP1A1 converts polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (from smoke, grilled food, pollution) into reactive metabolites that damage DNA. Paradoxically, the enzyme meant to detoxify actually creates carcinogens.
CYP1A1.what_it_means: CYP1A1 is induced by AhR (aryl hydrocarbon receptor) when PAHs are detected. It oxidizes them for excretion — but the intermediate metabolites can bind DNA and cause mutations. More CYP1A1 can mean more carcinogen activation.
CYP1A1.carrier_note: The MspI variant (rs4646903) and Ile462Val (rs1048943) affect expression and activity. High-activity variants combined with smoking exposure may increase lung cancer risk, though studies show mixed results.
CYP1A1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate higher CYP1A1 inducibility. Most relevant in context of carcinogen exposure (smoking, occupational PAH exposure).
CYP1A1.actionability: Most relevant for smokers and those with occupational PAH exposure. Not smoking eliminates the main substrate. Cruciferous vegetables modulate AhR/CYP1A1 activity.
CYP1A1.fun_fact: CYP1A1 is why cigarette smoke causes cancer but clean air doesn't. The enzyme is barely expressed until PAHs arrive and activate the AhR receptor. It's a perfect example of gene-environment interaction: the genotype only matters if you have the exposure.
