RUNX1.editorial_description: The master blood transcription factor. RUNX1 controls the birth of all blood cells from hematopoietic stem cells. Its disruption is one of the most common events in leukemia — especially through the t(8;21) translocation in AML.
RUNX1.what_it_means: RUNX1 is essential for definitive hematopoiesis — without it, no blood stem cells form. It activates genes needed for blood cell maturation. Translocations create RUNX1 fusion proteins that block differentiation, trapping cells as immortal blasts.
RUNX1.carrier_note: Germline RUNX1 mutations cause familial platelet disorder with predisposition to AML (FPD-AML) — rare but high penetrance. Common variants are benign.
RUNX1.nonref_interpretation: Common non-reference variants are benign. Pathogenic mutations are rare and identified through family history of thrombocytopenia progressing to leukemia.
RUNX1.actionability: Families with inherited thrombocytopenia and/or multiple AML cases should consider RUNX1 testing. Carriers need hematologic surveillance for MDS/AML transformation.
RUNX1.fun_fact: The t(8;21) translocation (creating RUNX1-ETO fusion) was described in 1973 as one of the first recurrent chromosomal abnormalities in cancer. It causes ~10% of AML and paradoxically has a relatively good prognosis.

IL2.editorial_description: The T cell growth factor. IL-2 drives T cell proliferation and was the first cancer immunotherapy ever (approved 1992 for melanoma). High-dose IL-2 caused complete, durable remissions in some melanoma patients — but with brutal side effects.
IL2.what_it_means: IL-2 is produced by activated T cells and drives their own expansion (autocrine loop). It also expands regulatory T cells (Tregs) and NK cells. This dual role — activating effector AND suppressor cells — complicates its therapeutic use.
IL2.carrier_note: Common IL2 variants modestly affect expression levels. Their clinical impact on individual health is minimal. IL-2 biology matters more for immunotherapy response than personal genotype.
IL2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants have subtle effects on IL-2 expression. Not clinically actionable for most people.
IL2.actionability: No direct action for common variants. IL-2 therapy (aldesleukin) is still used for metastatic melanoma and renal cell carcinoma. Engineered IL-2 variants with better safety profiles are in clinical trials.
IL2.fun_fact: High-dose IL-2 immunotherapy (1992) could cure ~8% of metastatic melanoma patients — the first proof that the immune system could eliminate established cancer. The side effects were so severe (vascular leak syndrome) that only the healthiest patients could tolerate it.

YAP1.editorial_description: The organ size controller. YAP is the final effector of the Hippo pathway — it decides when organs should stop growing. When Hippo is off, YAP floods the nucleus and drives proliferation. Dysregulation leads to organ overgrowth and cancer.
YAP1.what_it_means: YAP is a transcriptional coactivator normally kept inactive by Hippo pathway kinases (LATS1/2) through phosphorylation and cytoplasmic sequestration. When active, YAP enters the nucleus and activates TEAD target genes driving growth and stemness.
YAP1.carrier_note: Germline YAP1 variants are rare because the gene is highly constrained. Somatic YAP amplification or activation occurs in liver cancer, lung cancer, and mesothelioma.
YAP1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants are generally benign. YAP is too essential for major inherited variation. Cancer-relevant changes are somatic.
YAP1.actionability: No germline action. YAP/TEAD inhibitors are in early clinical development as potential cancer therapies for Hippo-dysregulated tumors.
YAP1.fun_fact: The Hippo pathway was discovered in Drosophila when mutations caused organs to grow far larger than normal (like a hippo). It's one of the most conserved growth control pathways — it determines why your liver is the right size and stops growing when it should.

GSK3B.editorial_description: The everything kinase. GSK-3β touches glycogen metabolism, Wnt signaling, mood regulation, and neurodegeneration. Lithium (the oldest psychiatric drug) works by inhibiting GSK-3β — connecting it directly to bipolar disorder treatment.
GSK3B.what_it_means: GSK-3β phosphorylates glycogen synthase (blocking glycogen storage), beta-catenin (targeting it for destruction), and tau (promoting tangles). It's constitutively active and is INHIBITED by insulin, Wnt, and lithium.
GSK3B.carrier_note: Common GSK3B variants have modest effects. The gene is highly conserved due to its essential roles. Genetic variation mainly affects promoter activity and subtle kinase regulation.
GSK3B.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may subtly affect GSK-3β expression. Context for understanding Wnt signaling, insulin sensitivity, and potentially lithium response.
GSK3B.actionability: Context for understanding lithium mechanism and metabolic signaling. Exercise inhibits GSK-3β via the insulin/AKT pathway, providing metabolic and neuroprotective benefits.
GSK3B.fun_fact: Lithium has treated bipolar disorder since 1949, but we only recently confirmed it works via GSK-3β inhibition. GSK-3β also phosphorylates tau — making lithium a candidate Alzheimer's prevention drug, currently in clinical trials.

SMAD4.editorial_description: The TGF-β messenger to the nucleus. SMAD4 is the shared relay for all TGF-β superfamily signals. Its loss causes juvenile polyposis syndrome and disables TGF-β's tumor suppressive growth arrest — unleashing pancreatic and colorectal cancer.
SMAD4.what_it_means: SMAD4 partners with receptor-activated SMADs (2/3) to enter the nucleus and activate TGF-β target genes. Without SMAD4, cells can't respond to TGF-β's growth-stopping commands.
SMAD4.carrier_note: Germline SMAD4 mutations cause juvenile polyposis (multiple GI polyps, ~1 in 100,000). Somatic SMAD4 loss occurs in ~55% of pancreatic cancers and ~10-35% of colorectal cancers.
SMAD4.nonref_interpretation: Common variants are benign. Pathogenic mutations are rare and identified through family history of juvenile polyposis or hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia.
SMAD4.actionability: Juvenile polyposis carriers need GI surveillance starting in teens. SMAD4-related polyposis also overlaps with hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT), requiring vascular screening.
SMAD4.fun_fact: SMAD4 was originally called DPC4 ('Deleted in Pancreatic Cancer 4'). Its name was changed to SMAD4 after the protein family — SMADs are named by combining 'Sma' (C. elegans) and 'Mad' (Drosophila). Genetics naming is deeply unserious.

PTPN11.editorial_description: The Noonan syndrome gene. PTPN11 encodes SHP-2, a phosphatase that amplifies RAS signaling. Gain-of-function mutations cause the most common RASopathy — and SHP-2 inhibitors are an exciting new class of cancer drugs.
PTPN11.what_it_means: SHP-2 removes inhibitory phosphates, amplifying RAS/MAPK signaling downstream of growth factor receptors. Activating mutations increase RAS pathway output, causing developmental abnormalities or contributing to cancer.
PTPN11.carrier_note: PTPN11 mutations cause ~50% of Noonan syndrome (short stature, heart defects, facial features; ~1 in 1,000-2,500 births). Somatic mutations are found in juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia and some solid tumors.
PTPN11.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants should be evaluated carefully — some may be pathogenic Noonan mutations. Common polymorphisms exist but known activating mutations have specific clinical features.
PTPN11.actionability: Noonan syndrome is managed with growth hormone, cardiac surveillance, and developmental support. SHP-2 inhibitors are in trials for cancers driven by RAS pathway activation.
PTPN11.fun_fact: Noonan syndrome is as common as Turner syndrome but was only genetically explained in 2001. SHP-2 inhibitors are generating excitement because they can overcome resistance to MEK and KRAS inhibitors — attacking the RAS pathway from a new angle.

DNMT1.editorial_description: The methylation copier. DNMT1 maintains DNA methylation patterns through cell division — ensuring daughter cells inherit the same epigenetic state as parents. Without it, cells lose their identity and gene silencing unravels.
DNMT1.what_it_means: After DNA replication, one strand is methylated (old) and one isn't (new). DNMT1 reads the old strand and copies methylation to the new strand, maintaining epigenetic memory across generations of cells.
DNMT1.carrier_note: Germline DNMT1 mutations cause hereditary sensory neuropathy with dementia and hearing loss (rare). Common variants are benign. DNMT1 is too essential for major germline variation.
DNMT1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign polymorphisms. DNMT1 is highly conserved due to its critical role in maintaining cellular identity.
DNMT1.actionability: No direct action. DNMT inhibitors (azacitidine, decitabine) are approved for myelodysplastic syndromes and AML — they force demethylation and reactivation of silenced tumor suppressors.
DNMT1.fun_fact: DNMT1 copies methylation with ~95% fidelity per division. That 5% error rate means epigenetic patterns slowly drift over a lifetime — a form of 'epigenetic aging' that may explain why identical twins become less identical over time.

LGALS3.editorial_description: Better known as Galectin-3 — an emerging biomarker for heart failure and fibrosis. This sugar-binding lectin promotes scarring in the heart, liver, and kidneys. Blood galectin-3 levels predict heart failure progression and are now an FDA-cleared test.
LGALS3.what_it_means: Galectin-3 binds beta-galactosides on cell surfaces and in the matrix, promoting fibroblast activation and collagen deposition. It drives cardiac fibrosis, renal fibrosis, and the tumor microenvironment.
LGALS3.carrier_note: Common LGALS3 variants (e.g., rs4644, rs4652) affect galectin-3 levels and are found in 15-30% of people. Higher levels are associated with heart failure and mortality risk.
LGALS3.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered galectin-3 expression. Context for interpreting galectin-3 blood tests and understanding fibrosis predisposition.
LGALS3.actionability: Galectin-3 blood test can add prognostic information for heart failure patients. Modified citrus pectin may bind and reduce galectin-3, though clinical evidence is preliminary.
LGALS3.fun_fact: Galectin-3 was approved as a cardiac biomarker by the FDA in 2010 — joining BNP/NT-proBNP as a heart failure test. It specifically predicts fibrosis progression, not acute decompensation, filling a different clinical niche.

HDAC1.editorial_description: The gene compactor. HDAC1 removes acetyl marks from histones, tightening DNA into silent chromatin. HDAC inhibitors (vorinostat, romidepsin) force open cancer genomes, reactivating tumor suppressors — epigenetic cancer therapy.
HDAC1.what_it_means: Histone deacetylation by HDAC1 compacts chromatin and silences gene expression. It's recruited to gene promoters by transcriptional repressors. In cancer, excessive HDAC activity silences tumor suppressor genes.
HDAC1.carrier_note: HDAC1 is highly conserved with few common functional variants — it's too essential for major germline variation. Loss would be incompatible with normal development.
HDAC1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. HDAC1 is ubiquitously expressed and essential for normal gene regulation.
HDAC1.actionability: No germline action. HDAC inhibitors are approved for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and multiple myeloma. Valproate (epilepsy drug) is also an HDAC inhibitor.
HDAC1.fun_fact: Sodium butyrate (from gut bacteria fermenting fiber) is a natural HDAC inhibitor. This means your microbiome directly modulates your epigenetics through short-chain fatty acids — one mechanism linking dietary fiber to reduced colon cancer risk.

BAX.editorial_description: The cell death executioner. BAX punches holes in mitochondrial membranes, releasing cytochrome c and triggering the apoptotic cascade. It directly opposes BCL-2 — the balance between them decides if a cell lives or dies.
BAX.what_it_means: BAX oligomerizes on mitochondrial outer membranes when death signals outweigh survival signals, forming pores that release cytochrome c into the cytoplasm. This activates caspases and commits the cell to apoptosis.
BAX.carrier_note: Common BAX variants (e.g., -248 G>A promoter polymorphism) modestly affect expression. The G repeat in codon region is a common mutation site in microsatellite-unstable cancers.
BAX.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered pro-apoptotic capacity. Context for understanding the BCL-2/BAX apoptotic balance.
BAX.actionability: No direct action. BAX biology matters for cancer therapy response — tumors with BAX mutations resist apoptosis-inducing chemotherapy.
BAX.fun_fact: BAX (BCL-2 Associated X protein) was discovered as BCL-2's binding partner and nemesis. The cell death field describes their interaction as a molecular 'battle' — BAX and BAK trying to kill the cell while BCL-2 and BCL-XL try to save it.

CCL2.editorial_description: The macrophage recruiter. CCL2 (MCP-1) attracts monocytes to sites of inflammation and into adipose tissue. It's chronically elevated in obesity and atherosclerosis — the molecular link between fat, inflammation, and cardiovascular disease.
CCL2.what_it_means: CCL2 is a chemokine secreted by endothelial cells, macrophages, and adipocytes. It recruits monocytes via CCR2. In atherosclerosis, CCL2 pulls monocytes into artery walls where they become foam cells.
CCL2.carrier_note: The -2518 A>G variant (rs1024611) affects CCL2 expression and is found in ~25-35% of Europeans. The G allele (higher expression) is associated with higher monocyte recruitment and modestly increased cardiovascular risk.
CCL2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs1024611 may indicate higher CCL2 expression — enhanced monocyte recruitment and potentially more inflammatory macrophage infiltration.
CCL2.actionability: CCL2 is reduced by weight loss, exercise, and omega-3 fatty acids. Anti-CCL2/CCR2 therapies are in trials for diabetic kidney disease and cancer.
CCL2.fun_fact: CCL2 is one of the main reasons visceral fat is dangerous — belly fat secretes enormous amounts of CCL2, recruiting macrophages that create a state of chronic low-grade inflammation driving metabolic syndrome and cardiovascular disease.

AGTR1.editorial_description: The blood pressure drug target. AT1R is where angiotensin II acts to raise blood pressure. ARBs (losartan, valsartan) block this receptor — one of the most prescribed drug classes globally with billions in annual sales.
AGTR1.what_it_means: AGTR1 encodes the angiotensin II type 1 receptor, which mediates vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and cardiac remodeling when activated by angiotensin II. Blocking it lowers blood pressure and protects the heart.
AGTR1.carrier_note: The A1166C variant (rs5186) is found in ~25-30% of Europeans. The C allele is associated with slightly higher blood pressure and enhanced AT1R signaling, though effect sizes are modest.
AGTR1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs5186 may indicate enhanced angiotensin II signaling. Context for understanding blood pressure genetics and potentially ARB responsiveness.
AGTR1.actionability: Context for cardiovascular risk. ARBs are effective regardless of AGTR1 genotype. Standard hypertension management applies.
AGTR1.fun_fact: Losartan (the first ARB) was developed in 1986 after scientists realized ACE inhibitors had an annoying side effect (cough) caused by bradykinin accumulation. Blocking the receptor directly avoids this — pure pharmaceutical problem-solving.

IL1A.editorial_description: The cellular damage alarm. Unlike IL-1β (which requires processing), IL-1α is released passively when cells die — making it a 'damage-associated molecular pattern' that alerts the immune system to tissue injury and necrosis.
IL1A.what_it_means: IL-1α is constitutively expressed in epithelial cells and released upon cell death. It doesn't need inflammasome processing like IL-1β. It acts as an alarmin — signaling sterile inflammation after trauma, burns, or ischemia.
IL1A.carrier_note: Common IL1A variants have modest effects on expression and inflammatory responses. The gene is in a cluster with IL1B and IL1RN on chromosome 2, and variants are often in linkage disequilibrium.
IL1A.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may modestly affect IL-1α expression. Part of the broader IL-1 family inflammatory genetics landscape.
IL1A.actionability: Context for inflammatory phenotype. No specific action for common variants. IL-1 blockade (anakinra) targets both IL-1α and IL-1β.
IL1A.fun_fact: IL-1α has a unique 'dual life' — it works as a transcription factor INSIDE cells (bound to chromatin) and as a cytokine OUTSIDE cells (released upon death). Very few molecules have such radically different roles depending on location.

IL1RN.editorial_description: Nature's IL-1 blocker. IL-1Ra naturally antagonizes IL-1 by binding its receptor without activating it. Anakinra (the drug version) is a synthetic copy of this natural brake — treating rheumatoid arthritis, gout flares, and autoinflammatory diseases.
IL1RN.what_it_means: IL-1Ra competes with IL-1α and IL-1β for receptor binding but triggers no signal — a pure competitive antagonist. The balance between IL-1 and IL-1Ra determines net inflammatory state.
IL1RN.carrier_note: A VNTR polymorphism in IL1RN (IL-1Ra) intron 2 affects expression. The *2 allele (shorter) is associated with increased IL-1Ra production and is found in ~20-25% of Europeans.
IL1RN.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants affect the IL-1/IL-1Ra balance. Higher IL-1Ra is generally anti-inflammatory. The specific VNTR is difficult for arrays to capture directly.
IL1RN.actionability: Context for inflammatory balance. Anakinra (recombinant IL-1Ra) is prescribed for RA, gout, CAPS, and Still's disease. Your endogenous IL-1Ra levels are influenced by this gene.
IL1RN.fun_fact: Babies with DIRA (Deficiency of IL-1Ra) develop severe pustular skin disease and bone inflammation from birth — proving that IL-1Ra isn't optional, it's essential for preventing uncontrolled inflammation. Anakinra is life-saving for these infants.

KDR.editorial_description: VEGFR-2 — the main receptor through which VEGF drives blood vessel growth. Anti-VEGF therapies (bevacizumab, sunitinib) largely work by blocking KDR signaling. It's the therapeutic target side of the VEGF angiogenesis story.
KDR.what_it_means: KDR (VEGFR-2) is the primary receptor for VEGF-A on endothelial cells. Its activation triggers proliferation, migration, and survival of endothelial cells — building new blood vessels (angiogenesis).
KDR.carrier_note: Common KDR variants (e.g., Q472H, rs1870377) affect receptor function and are found in 15-25% of people. They may modestly influence angiogenic responses and anti-VEGF drug sensitivity.
KDR.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered VEGFR-2 signaling efficiency. Potentially relevant for anti-VEGF therapy response, though clinical use is not yet established.
KDR.actionability: Mostly research context. KDR variants are being investigated as predictors of anti-VEGF therapy response in cancer. No established clinical pharmacogenomic application yet.
KDR.fun_fact: KDR stands for 'Kinase Insert Domain Receptor' — a terrible name for what's arguably the most therapeutically important receptor in oncology. Multi-kinase inhibitors targeting KDR (sunitinib, sorafenib, lenvatinib) generate billions in annual cancer drug revenue.

CASP8.editorial_description: The apoptosis initiator. Caspase-8 is activated by death receptors (FAS, TRAIL-R) and cleaves downstream executioner caspases to kill the cell. It's the bridge between external death signals and internal destruction machinery.
CASP8.what_it_means: Caspase-8 is recruited to the DISC (death-inducing signaling complex) at death receptors. Once activated, it cleaves caspase-3 and BID, committing the cell to apoptosis. It's essential for immune-mediated cell killing.
CASP8.carrier_note: The -652 6N del variant (rs3834129) is found in ~20-30% of people. Deletions reduce caspase-8 expression and have been associated with reduced cancer risk in some populations (controversial across studies).
CASP8.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may affect caspase-8 expression and apoptotic threshold. Population-specific effects on cancer risk have been reported.
CASP8.actionability: No direct action. Caspase biology is important for understanding cancer therapy response — tumors resistant to apoptosis resist most chemotherapy.
CASP8.fun_fact: Caspase-8 has a surprising second job — it PREVENTS necroptosis (inflammatory cell death). Without caspase-8, cells die by necroptosis instead of apoptosis, causing massive inflammation. It's both a killer and a peacekeeper.

STAT1.editorial_description: The interferon signal relay. STAT1 transmits signals from type I, II, and III interferons to activate antiviral genes. Gain-of-function mutations cause chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis; loss-of-function causes mycobacterial susceptibility.
STAT1.what_it_means: STAT1 is phosphorylated by JAKs downstream of interferon receptors, dimerizes, and enters the nucleus to activate interferon-stimulated genes (ISGs). These ISGs create an antiviral state in cells.
STAT1.carrier_note: Gain-of-function STAT1 mutations cause chronic fungal infections (CMC) — rare. Loss-of-function causes severe mycobacterial and viral infections. Common variants are mostly benign.
STAT1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. True pathogenic mutations present with recurrent infections in childhood and require clinical immunology workup.
STAT1.actionability: Relevant for patients with recurrent mucocutaneous candidiasis or unusual mycobacterial infections. STAT1 gain-of-function is treated with JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib).
STAT1.fun_fact: STAT1 knockout mice are hypersusceptible to viral infections but completely resistant to interferon's antiproliferative effects. This proved that interferons fight cancer partly through STAT1 — connecting antiviral and antitumor immunity.

MIF.editorial_description: Macrophage Migration Inhibitory Factor — a bizarre cytokine that looks like an enzyme and was one of the first lymphokines discovered (1966). It counteracts glucocorticoid suppression, sustaining inflammation when your body tries to shut it down.
MIF.what_it_means: MIF opposes cortisol's anti-inflammatory effects at inflammatory sites. It's released by immune cells AND pituitary gland, creating a counter-regulatory system. High MIF = sustained inflammation despite cortisol.
MIF.carrier_note: A CATT repeat (5-8 repeats) at position -794 affects MIF expression. Higher repeats (7-8) = more MIF production and are associated with more severe inflammatory diseases. Found in ~20-30% of people.
MIF.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered MIF expression. Higher-expression variants are associated with more robust inflammatory responses and potentially steroid resistance.
MIF.actionability: Context for understanding inflammatory responses and potentially glucocorticoid resistance. MIF inhibitors are in clinical trials for inflammatory diseases.
MIF.fun_fact: MIF was discovered in 1966, making it one of the very first cytokines described. Despite 57+ years of research, it remains enigmatic — it has tautomerase enzymatic activity with no known physiological substrate. Nobody knows why a cytokine moonlights as an enzyme.

TNFSF10.editorial_description: Better known as TRAIL (TNF-Related Apoptosis-Inducing Ligand). It kills cancer cells while sparing normal ones, making it one of the most exciting (and frustrating) immunotherapy concepts. Despite decades of work, harnessing TRAIL clinically remains challenging.
TNFSF10.what_it_means: TRAIL (TNF-Related Apoptosis-Inducing Ligand) binds death receptors DR4/DR5 on target cells, activating caspase-8 and apoptosis. Normal cells resist TRAIL through decoy receptors and high c-FLIP — cancer cells often lack these protections.
TNFSF10.carrier_note: Common TNFSF10 variants have minimal clinical significance for individual health. TRAIL biology is primarily relevant for cancer therapy and immune surveillance research.
TNFSF10.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign polymorphisms. TRAIL's importance is in tumor biology and therapy, not germline genetics.
TNFSF10.actionability: No germline action. TRAIL receptor agonists are in ongoing clinical trials. Exercise increases circulating TRAIL levels — one proposed mechanism for exercise's anti-cancer effect.
TNFSF10.fun_fact: TRAIL was discovered in 1995 and immediately generated enormous excitement because it killed cancer cells but not normal ones in mice. Two decades and multiple failed clinical trials later, new formulations (nanoparticles, combination strategies) are reviving the dream.

TLR2.editorial_description: The bacterial lipid sensor. TLR2 detects lipoproteins, peptidoglycan, and fungal cell walls — a broader pathogen palette than TLR4. Variants affect susceptibility to tuberculosis, sepsis, and inflammatory diseases.
TLR2.what_it_means: TLR2 heterodimerizes with TLR1 or TLR6 to recognize different microbial lipids. It activates NF-κB and inflammatory cytokine production. Polymorphisms affect ligand recognition and signaling intensity.
TLR2.carrier_note: The R753Q variant (rs5743708) is found in ~3-8% of Europeans. It reduces TLR2 responsiveness and is associated with increased susceptibility to tuberculosis and staphylococcal infections in some populations.
TLR2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs5743708 indicates reduced TLR2 signaling. May provide context for infection susceptibility patterns.
TLR2.actionability: Context for infection susceptibility. No direct action for most people. TLR2 agonists are being developed as vaccine adjuvants.
TLR2.fun_fact: TLR2 is one of the most promiscuous pattern recognition receptors — it recognizes lipoproteins from bacteria, zymosan from fungi, and even some viral proteins. Its flexibility comes from forming different heterodimers (TLR1/TLR2 vs TLR2/TLR6) to expand its detection range.

CXCL8.editorial_description: Better known as IL-8 — the neutrophil recruiter. CXCL8 is the primary chemokine that summons neutrophils to infection and injury sites. It's chronically elevated in COPD, cystic fibrosis, and the tumor microenvironment.
CXCL8.what_it_means: CXCL8 creates a chemical gradient that guides neutrophils from blood to tissue. It binds CXCR1/CXCR2 on neutrophils, triggering migration, degranulation, and oxidative burst at the infection site.
CXCL8.carrier_note: The -251 A>T variant (rs4073) affects CXCL8 expression and is found in ~40% of Europeans. The A allele (higher expression) is associated with more robust neutrophil responses.
CXCL8.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered CXCL8 expression. Context for understanding neutrophilic inflammation intensity.
CXCL8.actionability: Context for inflammatory phenotype. CXCR1/CXCR2 antagonists are in trials for COPD and cancer. No direct action for common variants.
CXCL8.fun_fact: CXCL8 was originally called IL-8 and was one of the first chemokines discovered (1987). It was renamed when the systematic chemokine nomenclature was adopted — but most researchers still call it IL-8 out of habit, creating perpetual confusion in the literature.

HMGB1.editorial_description: The danger signal protein. HMGB1 normally organizes DNA inside the nucleus, but when released from dying cells, it becomes a potent alarmin — one of the key molecules driving sepsis, sterile inflammation, and autoimmunity.
HMGB1.what_it_means: Inside cells, HMGB1 bends DNA and facilitates transcription. Outside cells (released by necrosis or actively secreted), it binds RAGE and TLR4, triggering massive inflammatory responses. It's a quintessential damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP).
HMGB1.carrier_note: HMGB1 is one of the most conserved proteins in nature (>99% identical between species). It has essentially no common functional variants in humans — it's too essential to tolerate mutation.
HMGB1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are extremely rare. HMGB1's importance is in pathophysiology (sepsis, trauma), not personal genetics.
HMGB1.actionability: No germline action. Anti-HMGB1 therapies are in development for sepsis and inflammatory diseases. Ethyl pyruvate inhibits HMGB1 release.
HMGB1.fun_fact: HMGB1 is released by necrotic (but not apoptotic) cells, which is how your immune system distinguishes accidental death from programmed death. Kevin Tracey discovered that blocking HMGB1 can prevent sepsis death in mice — it's a critical late mediator.

IL18.editorial_description: The interferon-gamma inducer. IL-18 synergizes with IL-12 to drive Th1 immunity and NK cell activation. It's inflammasome-processed (like IL-1β) and elevated in autoimmune diseases, particularly macrophage activation syndrome.
IL18.what_it_means: IL-18 is processed by caspase-1 (inflammasome) and potently induces IFN-γ production from T cells and NK cells. Combined with IL-12, it's the strongest known stimulus for Th1 polarization.
IL18.carrier_note: Promoter variants (-607 C>A, -137 G>C) affect IL-18 expression. The high-expression haplotype is found in ~20-30% of people and associated with more robust Th1 responses.
IL18.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference promoter variants may indicate altered IL-18 production — affecting Th1/Th2 balance and inflammatory phenotype.
IL18.actionability: Context for immune phenotype. IL-18 binding protein (natural antagonist) and anti-IL-18 antibodies are in trials for macrophage activation syndrome and Still's disease.
IL18.fun_fact: IL-18 was originally discovered as 'IFN-gamma-inducing factor' (IGIF) in 1995 during a search for what makes T cells produce interferon. It was renamed IL-18 to fit the interleukin numbering system. Its crystal structure revealed surprising similarity to IL-1β.

HLA-DQB1.editorial_description: The celiac disease and type 1 diabetes gene. HLA-DQB1 alleles are the strongest genetic determinants of both conditions. Nearly 100% of celiac patients carry DQ2 or DQ8 — making it one of the strongest HLA-disease associations known.
HLA-DQB1.what_it_means: HLA-DQB1 forms half of the HLA-DQ heterodimer (with DQA1) that presents peptides to CD4+ T cells. DQ2 (DQB1*02) and DQ8 (DQB1*03:02) present gluten peptides particularly well, triggering autoimmune attack on intestinal tissue.
HLA-DQB1.carrier_note: DQ2 is carried by ~25-30% of Europeans but only ~1% develop celiac disease — necessary but not sufficient. DQ2/DQ8 testing has high negative predictive value (absence virtually rules out celiac).
HLA-DQB1.nonref_interpretation: Consumer array HLA-DQ typing is imprecise. Clinical HLA-DQ testing is used to rule OUT celiac disease (negative test = <1% chance of celiac regardless of symptoms).
HLA-DQB1.actionability: DQ2/DQ8 status is useful for ruling out celiac disease. Presence doesn't confirm it (most carriers are healthy). Relevant for type 1 diabetes family risk assessment.
HLA-DQB1.fun_fact: HLA-DQ2 is so strongly associated with celiac disease that if you don't carry it (or DQ8), you can eat all the gluten you want without ever developing celiac — making HLA-DQ testing one of the few genetic tests with near-perfect negative predictive value.

SRC.editorial_description: The first oncogene ever discovered. v-Src was identified in Rous sarcoma virus in 1976, proving cancer could be caused by single genes. This discovery launched the entire field of molecular oncology and won a Nobel Prize.
SRC.what_it_means: SRC is a non-receptor tyrosine kinase that relays growth factor signals. It's activated by EGFR, integrins, and other receptors. Normal c-SRC is tightly regulated; viral v-Src is constitutively active (causing sarcomas).
SRC.carrier_note: Germline SRC variants are rare and mostly benign. SRC overactivity in cancer is typically due to upstream pathway activation, not SRC mutations. The gene is more important as a signaling node than a mutation target.
SRC.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. SRC is not commonly mutated in human cancers despite being the first identified oncogene.
SRC.actionability: No germline action. Dasatinib (a CML drug) also potently inhibits SRC, which contributes to its activity in certain solid tumors.
SRC.fun_fact: Peyton Rous discovered in 1911 that a virus could cause cancer in chickens, but wasn't believed for 55 years. Harold Varmus and Michael Bishop won the 1989 Nobel Prize for showing the viral oncogene (v-src) was actually a stolen, mutated version of a normal chicken gene (c-src).

EP300.editorial_description: The epigenetic writer. p300 acetylates histones to open chromatin and activate genes. It's a transcriptional coactivator recruited by hundreds of transcription factors — making it a master integrator of gene activation signals.
EP300.what_it_means: p300 (and its paralog CBP/CREBBP) adds acetyl groups to histones, loosening DNA-histone interactions and enabling gene transcription. It's recruited by p53, NF-κB, HIF, and many other transcription factors.
EP300.carrier_note: Germline EP300 mutations cause Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome type 2 (intellectual disability, broad thumbs, facial features — rare). Somatic mutations are found in various cancers. Common variants are benign.
EP300.nonref_interpretation: Common non-reference variants are generally benign polymorphisms. Pathogenic mutations are rare and present with developmental features.
EP300.actionability: Relevant for families with Rubinstein-Taybi features. p300 inhibitors and degraders are in early cancer clinical trials.
EP300.fun_fact: p300 is named for its molecular weight (300 kDa). It and CBP together acetylate more substrates than any other acetyltransferases in the cell. They're so important that adenovirus E1A protein specifically evolved to hijack p300/CBP — diverting the cell's gene activation machinery for viral replication.

RELA.editorial_description: The NF-κB p65 subunit — the transcription factor that actually enters the nucleus and turns on inflammatory genes. RELA is the 'business end' of the NF-κB complex, directly binding DNA at hundreds of inflammatory gene promoters.
RELA.what_it_means: RELA (p65) heterodimerizes with NFKB1 (p50) to form the canonical NF-κB complex. When released from IκB inhibition, p65's transactivation domain drives inflammatory gene expression.
RELA.carrier_note: RELA is extremely conserved. Common variants have minimal characterized effects. The gene is essential — RELA knockout is embryonic lethal in mice.
RELA.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are rare and generally benign. RELA's importance is in inflammatory pathophysiology, not personal genetic variation.
RELA.actionability: No direct action. NF-κB/RELA is suppressed by corticosteroids, aspirin, and many natural compounds (curcumin, resveratrol). These effects are independent of RELA genotype.
RELA.fun_fact: RELA was named 'v-rel avian reticuloendotheliosis viral oncogene homolog A' — one of the worst gene names ever. It was discovered through a turkey retrovirus that caused lymphomas by hijacking this inflammatory transcription factor.

CAV1.editorial_description: Caveolin-1 — the scaffolding protein that creates membrane pits (caveolae) on cell surfaces. It regulates cholesterol transport, eNOS signaling, and mechanosensing. Loss drives aggressive cancer and pulmonary fibrosis.
CAV1.what_it_means: CAV1 forms flask-shaped membrane invaginations (caveolae) that compartmentalize signaling molecules. It directly inhibits eNOS, EGFR, and other signaling proteins, acting as a general signaling dampener.
CAV1.carrier_note: Common CAV1 variants are poorly characterized for health effects. Somatic CAV1 loss occurs in aggressive breast and prostate cancers. Germline mutations cause lipodystrophy (rare).
CAV1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants have uncertain significance for most people. CAV1 biology is complex and context-dependent.
CAV1.actionability: No direct action for common variants. CAV1 expression is modulated by cholesterol and mechanical forces (exercise, shear stress).
CAV1.fun_fact: Caveolae ('little caves') were first seen by electron microscopy in 1953. They make up to 30% of endothelial cell surface area. Some cells have thousands of these tiny pits, which serve as signaling platforms, endocytic carriers, and mechanical shock absorbers.

ERCC2.editorial_description: The nucleotide excision repair helicase. ERCC2 (XPD) unwinds DNA at UV damage sites for repair. Variants affect cancer susceptibility from sun exposure and predict platinum chemotherapy response in bladder cancer.
ERCC2.what_it_means: ERCC2 is a DNA helicase essential for nucleotide excision repair (NER) — the pathway that fixes UV-induced pyrimidine dimers and bulky DNA adducts. It also functions in transcription as part of TFIIH.
ERCC2.carrier_note: The Lys751Gln variant (rs13181) is found in ~35% of Europeans. Gln751 is associated with reduced DNA repair capacity and modestly elevated cancer risk, particularly in smokers and sun-exposed individuals.
ERCC2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs13181 (Gln751) indicates reduced NER capacity. Context for UV damage repair and potentially platinum drug sensitivity.
ERCC2.actionability: Reduced repair variants may benefit most from sun protection and avoiding carcinogen exposure. ERCC2 mutations in bladder tumors predict cisplatin sensitivity — a pharmacogenomic biomarker.
ERCC2.fun_fact: Biallelic ERCC2 mutations cause xeroderma pigmentosum (XP) — extreme sun sensitivity with 1000x increased skin cancer risk. XP patients must avoid all UV light, earning them the name 'children of the night.' It proves how essential DNA repair is for sun-exposed tissues.

ESR2.editorial_description: Estrogen receptor beta — ESR1's counterbalancing sibling. While ESR1 (ERα) drives cell proliferation, ESR2 (ERβ) generally opposes it. The balance between them influences breast cancer biology, bone health, and mood.
ESR2.what_it_means: ERβ binds estrogen and often antagonizes ERα's proliferative effects. It promotes apoptosis and differentiation in some tissues while mediating estrogen's protective effects in brain, colon, and bone.
ESR2.carrier_note: Common ESR2 variants (e.g., rs4986938, rs1256049) affect expression and are found in 20-40% of people. Effects on bone density, depression, and cancer risk have been reported but are modest.
ESR2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered ERβ expression. Context for understanding tissue-specific estrogen responses.
ESR2.actionability: Context for estrogen biology. Phytoestrogens (soy isoflavones) preferentially bind ERβ over ERα, which may explain some of their distinct biological effects.
ESR2.fun_fact: ERβ was only discovered in 1996 (30 years after ERα), hiding in prostate and ovary. Its discovery explained why estrogen has opposing effects in different tissues — the ratio of ERα to ERβ determines whether estrogen stimulates or inhibits growth.

BIRC5.editorial_description: Better known as Survivin — the anti-death protein expressed in virtually every tumor but almost never in normal adult tissue. This makes it one of the most cancer-specific targets known, driving intense drug and vaccine development.
BIRC5.what_it_means: Survivin inhibits apoptosis by blocking caspase activation and is essential for chromosome segregation during mitosis. It's expressed in embryonic tissues and reactivated in cancer, enabling both immortality and division.
BIRC5.carrier_note: Common BIRC5 promoter variants (e.g., -31 G>C, rs9904341) affect survivin expression and are found in ~40% of people. Higher-expression variants are associated with modestly increased cancer risk.
BIRC5.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs9904341 may indicate altered survivin expression. Context for cancer risk but not individually actionable.
BIRC5.actionability: No direct action. Survivin inhibitors and survivin-targeting cancer vaccines are in clinical trials — its tumor-specificity makes it an attractive target with potentially few side effects.
BIRC5.fun_fact: Survivin is the fourth most commonly overexpressed gene across all human cancers. Its near-absence in normal adult tissue (except thymus and placenta) makes it a near-perfect tumor marker. Survivin vaccines are being tested to train the immune system to kill survivin-expressing cancer cells.

MAPK1.editorial_description: Better known as ERK2 — the final kinase in the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK cascade. It enters the nucleus and activates transcription factors driving cell proliferation. MEK inhibitors (trametinib) blocking ERK activation are standard cancer therapy.
MAPK1.what_it_means: ERK2 (MAPK1) is phosphorylated by MEK1/2 and translocates to the nucleus where it activates transcription factors (ELK1, MYC, FOS). It's the terminal effector of the RAS/MAPK growth signaling pathway.
MAPK1.carrier_note: MAPK1 is highly conserved with no well-characterized common pathogenic variants. It's too essential for significant germline variation. Pathway activation in cancer comes from upstream mutations (RAS, RAF).
MAPK1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. ERK2 itself is rarely mutated — cancer activates this pathway through upstream oncogenes.
MAPK1.actionability: No germline action. MEK inhibitors (trametinib, cobimetinib) prevent ERK activation and are standard-of-care for BRAF-mutant melanoma in combination with BRAF inhibitors.
MAPK1.fun_fact: The RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK cascade is sometimes called the 'MAPK pathway' even though there are actually three parallel MAPK pathways in mammals (ERK, JNK, p38). This naming confusion has persisted for 30 years and still trips up students.

SMAD3.editorial_description: The TGF-β signal carrier. SMAD3 is phosphorylated by TGF-β receptors and partners with SMAD4 to enter the nucleus. It mediates fibrosis, immune regulation, and growth arrest — making it relevant to scarring, autoimmunity, and cancer.
SMAD3.what_it_means: SMAD3 is directly activated by TGF-β type I receptor kinase. Once phosphorylated, it binds SMAD4, translocates to the nucleus, and activates target genes including collagen, fibronectin, and cell cycle inhibitors.
SMAD3.carrier_note: Germline SMAD3 mutations cause Loeys-Dietz syndrome type 3 (aneurysms, skeletal anomalies) — rare. Common variants modestly affect TGF-β responsiveness and are found in normal variation.
SMAD3.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are mostly benign. Rare pathogenic mutations present with aortic aneurysms and connective tissue features.
SMAD3.actionability: Families with aortic aneurysms, especially with skeletal features, should consider SMAD3 testing. Confirmed carriers need aortic imaging surveillance.
SMAD3.fun_fact: SMAD3 mediates TGF-β's fibrotic effects in virtually every organ — liver cirrhosis, pulmonary fibrosis, kidney scarring, and cardiac fibrosis all involve SMAD3-driven collagen production. It's a unifying mechanism across fibrotic diseases.

IL4.editorial_description: The allergy cytokine. IL-4 drives Th2 immunity, IgE class switching, and eosinophil recruitment. It's the master regulator behind asthma, eczema, and allergic rhinitis. Dupilumab blocking IL-4/IL-13 transformed atopic disease treatment.
IL4.what_it_means: IL-4 polarizes T cells to Th2, drives B cells to produce IgE antibodies, and promotes mucus production and eosinophil recruitment. It signals through IL-4Rα, which is shared with IL-13.
IL4.carrier_note: The -590 C>T promoter variant (rs2243250) increases IL-4 expression and is found in ~15-25% of Europeans. The T allele is associated with higher IgE levels and atopic disease risk.
IL4.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs2243250 indicates higher IL-4 expression — context for understanding Th2 bias, allergy susceptibility, and IgE levels.
IL4.actionability: Context for atopic phenotype. Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα) treats moderate-severe atopic dermatitis and asthma regardless of IL4 genotype.
IL4.fun_fact: Dupilumab (blocking IL-4 and IL-13 simultaneously) achieved 75% eczema improvement in patients who had failed everything else. It's now being tested in COPD, food allergy, and chronic sinusitis — suggesting Th2 inflammation underlies far more disease than previously recognized.

JUN.editorial_description: c-Jun — half of the AP-1 transcription factor that responds to stress, growth factors, and UV radiation. It was one of the earliest oncogenes identified and connects extracellular signals to gene expression changes within minutes.
JUN.what_it_means: c-Jun heterodimerizes with c-Fos to form AP-1, which binds TRE/CRE elements in promoters of genes controlling proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis. It's rapidly activated by JNK phosphorylation in response to stress.
JUN.carrier_note: JUN is highly conserved with no well-characterized common pathogenic variants. It's essential for development and wound healing. Variation is minimal because disruption is incompatible with normal function.
JUN.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. JUN's importance is in signal transduction biology, not personal genetic variation.
JUN.actionability: No direct action. AP-1 is activated by UV radiation, oxidative stress, and inflammatory cytokines. Sun protection reduces aberrant AP-1 activation in skin.
JUN.fun_fact: JUN comes from 'ju-nana' (Japanese for 17) — avian sarcoma virus 17 carried v-jun. Combined with FOS (from FBJ osteosarcoma virus), they form AP-1 — perhaps the most rapidly responsive transcription factor in mammalian cells, activating within 15 minutes of stimulation.

CD4.editorial_description: The HIV receptor and immune system conductor. CD4 marks helper T cells — the maestros that coordinate all adaptive immunity. HIV targets CD4+ cells for destruction, which is why AIDS causes total immune collapse.
CD4.what_it_means: CD4 is a co-receptor on helper T cells that stabilizes MHC class II interactions during antigen recognition. It also recruits Lck kinase to amplify TCR signaling. HIV gp120 binds CD4 as its primary receptor for cell entry.
CD4.carrier_note: CD4 the gene has minimal pathogenic variation in the general population. CD4 T cell COUNT (measured in blood tests) is the critical clinical parameter — it reflects immune status, not genetics.
CD4.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants in CD4 are rare and generally benign. The gene is clinically relevant as a cell surface marker and HIV receptor, not as a genetic risk factor.
CD4.actionability: No genetic action. CD4 count monitoring guides HIV treatment decisions. CD4 <200 cells/µL defines AIDS.
CD4.fun_fact: The CD4 count was the first surrogate marker for AIDS progression (before viral load testing existed). A normal count is 500-1500 cells/µL. Without treatment, HIV systematically depletes CD4 cells over ~10 years until the immune system collapses.

HSP90AA1.editorial_description: Better known as HSP90 — the protein chaperone that cancer depends on. It helps hundreds of client proteins fold correctly — including many oncoproteins. Cancer cells are 'addicted' to HSP90, making it an attractive drug target for simultaneously hitting multiple cancer pathways.
HSP90AA1.what_it_means: HSP90 stabilizes client proteins (including HER2, EGFR, AKT, RAF, mutant p53) in their active conformations. Under stress, it prevents protein aggregation. Cancer cells have higher HSP90 activity to support their mutated proteome.
HSP90AA1.carrier_note: HSP90AA1 is highly conserved and essential. Common variants have minimal characterized health effects. The gene is more important as a drug target than as a source of genetic risk.
HSP90AA1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. HSP90 biology matters for cancer therapy and proteostasis, not personal genetic risk assessment.
HSP90AA1.actionability: No germline action. HSP90 inhibitors have been extensively trialed in cancer (17 compounds), with some combinations showing promise in HER2+ breast cancer and ALK+ lung cancer.
HSP90AA1.fun_fact: HSP90 constitutes 1-2% of total cellular protein even without stress — one of the most abundant proteins in every cell. Cancer cells upregulate it further to 3-5%, essentially needing extra chaperoning capacity to maintain their unstable mutant proteomes.

VIM.editorial_description: Better known as Vimentin — the intermediate filament of mesenchymal cells. It's the hallmark of epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) in cancer. When tumor cells gain vimentin, they've switched from growing to invading.
VIM.what_it_means: Vimentin provides structural support and mechanical resilience to mesenchymal cells (fibroblasts, endothelial cells, immune cells). In carcinomas, vimentin expression indicates EMT — cells becoming mobile and invasive.
VIM.carrier_note: VIM has no well-characterized pathogenic germline variants. It's more important as a diagnostic/prognostic marker (EMT indicator in tumors) than as a source of genetic disease.
VIM.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. Vimentin's clinical relevance is in tumor pathology and EMT assessment.
VIM.actionability: No germline action. Vimentin expression in tumor biopsies indicates mesenchymal/invasive phenotype and may predict therapy resistance.
VIM.fun_fact: Vimentin-expressing circulating tumor cells (CTCs) are associated with metastatic spread. Pathologists use vimentin immunostaining to identify mesenchymal tumors (sarcomas) vs epithelial tumors (carcinomas) — it's one of the most ordered diagnostic stains.

MKI67.editorial_description: Better known as Ki-67 — the proliferation marker used in virtually every cancer pathology report. The percentage of tumor cells staining positive for Ki-67 directly indicates how fast the cancer is growing. High Ki-67 = aggressive; low = indolent.
MKI67.what_it_means: Ki-67 is expressed exclusively in proliferating cells (all cell cycle phases except G0). It coats chromosomes during mitosis, though its exact function remains surprisingly unclear. Its clinical value is purely as a proliferation marker.
MKI67.carrier_note: MKI67 has no clinically relevant germline variants. Its importance is entirely as a tumor proliferation marker — the Ki-67 index on biopsy reports guides treatment intensity decisions.
MKI67.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are not clinically significant. Ki-67's role is in tumor pathology, not personal genetics.
MKI67.actionability: No germline action. Ki-67 labeling index on tumor biopsies guides chemotherapy decisions, particularly in breast cancer (high Ki-67 = benefit from additional chemo).
MKI67.fun_fact: Ki-67 was named after the city of Kiel, Germany (Ki) and the number of the original clone in the 67th well of a 96-well plate. Despite being used in pathology for 40 years, nobody fully understands what Ki-67 DOES — we just know proliferating cells express it.

CXCL12.editorial_description: SDF-1 — the stem cell 'home' signal. CXCL12 anchors stem cells in bone marrow niches and guides them during development. It's the ligand for CXCR4, and disrupting their interaction mobilizes stem cells into blood for transplant.
CXCL12.what_it_means: CXCL12 (Stromal cell-Derived Factor 1) is constitutively expressed by bone marrow stromal cells, creating a gradient that retains hematopoietic stem cells. It also guides neuronal migration and vascular development.
CXCL12.carrier_note: The 3'UTR variant (rs1801157) affects CXCL12 expression and is found in ~20-25% of Europeans. It's been associated with HIV progression rate and various cancer outcomes in multiple studies.
CXCL12.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered CXCL12 expression. Context for understanding stem cell biology, HIV susceptibility, and cancer metastasis.
CXCL12.actionability: No direct action. Plerixafor (CXCR4 antagonist) disrupts the CXCL12-CXCR4 axis to mobilize stem cells from bone marrow for collection before transplant.
CXCL12.fun_fact: CXCL12 was the last essential chemokine to be knocked out in mice (2003) — it's lethal before birth due to defects in heart development, blood formation, and brain wiring. It's one of the few chemokines essential for development rather than just immunity.

CREBBP.editorial_description: CBP — p300's twin and co-writer of the epigenetic code. CREBBP acetylates histones and coactivates transcription factors. Germline mutations cause Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome; somatic mutations are common in lymphomas.
CREBBP.what_it_means: CBP (CREB-Binding Protein) is a histone acetyltransferase and transcriptional coactivator nearly identical to p300 (EP300). It was discovered through its interaction with CREB, linking cAMP signaling to gene activation.
CREBBP.carrier_note: Germline CREBBP mutations cause Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome type 1 (intellectual disability, broad thumbs, facial features; ~1 in 125,000). Somatic mutations are found in ~30% of follicular lymphomas.
CREBBP.nonref_interpretation: Common variants are benign. Pathogenic mutations are rare and present with developmental features in childhood.
CREBBP.actionability: Relevant for Rubinstein-Taybi diagnosis. In lymphoma, CREBBP mutations may predict response to HDAC inhibitors (restoring acetylation that CBP loss eliminated).
CREBBP.fun_fact: CBP and p300 are so similar (63% identical) that they were discovered independently — CBP through cAMP signaling, p300 through adenovirus biology. They share most functions but have some non-redundant roles, as proven by the fact that mutations in either cause distinct syndromes.

AGER.editorial_description: Better known as RAGE (Receptor for Advanced Glycation End-products). It binds damage signals (AGEs, HMGB1, amyloid) and drives chronic inflammation. It's chronically activated in diabetes, Alzheimer's, and atherosclerosis — the diseases of aging.
AGER.what_it_means: RAGE binds multiple ligands including AGEs (from glycation), HMGB1 (from cell damage), S100 proteins, and amyloid-β. All these signals converge on NF-κB activation and sustained inflammation.
AGER.carrier_note: The Gly82Ser variant (rs2070600) affects RAGE ligand binding and is found in ~5-15% of populations. Soluble RAGE (sRAGE) acts as a decoy receptor — common variants affect the sRAGE/membrane RAGE ratio.
AGER.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may alter RAGE signaling or sRAGE production. Context for understanding AGE-driven inflammation in metabolic disease.
AGER.actionability: Reduce AGE formation: cook at lower temperatures, manage blood glucose, avoid browning/charring foods. AGE accumulation activates RAGE and drives diabetic complications.
AGER.fun_fact: AGEs (Advanced Glycation End-products) form when sugars react with proteins non-enzymatically — the same Maillard reaction that browns toast. High blood sugar accelerates AGE formation throughout the body, and RAGE amplifies the damage. It's why diabetes accelerates every aging process.

NPM1.editorial_description: Nucleophosmin — the most commonly mutated gene in acute myeloid leukemia. NPM1 mutations cause its protein to mislocalize from the nucleolus to the cytoplasm, creating a diagnostic marker visible by simple immunostaining.
NPM1.what_it_means: NPM1 shuttles between nucleus and cytoplasm, chaperoning ribosome assembly, centrosome duplication, and genomic stability. AML mutations add a nuclear export signal, trapping it in the cytoplasm where it can't perform nuclear functions.
NPM1.carrier_note: NPM1 mutations are somatic (found in ~30% of adult AML). They're actually a FAVORABLE prognostic marker — NPM1-mutated AML without FLT3-ITD has good outcomes with chemotherapy. Germline variants are benign.
NPM1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference germline variants are benign polymorphisms. Cancer-relevant NPM1 mutations are somatic and detected by AML diagnostic panels.
NPM1.actionability: No germline action. NPM1 mutation status in AML guides prognosis and treatment decisions (favorable risk, may not need transplant in first remission).
NPM1.fun_fact: NPM1 mutations are the single most common genetic event in AML (30% of cases) and define their own WHO leukemia subtype. The cytoplasmic mislocalization is so consistent that pathologists can diagnose NPM1-mutant AML with a simple immunostain — no sequencing needed.

TP63.editorial_description: p53's skin-and-bone sibling. p63 is essential for epithelial development — without it, no skin, limbs, or teeth form. Unlike p53 (tumor suppressor), p63 primarily controls stem cell maintenance and epithelial differentiation.
TP63.what_it_means: p63 maintains basal stem cells in skin, breast, and prostate epithelium. The ΔNp63 isoform sustains self-renewal while TAp63 promotes differentiation. It's structurally similar to p53 but functionally distinct.
TP63.carrier_note: Germline TP63 mutations cause ectodermal dysplasia syndromes (EEC syndrome: ectrodactyly, ectodermal dysplasia, cleft lip/palate) — rare but striking. Common variants are benign.
TP63.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign polymorphisms. Pathogenic mutations cause visible developmental abnormalities from birth.
TP63.actionability: Relevant for families with split hand/foot malformations, ectodermal dysplasia, or cleft lip with limb anomalies.
TP63.fun_fact: TP63 knockout mice are born without skin, limbs, or teeth — one of the most dramatic developmental phenotypes known. p63 was the first p53 family member discovered (p73 was second), revealing that p53 has relatives with completely different day jobs.

CASP3.editorial_description: The executioner caspase. Once caspase-3 is activated, cell death is irreversible — it cleaves hundreds of substrates, dismantling the cell in minutes. It's the convergence point where all apoptosis pathways commit to destruction.
CASP3.what_it_means: Caspase-3 is activated by both intrinsic (mitochondrial/caspase-9) and extrinsic (death receptor/caspase-8) pathways. It cleaves structural proteins, DNA repair enzymes, and activates endonucleases that fragment DNA.
CASP3.carrier_note: Common CASP3 variants have minimal characterized health effects. The gene is highly conserved due to its essential role in development (apoptosis sculpts organs during embryogenesis).
CASP3.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. Caspase-3 activity matters for cancer therapy response, not personal genetics.
CASP3.actionability: No direct action. Caspase-3 activation is the readout used to measure whether cancer cells are actually dying during chemotherapy.
CASP3.fun_fact: Caspase-3 cleaves over 600 different substrates during apoptosis. One target is ICAD (inhibitor of caspase-activated DNase) — releasing the endonuclease that creates the 'DNA ladder' pattern, a classic laboratory hallmark of apoptosis.

HLA-G.editorial_description: The pregnancy tolerance molecule. HLA-G is expressed by placental trophoblasts to prevent maternal immune rejection of the fetus. It's also exploited by tumors and transplanted organs to evade immune attack.
HLA-G.what_it_means: HLA-G inhibits NK cells, T cells, and dendritic cells through ILT2/ILT4 receptors. At the maternal-fetal interface, it creates immune privilege. Tumors re-express HLA-G to mimic this fetal immune evasion strategy.
HLA-G.carrier_note: The 14bp insertion/deletion in the 3'UTR affects HLA-G expression and mRNA stability. The deletion allele (higher expression) is found in ~40-60% of people. Low HLA-G is associated with recurrent pregnancy loss.
HLA-G.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered HLA-G expression. Potentially relevant for reproductive immunology and pregnancy outcomes.
HLA-G.actionability: Context for reproductive health. Low HLA-G expression has been associated with recurrent miscarriage and pre-eclampsia, though clinical testing is not yet standard.
HLA-G.fun_fact: HLA-G is one of the least polymorphic HLA genes (opposite of HLA-A/B/C) because the fetus needs to hide from ALL maternal immune responses regardless of HLA mismatch. Selection pressure drove it toward universal immune suppression rather than pathogen-specific presentation.

HLA-C.editorial_description: The NK cell regulator. HLA-C is unique among class I HLA for being the dominant ligand for killer immunoglobulin-like receptors (KIR) on NK cells. The HLA-C/KIR interaction determines NK cell education and controls psoriasis susceptibility.
HLA-C.what_it_means: HLA-C presents peptides to CD8+ T cells (like HLA-A/B) but is uniquely important for NK cell regulation through KIR interactions. HLA-C*06:02 is the strongest genetic risk factor for psoriasis.
HLA-C.carrier_note: HLA-C*06:02 confers 8-10x psoriasis risk and is carried by ~10% of Europeans. HLA-C typing also matters for KIR-based NK cell immunotherapy donor selection.
HLA-C.nonref_interpretation: Consumer array HLA-C typing is imprecise. Clinical significance depends on specific alleles, particularly C*06:02 for psoriasis and C1/C2 grouping for KIR education.
HLA-C.actionability: HLA-C*06:02 carriers have high psoriasis risk — early treatment with biologics is especially effective. Also relevant for NK-based immunotherapy donor matching.
HLA-C.fun_fact: HLA-C was historically called 'Class I's neglected child' because it's expressed at lower levels than HLA-A/B. Its importance was only appreciated when KIR genetics revealed that HLA-C is the dominant NK cell educator — controlling innate immune surveillance.

MAPK14.editorial_description: Better known as p38α — the stress-activated kinase that translates cellular stress into inflammatory cytokine production. It's the kinase that makes TNF, IL-1, and IL-6 during inflammation. p38 inhibitors have been attempted for decades in rheumatology.
MAPK14.what_it_means: p38α (MAPK14) is activated by environmental stress, UV, osmotic shock, and inflammatory cytokines. It stabilizes inflammatory mRNA transcripts and activates MK2, which produces TNF and IL-6.
MAPK14.carrier_note: MAPK14 is highly conserved with minimal common functional variants. Its importance is as a drug target and signaling node rather than a source of genetic risk.
MAPK14.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. p38 biology matters for inflammation pharmacology, not personal genetics.
MAPK14.actionability: No direct action. p38 inhibitors have failed many clinical trials (too toxic — p38 has too many essential functions). Downstream targets (MK2) may be more druggable.
MAPK14.fun_fact: Over 20 p38 MAPK inhibitors have entered clinical trials since the 1990s, and virtually all failed due to toxicity or lack of efficacy. It's one of pharma's most expensive failures — the biology is validated but the target is too essential to block safely.

CDK1.editorial_description: The mitosis trigger. CDK1 is the only CDK absolutely required for cell division in all eukaryotes. When it fires, cells commit to mitosis. It's been conserved essentially unchanged for over a billion years of evolution.
CDK1.what_it_means: CDK1 (also called CDC2) binds cyclin B and triggers mitotic entry by phosphorylating hundreds of substrates simultaneously — nuclear envelope breakdown, chromosome condensation, spindle assembly. It's the master switch.
CDK1.carrier_note: CDK1 has no known common pathogenic variants. It's the most essential CDK — the only one whose loss cannot be compensated by other family members. Genetic variation is minimal.
CDK1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are extremely rare and expected to be benign. CDK1 is under intense purifying selection.
CDK1.actionability: No direct action. CDK1 is too essential to safely inhibit as a drug target, unlike CDK4/6 (which can be blocked because CDK2 compensates).
CDK1.fun_fact: Paul Nurse won the 2001 Nobel Prize for discovering CDK1 (in yeast, as cdc2). His experiment was beautifully simple: human cdc2 could rescue yeast lacking their own copy — proving the cell cycle machinery is conserved across a billion years of evolution.

PLK1.editorial_description: Polo-like kinase 1 — the mitotic choreographer. PLK1 coordinates almost every step of cell division: centrosome maturation, spindle assembly, chromosome attachment, and cytokinesis. It's overexpressed in most cancers.
PLK1.what_it_means: PLK1 phosphorylates dozens of mitotic substrates at precisely timed intervals. It creates the coordination that ensures chromosomes separate correctly. Without PLK1, mitosis is chaotic and lethal to dividing cells.
PLK1.carrier_note: PLK1 has no well-characterized common pathogenic variants. It's essential for cell division and under strong selective constraint. Overexpression in tumors is a somatic event.
PLK1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. PLK1's relevance is as a cancer drug target, not a source of genetic risk.
PLK1.actionability: No germline action. PLK1 inhibitors (volasertib, onvansertib) are in cancer clinical trials, particularly for AML and solid tumors.
PLK1.fun_fact: PLK1 is named after 'polo' in Drosophila — mutant fly cells have abnormal spindle poles (resembling polo balls). PLK1 inhibition selectively kills rapidly dividing cancer cells because normal cells can pause at checkpoints, but cancer cells with defective checkpoints cannot.

PCNA.editorial_description: The DNA replication sliding clamp. PCNA encircles DNA and tethers polymerases during replication. It's also a proliferation marker used in pathology (like Ki-67) and a platform for recruiting repair enzymes to replication forks.
PCNA.what_it_means: PCNA forms a trimeric ring that slides along DNA, acting as a processivity factor for DNA polymerase δ. It also recruits mismatch repair, translesion synthesis, and chromatin remodeling factors to replication sites.
PCNA.carrier_note: PCNA is one of the most conserved eukaryotic proteins. Pathogenic variants are essentially incompatible with life. Common genetic variation is minimal.
PCNA.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are extremely rare and expected to be benign. PCNA is under intense purifying selection.
PCNA.actionability: No germline action. PCNA immunostaining (like Ki-67) is used to assess proliferation rate in tumor biopsies.
PCNA.fun_fact: PCNA was independently discovered three times: as a lupus autoantigen (1978), a cell cycle protein (1980), and a DNA polymerase accessory factor (1984). It's one of the few proteins where patients with autoimmune disease (SLE) helped identify a fundamental cell biology component.

E2F1.editorial_description: The cell cycle trigger. E2F1 is the transcription factor that RB normally restrains. When RB is inactivated (by CDK phosphorylation or viral oncoproteins), E2F1 floods the nucleus with proliferation gene activation.
E2F1.what_it_means: E2F1 activates genes required for DNA replication (DNA polymerase, thymidine kinase, MCMs). It's held inactive by RB in quiescent cells. Growth signals release E2F1 through CDK4/6-mediated RB phosphorylation.
E2F1.carrier_note: E2F1 is highly conserved with no well-characterized pathogenic germline variants. Its dysregulation in cancer comes from upstream pathway changes (RB loss, CDK amplification), not E2F1 mutations.
E2F1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. E2F1's role in cancer is through pathway dysregulation, not direct mutation.
E2F1.actionability: No direct action. The RB-E2F1 axis is indirectly targeted by CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib), which keep RB active and E2F1 suppressed.
E2F1.fun_fact: E2F was discovered through its ability to bind the adenovirus E2 promoter ('E2 Factor'). HPV oncoproteins (E7) and adenovirus proteins (E1A) both evolved to displace RB from E2F — proving that viruses independently discovered the RB-E2F switch as the key to unlocking cell proliferation.

MMP1.editorial_description: Collagenase-1 — the enzyme that breaks down type I collagen (your body's most abundant protein). MMP-1 degrades skin collagen during photoaging, joint cartilage in arthritis, and tumor stroma during cancer invasion.
MMP1.what_it_means: MMP-1 makes the initial cleavage in fibrillar collagen (types I, II, III), destabilizing the triple helix for further degradation. It's induced by UV radiation, inflammatory cytokines, and growth factors.
MMP1.carrier_note: The -1607 1G/2G promoter polymorphism (rs1799750) affects MMP-1 expression. The 2G allele (higher expression) is found in ~50% of people and associated with more rapid collagen degradation.
MMP1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference (2G allele) indicates higher MMP-1 expression — potentially faster collagen turnover, relevant for skin aging, arthritis, and wound healing speed.
MMP1.actionability: Sun protection reduces UV-induced MMP-1 activation (the main driver of skin photoaging). Retinoids suppress MMP-1 expression, which is partly why retinol reduces wrinkles.
MMP1.fun_fact: UV radiation induces MMP-1 within hours, which then degrades dermal collagen. This is the molecular mechanism behind photoaging — and why sunscreen is the single most effective anti-wrinkle intervention. Repeated UV exposure + MMP-1 = cumulative collagen loss = wrinkles.

CDKN1B.editorial_description: Better known as p27 — the cell cycle brake that growth factors release. It inhibits CDK2 to keep cells quiescent. Its degradation is the key event allowing cells to commit to division. Low p27 in tumors predicts aggressive behavior.
CDKN1B.what_it_means: p27 binds and inhibits CDK2/cyclin E complexes, preventing S-phase entry. Mitogenic signals trigger p27 phosphorylation and ubiquitin-mediated degradation, releasing the brake on proliferation.
CDKN1B.carrier_note: Germline CDKN1B mutations cause MEN4 (multiple endocrine neoplasia type 4) — very rare. Common variants modestly affect cancer risk. Low p27 expression in tumors is a well-established poor prognostic marker.
CDKN1B.nonref_interpretation: Common variants may subtly affect p27 expression or stability. Context for cell cycle control but not individually actionable for most people.
CDKN1B.actionability: No direct action for common variants. p27 levels in tumor biopsies provide prognostic information in breast, prostate, and other cancers.
CDKN1B.fun_fact: p27 is degraded by the SCF-Skp2 ubiquitin ligase — Skp2 overexpression in cancer drives p27 destruction and uncontrolled proliferation. Interestingly, p27 degradation is so tightly regulated that its half-life changes from hours (quiescent) to minutes (stimulated) upon growth factor addition.

IGF1R.editorial_description: The growth hormone effector receptor. IGF1R mediates IGF-1's growth and survival signals. Reduced IGF1R signaling extends lifespan in every model organism tested — connecting growth factor signaling directly to aging.
IGF1R.what_it_means: IGF1R is a receptor tyrosine kinase that activates PI3K/AKT and RAS/MAPK pathways when bound by IGF-1 or IGF-2. It drives cell growth, survival, and metabolism. Lower signaling = longer lifespan in model organisms.
IGF1R.carrier_note: Common IGF1R variants contribute modestly to height and body composition variation. Rare heterozygous loss-of-function carriers tend to be shorter but may have increased longevity (under investigation).
IGF1R.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may subtly affect IGF1R sensitivity or expression. Context for understanding growth signaling and potentially longevity biology.
IGF1R.actionability: IGF1R signaling is reduced by fasting, caloric restriction, and protein restriction. These interventions mimic the longevity phenotype of IGF1R reduction in animals.
IGF1R.fun_fact: Female mice with one IGF1R copy deleted live 33% longer than normal mice. Centenarian studies show that long-lived humans have reduced IGF signaling. The growth-longevity trade-off (grow fast/die young vs grow slow/live long) appears hardwired through IGF1R.

MMP2.editorial_description: Gelatinase A — the silent tissue remodeler. MMP-2 degrades basement membranes (type IV collagen), enabling cell migration during development, wound healing, and cancer invasion. It works with MMP-9 but is constitutively expressed rather than induced.
MMP2.what_it_means: MMP-2 cleaves type IV collagen (basement membrane), gelatin, and elastin. Unlike most MMPs (induced by inflammation), MMP-2 is constitutively expressed and activated at the cell surface by MT1-MMP.
MMP2.carrier_note: The -1306 C>T variant (rs243865) in the MMP-2 promoter affects expression and is found in ~20-25% of Europeans. Lower-expression variants may reduce cancer invasion capacity.
MMP2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs243865 indicates lower MMP-2 expression. May be context-dependent — less tissue remodeling capacity.
MMP2.actionability: No direct action. MMP-2 levels reflect tissue remodeling activity. Relevant in cancer invasion and cardiovascular disease research.
MMP2.fun_fact: MMP inhibitors seemed like a cancer cure in the 1990s (block invasion = block metastasis). Multiple clinical trials failed catastrophically — patients got WORSE because MMPs also have anti-tumor and anti-inflammatory roles. It was one of oncology's most painful lessons about pathway complexity.

ITGB3.editorial_description: The platelet integrin. ITGB3 (α3 integrin) forms the GPIIb/IIIa complex on platelets that binds fibrinogen to crosslink platelets during clotting. It's the direct target of abciximab and eptifibatide — potent antiplatelet drugs used during heart attacks.
ITGB3.what_it_means: GPIIb/IIIa is the most abundant platelet surface receptor (~80,000 per platelet). When activated, it binds fibrinogen between platelets, forming the platelet plug. ITGB3 mutations cause Glanzmann thrombasthenia (severe bleeding).
ITGB3.carrier_note: The PlA1/PlA2 polymorphism (rs5918, Leu33Pro) is found in ~15-20% of Europeans (PlA2). PlA2 is associated with modestly increased thrombotic risk and is the most common cause of neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia.
ITGB3.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference at rs5918 (PlA2/Pro33) indicates altered GPIIIa conformation. Context for platelet function and thrombotic risk.
ITGB3.actionability: PlA2 carriers have modestly increased coronary stent thrombosis risk. Relevant for neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia (NAIT) in pregnancy when mother is PlA1/PlA1 and fetus inherits PlA2.
ITGB3.fun_fact: GPIIb/IIIa inhibitors (abciximab) were revolutionary when introduced — blocking the final common pathway of platelet aggregation. Abciximab is actually a chimeric antibody fragment, making platelets completely unable to crosslink regardless of activation signal.

GRB2.editorial_description: The RAS pathway adapter. GRB2 is a tiny protein with no enzymatic activity — it just connects growth factor receptors to RAS activation via SOS. Without this single linker molecule, growth factor signaling to the nucleus would collapse.
GRB2.what_it_means: GRB2 has one SH2 domain (binds phospho-tyrosines on activated receptors) and two SH3 domains (bind SOS, a RAS activator). It's the physical bridge between receptor activation and RAS signaling.
GRB2.carrier_note: GRB2 is extremely conserved and essential. No common pathogenic variants are characterized. It's too critical to tolerate functional variation.
GRB2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are expected to be benign. GRB2 is under strong purifying selection due to its essential adapter function.
GRB2.actionability: No direct action. GRB2 is a potential cancer drug target (blocking its SH2 domain could disconnect growth factors from RAS), but no approved drugs exist yet.
GRB2.fun_fact: GRB2 was the key to solving how receptor tyrosine kinases activate RAS — a mystery that consumed cell biology in the early 1990s. The answer (receptor → GRB2 → SOS → RAS) was published simultaneously by three groups in 1993 and unified 15 years of confusing biochemistry.

RHOA.editorial_description: The cell shape controller. RhoA orchestrates the actin cytoskeleton — determining whether cells are round or spread, stationary or migrating. It controls everything from wound healing to cancer cell invasion to muscle contraction.
RHOA.what_it_means: RhoA is a small GTPase that activates ROCK kinase, promoting actin stress fiber formation and cell contractility. It opposes Rac1 (which promotes spreading/migration). The RhoA/Rac1 balance determines cell behavior.
RHOA.carrier_note: RHOA is highly conserved with no well-characterized common pathogenic variants. Somatic gain-of-function mutations are found in some T-cell lymphomas and diffuse gastric cancers.
RHOA.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. RhoA biology matters for cancer invasion and fibrosis research, not personal genetics.
RHOA.actionability: No direct action. ROCK inhibitors (fasudil, ripasudil) targeting RhoA's downstream effector are approved for pulmonary hypertension and glaucoma.
RHOA.fun_fact: RhoA was named from 'Ras Homolog gene family, member A.' The Rho GTPases (Rho, Rac, Cdc42) control cell shape so precisely that you can predict a cell's behavior by measuring which one is active. They're essentially the cell's steering wheel.

SPP1.editorial_description: Better known as Osteopontin (OPN) — a multifunctional secreted protein involved in bone remodeling, immune activation, and cancer metastasis. It's a biomarker for tumor burden and inflammation, elevated in nearly every aggressive cancer.
SPP1.what_it_means: Osteopontin (OPN) bridges cells to mineralized matrix in bone, activates macrophages and T cells, and promotes cancer cell survival and metastasis. It signals through integrins (esp. αvβ3) and CD44.
SPP1.carrier_note: Common SPP1 variants affect circulating osteopontin levels. Higher levels are associated with autoimmune disease, cardiovascular disease, and cancer progression, though causality vs biomarker status is debated.
SPP1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants may indicate altered osteopontin expression. Context for understanding bone turnover, immune activation, and cancer biology.
SPP1.actionability: No direct action for common variants. Plasma osteopontin is used as a research biomarker for tumor burden and inflammatory disease activity.
SPP1.fun_fact: Osteopontin was named because it 'bridges' (pontin) bone (osteo) cells to mineral matrix. It's one of the few proteins found in both milk and urine, kidney stones and bones, immune cells and cancer cells — truly multitasking.

HSPA4.editorial_description: A heat shock protein in the HSP70 superfamily. HSPA4 assists protein folding under stress and helps clear misfolded aggregates. It's part of the proteostasis network that prevents neurodegeneration and maintains cellular health.
HSPA4.what_it_means: HSPA4 (also called HSP70RY/APG-2) is a nucleotide exchange factor for HSP70 chaperones. It accelerates their ATPase cycle, making them more efficient at refolding misfolded proteins or targeting them for degradation.
HSPA4.carrier_note: HSPA4 variants are poorly characterized for health effects. The heat shock protein family is highly conserved and redundant — multiple family members provide backup.
HSPA4.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants have uncertain significance. HSP biology is important for proteostasis but individual gene variants are rarely impactful due to redundancy.
HSPA4.actionability: No direct action. Heat shock proteins are induced by sauna, exercise, and mild heat stress — the basis for hormesis-based health practices.
