HSPA4.fun_fact: Heat shock proteins were discovered in 1962 when Ferruccio Ritossa accidentally left fruit fly incubators at elevated temperatures overnight. The 'puffing' patterns on chromosomes (indicating gene activation) he observed the next morning launched an entire field of stress biology.

RAC1.editorial_description: The cell migration driver. Rac1 pushes the leading edge of migrating cells forward by building actin networks. It opposes RhoA's contractility, and the balance between them determines whether cells stay put or move.
RAC1.what_it_means: Rac1 activates the WAVE complex and PAK kinases to build branched actin networks (lamellipodia) at cell edges. This drives cell spreading, migration, and phagocytosis. It's essential for immune cell chemotaxis.
RAC1.carrier_note: RAC1 is essential with no common pathogenic germline variants. Somatic RAC1 P29S mutations are found in ~5% of melanomas (a UV-signature oncogenic mutation).
RAC1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. RAC1 mutations in melanoma are somatic, not germline.
RAC1.actionability: No germline action. RAC1 P29S in melanoma may predict response to specific targeted therapies under investigation.
RAC1.fun_fact: Rac1 was named 'Ras-related C3 botulinum toxin substrate 1' because C3 toxin (from Clostridium botulinum) specifically inactivates Rho but NOT Rac — allowing researchers to separate their functions. The naming reveals how early cell biologists used bacterial toxins as molecular tools.

PRKDC.editorial_description: Better known as DNA-PKcs (DNA-dependent Protein Kinase catalytic subunit). This massive kinase repairs double-strand DNA breaks (the most dangerous lesion) and is essential for immune system development (V(D)J recombination generates antibody diversity).
PRKDC.what_it_means: DNA-PKcs is recruited to broken DNA ends by Ku70/Ku80, where it phosphorylates itself and repair factors to coordinate non-homologous end joining (NHEJ). It's also needed for V(D)J recombination in lymphocyte development.
PRKDC.carrier_note: PRKDC is one of the largest protein-coding genes (4,128 amino acids). Loss-of-function causes severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) in mice and radiosensitivity. Common human variants are benign.
PRKDC.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. True loss-of-function would present as immunodeficiency in infancy.
PRKDC.actionability: No direct action. DNA-PKcs inhibitors are being developed as radiosensitizers — blocking cancer cells' ability to repair radiation-induced DNA breaks.
PRKDC.fun_fact: DNA-PKcs is one of the largest kinases known (469 kDa) and belongs to the PI3K-like kinase family (with ATM and ATR). SCID mice (which lack DNA-PKcs) can't make functional antibodies or T-cell receptors because V(D)J recombination creates DNA breaks that require NHEJ to resolve.

PTK2.editorial_description: Better known as FAK (Focal Adhesion Kinase) — the mechanosensor at cell-matrix attachment sites. It integrates signals from integrins and growth factors, telling cells whether their environment supports survival and migration.
PTK2.what_it_means: FAK autophosphorylates at focal adhesions when integrins engage extracellular matrix. This creates docking sites for SRC and PI3K, linking cell attachment to survival signaling. Detached cells without FAK signaling undergo anoikis (detachment-induced death).
PTK2.carrier_note: PTK2 has no well-characterized common pathogenic variants. FAK overexpression in cancer is a somatic event that promotes invasion and survival. The gene is essential for embryonic development.
PTK2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. FAK biology matters for cancer invasion and mechanobiology research.
PTK2.actionability: No germline action. FAK inhibitors (defactinib) are in cancer clinical trials, particularly for mesothelioma and ovarian cancer.
PTK2.fun_fact: FAK explains why normal cells die when detached from tissue (anoikis) — no adhesion means no FAK means no survival signals. Cancer cells override this by overexpressing FAK, allowing them to survive in blood and metastasize. It's literally the anti-anoikis kinase.

HSPA5.editorial_description: Better known as BiP (or GRP78) — the endoplasmic reticulum's master chaperone. It folds every secreted and membrane protein in your cells. When overwhelmed (ER stress), it activates the unfolded protein response (UPR) — a decision between adaptation and apoptosis.
HSPA5.what_it_means: BiP binds to unfolded proteins in the ER, assisting their folding. When misfolded proteins accumulate (ER stress), BiP releases from IRE1/PERK/ATF6 sensors, activating the UPR to restore proteostasis or trigger cell death if stress is irresolvable.
HSPA5.carrier_note: HSPA5 is extremely conserved and essential. No common pathogenic variants are characterized. Rare biallelic loss-of-function causes Marinesco-Sjogren syndrome-like features.
HSPA5.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. BiP/GRP78 biology is important for understanding ER stress in diabetes, neurodegeneration, and cancer.
HSPA5.actionability: No direct action. ER stress management is supported by adequate protein folding conditions: avoiding extreme metabolic stress, maintaining calcium homeostasis.
HSPA5.fun_fact: Cancer cells are 'addicted' to BiP because their high protein production rate creates chronic ER stress. Cell-surface BiP (abnormally relocated) is being targeted with antibodies and toxin conjugates as a cancer-selective therapy — normal cells keep BiP inside.

LCN2.editorial_description: Better known as NGAL (Neutrophil Gelatinase-Associated Lipocalin) — your body's iron theft-prevention system. It sequesters iron-laden siderophores that bacteria use to steal your iron. Also an early kidney injury biomarker — rising within hours of damage, far faster than creatinine.
LCN2.what_it_means: LCN2 binds bacterial siderophores (iron-chelating molecules), preventing bacteria from acquiring the iron they need to grow. It's part of nutritional immunity — starving pathogens of essential metals.
LCN2.carrier_note: Common LCN2 variants are poorly characterized for health effects. Clinical importance is primarily as a biomarker (NGAL for acute kidney injury) rather than a genetic risk factor.
LCN2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants have uncertain significance. LCN2's clinical relevance is as a kidney injury biomarker and antimicrobial protein.
LCN2.actionability: No direct genetic action. Urine NGAL is used clinically as an early acute kidney injury marker — detecting damage 24-48 hours before creatinine rises.
LCN2.fun_fact: NGAL (Neutrophil Gelatinase-Associated Lipocalin) rises within 2 hours of kidney injury while creatinine takes 24-72 hours. This time advantage could revolutionize ICU care — catching kidney damage early enough to intervene before permanent harm occurs.

ITGB1.editorial_description: Beta-1 integrin — the most widely expressed integrin subunit. It pairs with 12 different alpha subunits to form receptors for collagen, fibronectin, and laminin. It anchors nearly every cell type to the extracellular matrix.
ITGB1.what_it_means: ITGB1 forms heterodimers with alpha integrins to mediate cell-matrix adhesion, migration, and outside-in signaling. It's essential for development, wound healing, and maintaining tissue architecture.
ITGB1.carrier_note: ITGB1 is essential for embryonic development (knockout is lethal). No common pathogenic variants are characterized. It's under strong selective constraint.
ITGB1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. ITGB1's importance is in fundamental cell biology rather than personal genetic risk.
ITGB1.actionability: No direct action. Integrin biology is relevant for understanding wound healing, fibrosis, and cancer invasion.
ITGB1.fun_fact: ITGB1 partners with 12 different alpha subunits — more than any other integrin beta chain. This versatility makes it the universal 'matrix connector.' Integrins signal bidirectionally (inside-out AND outside-in), connecting mechanical forces to biochemical responses.

MAPK8.editorial_description: You know this one as JNK1 (c-Jun N-terminal Kinase 1). It's the stress-activated kinase linking inflammation to insulin resistance and obesity — activated by cellular stress and inflammatory signals, its chronic activation in fat tissue drives type 2 diabetes.
MAPK8.what_it_means: JNK1 (MAPK8) is activated by TNF, oxidative stress, and free fatty acids. It phosphorylates IRS-1 (inhibiting insulin signaling) and c-Jun (activating AP-1). Chronic JNK activation in adipose tissue causes insulin resistance.
MAPK8.carrier_note: MAPK8 is conserved with minimal common functional variants. JNK's role in metabolic disease is established through pathway biology, not individual genetic risk.
MAPK8.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. JNK biology matters for understanding the obesity-inflammation-diabetes connection.
MAPK8.actionability: No direct genetic action. JNK activation is reduced by exercise, weight loss, and anti-inflammatory interventions. These address the upstream signals that chronically activate JNK.
MAPK8.fun_fact: JNK was the critical link explaining WHY obesity causes insulin resistance. TNF from inflamed fat tissue activates JNK, which directly phosphorylates and inactivates the insulin receptor substrate. One kinase connects inflammation to diabetes.

HSPA8.editorial_description: Better known as HSC70 — the constitutive housekeeping chaperone that performs chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA). Unlike stress-induced HSP70, HSC70 works constantly, selecting damaged proteins one-by-one for lysosomal degradation.
HSPA8.what_it_means: HSC70 recognizes proteins with KFERQ-like motifs and delivers them to LAMP-2A on lysosomes for selective degradation (CMA). It also assists protein folding and clathrin-mediated endocytosis. It's always active, not stress-induced.
HSPA8.carrier_note: HSPA8 is one of the most conserved and abundant human proteins. No common pathogenic variants are characterized — it's too essential for significant variation.
HSPA8.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are expected to be benign. HSPA8 is under extreme purifying selection.
HSPA8.actionability: No direct action. CMA activity declines with aging, contributing to protein aggregate accumulation. Caloric restriction and exercise maintain CMA function.
HSPA8.fun_fact: CMA (chaperone-mediated autophagy) is unique to mammals and degrades ~30% of cytoplasmic proteins. Unlike macroautophagy (bulk degradation), CMA selects individual proteins by their sequence motif. It declines with age, and restoring it in old mice improves organ function.

SP1.editorial_description: The GC-box transcription factor — one of the first human transcription factors ever characterized. SP1 binds GC-rich promoter elements in thousands of genes, particularly housekeeping genes. It's ubiquitous and fundamental.
SP1.what_it_means: SP1 binds GC-rich DNA sequences (5'-GGGCGG-3') found in most housekeeping gene promoters. It activates basal transcription and interacts with the core transcriptional machinery. It regulates cell growth, differentiation, and apoptosis.
SP1.carrier_note: SP1 is extremely conserved with no characterized common pathogenic variants. It regulates too many essential genes to tolerate loss-of-function variation.
SP1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are expected to be benign. SP1's importance is in fundamental gene regulation biology.
SP1.actionability: No direct action. SP1 is more a subject of molecular biology textbooks than personal genetics.
SP1.fun_fact: SP1 was purified in 1983 by Robert Tjian — one of the very first human transcription factors isolated. Its name means 'Specificity Protein 1.' It binds over 12,000 promoter sites in the human genome, making it one of the most promiscuous transcription factors known.

CDK2.editorial_description: The S-phase driver. CDK2 partners with cyclin E to drive cells into DNA replication and with cyclin A to progress through S-phase. It's the kinase that CDK4/6 inhibitor-resistant cancers often rewire to depend on.
CDK2.what_it_means: CDK2/cyclin E phosphorylates RB (completing what CDK4/6 started), centrosome substrates, and replication factors. It commits cells to DNA synthesis. In cancer, CDK2 amplification can bypass CDK4/6 inhibitor therapy.
CDK2.carrier_note: CDK2 has no well-characterized common pathogenic variants. Unlike CDK4/6, CDK2 is not yet a clinical drug target (selective CDK2 inhibitors are in early trials). Variation is minimal due to essential function.
CDK2.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. CDK2 biology matters for understanding cancer drug resistance to CDK4/6 inhibitors.
CDK2.actionability: No germline action. Selective CDK2 inhibitors are in early clinical trials for CDK4/6 inhibitor-resistant breast cancer.
CDK2.fun_fact: CDK2 was the first CDK whose crystal structure was solved (1993) and has been co-crystallized with more inhibitors than any other kinase. Despite being one of the most structurally studied proteins in pharma, making a CDK2-SELECTIVE inhibitor (not hitting CDK1) took 25 years.

UBC.editorial_description: The ubiquitin gene. UBC encodes a polyubiquitin precursor — a chain of ubiquitin molecules that's cleaved to produce the individual ubiquitin tags cells attach to proteins destined for destruction. It's the raw material for the proteasome system.
UBC.what_it_means: UBC produces polyubiquitin (multiple ubiquitin molecules in tandem). Under stress, UBC transcription increases massively to supply the extra ubiquitin needed for protein quality control and stress response.
UBC.carrier_note: UBC is essential and highly conserved. Ubiquitin itself is one of the most conserved proteins in eukaryotes (96% identical from yeast to humans). Pathogenic variants would be incompatible with cellular function.
UBC.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are expected to be benign. Ubiquitin biology is fundamental to all protein turnover but not a source of personal genetic risk.
UBC.actionability: No direct action. The ubiquitin-proteasome system is targeted by bortezomib (proteasome inhibitor) in multiple myeloma.
UBC.fun_fact: Ubiquitin is only 76 amino acids but controls the fate of nearly every protein in the cell. Aaron Ciechanover, Avram Hershko, and Irwin Rose won the 2004 Nobel Prize for discovering ubiquitin-mediated protein degradation. The 'ubiquitin code' (different chain types = different fates) is still being decoded.

MAPK3.editorial_description: Better known as ERK1 — MAPK1/ERK2's partner in the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK cascade. ERK1 and ERK2 are 85% identical and largely redundant, though ERK2 is more abundant. Together they execute growth factor-driven transcriptional responses.
MAPK3.what_it_means: ERK1 (MAPK3) is activated alongside ERK2 by MEK1/2 and phosphorylates nuclear transcription factors. The ERK1/ERK2 duo together constitute the terminal effectors of the most important growth signaling pathway in cancer.
MAPK3.carrier_note: MAPK3 is conserved with minimal characterized variation. ERK1 knockout mice are viable (ERK2 compensates), suggesting ERK1 is partially dispensable. No known common pathogenic variants.
MAPK3.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. ERK1 is largely redundant with ERK2 for most functions.
MAPK3.actionability: No direct action. MEK inhibitors (trametinib) block both ERK1 and ERK2 activation simultaneously.
MAPK3.fun_fact: ERK1 knockout mice are surprisingly normal (just smaller), while ERK2 knockout is embryonic lethal. This suggests ERK2 handles most essential functions and ERK1 is a 'helper' — though some researchers argue they have distinct nuclear targets.

PRKCA.editorial_description: Protein Kinase C alpha — one of the original signal transduction kinases discovered through phorbol ester tumor promoters. PKCα mediates calcium and diacylglycerol signals, connecting membrane lipid events to gene expression changes.
PRKCA.what_it_means: PKCα is activated by calcium and DAG (produced when PLC cleaves membrane lipids). It phosphorylates diverse substrates controlling proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis depending on cell type and context.
PRKCA.carrier_note: Common PRKCA variants have minimal characterized health effects. PKC biology is complex and highly context-dependent, making genotype-phenotype correlations difficult.
PRKCA.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants have uncertain significance. PKC signaling is important but individual gene variants are not established risk factors.
PRKCA.actionability: No direct action. PKC was originally thought to be an oncogene (tumor promoters activate it), but PKC inhibitors failed in cancer trials, and some PKC family members are actually tumor suppressors.
PRKCA.fun_fact: Phorbol esters (from croton oil) have been used as tumor promoters since the 1940s. When PKC was identified as their target in 1982, everyone assumed PKC was an oncogene. 40 years later, the story is far more nuanced — different PKC isoforms can be either oncogenic or tumor-suppressive.

CSNK2A1.editorial_description: Casein Kinase 2 alpha — one of the most prolific kinases in the cell, phosphorylating over 300 substrates. CK2 is unusual: constitutively active, found in every subcellular compartment, and essential for cell survival. It's elevated in virtually all cancers.
CSNK2A1.what_it_means: CK2 is a tetrameric serine/threonine kinase that phosphorylates substrates involved in transcription, translation, DNA repair, and apoptosis suppression. Unlike most kinases, it's always 'on' and not regulated by second messengers.
CSNK2A1.carrier_note: Germline CSNK2A1 mutations cause Okur-Chung neurodevelopmental syndrome (intellectual disability, behavioral issues) — rare, de novo. Common variants are benign.
CSNK2A1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants are generally benign. Rare pathogenic variants present with developmental features in childhood.
CSNK2A1.actionability: No action for common variants. CK2 inhibitors (silmitasertib) are in cancer clinical trials for cholangiocarcinoma and COVID-19.
CSNK2A1.fun_fact: CK2 phosphorylates more substrates than almost any other kinase (~25% of the human phosphoproteome has CK2 sites). It's also one of the few kinases that can use GTP as a phosphate donor in addition to ATP — ensuring it always has fuel.

PSEN1.editorial_description: The most common cause of early-onset Alzheimer's disease. PSEN1 is the catalytic core of gamma-secretase — the enzyme that cuts APP to produce amyloid-beta. Over 300 mutations have been found, some causing dementia as early as age 30.
PSEN1.what_it_means: Presenilin-1 is the aspartyl protease within gamma-secretase that cleaves APP into amyloid-beta peptides. Mutations shift cleavage to produce longer, stickier Aβ42 fragments that aggregate into toxic plaques faster.
PSEN1.carrier_note: PSEN1 mutations are autosomal dominant with near-complete penetrance — carriers almost always develop Alzheimer's, typically between ages 30-60. Over 300 different mutations are known. They account for ~80% of familial early-onset AD.
PSEN1.nonref_interpretation: Non-reference variants in PSEN1 should be evaluated carefully. Many are pathogenic. Consumer arrays may detect some known mutations, but clinical sequencing is needed for comprehensive assessment.
PSEN1.actionability: Families with early-onset Alzheimer's should pursue genetic counseling and PSEN1 testing. Carriers are eligible for prevention trials (anti-amyloid antibodies). Predictive testing has profound implications — counseling essential.
PSEN1.fun_fact: The world's largest study of dominantly inherited Alzheimer's (DIAN) follows PSEN1 families, tracking biomarker changes 20+ years before symptoms. These families proved that amyloid accumulates decades before memory loss — fundamentally reshaping when we think Alzheimer's begins.
